detail: Confirmed defect. The automations feature ships a complete user-facing scheduling surface: a CRUD API (create/list/pause/resume + manual run in apps/automations/urls.py), an ACTIVE status, a computed next_run_at (compute_next_run_at in services.py), and a working dispatcher run_due_automations (scheduler.py) that selects Automation rows where status=ACTIVE and next_run_at<=now and executes them. But nothing ever calls run_due_automations_task on a schedule. It is absent from TASK_MAP in apps/cron/views.py (lines 70-198), so /api/cron/trigger/<name>/ cannot reach it; it is absent from SYSTEM_CRONS in register_system_crons.py (lines 19-111), so no QStash schedule is ever created; and there is no dedicated URL in apps/cron/urls.py or apps/automations/urls.py that invokes it. A repo-wide grep finds the function referenced only at its own definition, an agent-skills doc, and the audit notes. The audit's own inventory_notes (docs/feature-audit/areas/automations.json:132) confirm it is defined but NOT wired to any QStash cron registration or URL endpoint and exists as an internal callable only. User impact: a subscriber can create an automation, see it listed as ACTIVE, and run it manually, but it will NEVER fire automatically on its configured daily/weekly schedule. The core promise of the feature (automatic scheduled execution) is dead.
suggested_fix: Wire the dispatcher into the QStash cron path. (1) Add a TASK_MAP entry in apps/cron/views.py mapping a name like run_due_automations to apps.automations.tasks.run_due_automations_task. (2) Add a SYSTEM_CRONS tuple in register_system_crons.py such as ("run-due-automations", an every-minute or every-5-minute cron expr, "/api/cron/trigger/run_due_automations/") at a cadence consistent with the minute granularity of compute_next_run_at. (3) After deploy, run register_system_crons (or hit /api/cron/register-system-crons/) so the new QStash schedule is created. Add a test asserting every active scheduled-execution task is present in both TASK_MAP and SYSTEM_CRONS to prevent regressions.

apps/automations/scheduler.py:18-63 — get_due_automations/run_due_automations dispatcher filters status=ACTIVE, next_run_at<=now and executes
apps/cron/views.py:70-198 — TASK_MAP has no run_due_automations entry

docs/feature-audit/areas/automations.json:132 — inventory note confirms not wired to any QStash cron/URL endpoint
expected_behaviour: run_due_automations_task() (apps/automations/tasks.py) delegates to run_due_automations() (apps/automations/scheduler.py). Queries Automation rows with status=active and next_run_at__lte=now, ordered by next_run_at, limited to DEFAULT_DUE_BATCH_SIZE=100 per invocation. For each due automation, calls execute_automation(automation, trigger_source='schedule', scheduled_for=automation.next_run_at). Limit/rate checks apply: if last_run_at is within MIN_RUN_INTERVAL=120 minutes, or MAX_RUNS_PER_DAY=12 runs already exist in the tenant's local calendar day, the run is created with status=SKIPPED and next_run_at is still advanced. On dispatch success: run.status=SUCCEEDED, automation.last_run_at and automation.next_run_at are updated. On dispatch failure: run.status=FAILED, error_message set, next_run_at advanced (so schedule continues). Idempotency key for scheduled runs: 'schedule:<automation_id>:<epoch_timestamp>' — prevents duplicate runs if the task fires twice for the same window. Returns a summary dict with due_count, processed_count, succeeded, failed, skipped, errors counts. Unhandled exceptions per automation are caught, logged, and counted in 'errors' without aborting the batch.

detail: Confirmed real. The QStash-wired cron refresh_expiring_integrations_task (registered at apps/cron/views.py:86 as refresh_expiring_integrations) flips healthy Composio-managed reddit integrations to ERROR on every run. Chain verified end to end: (1) Reddit is a real Provider (models.py:20) and a Composio-managed provider (services.py:28, COMPOSIO_MANAGED_PROVIDERS contains reddit). (2) complete_composio_connection persists it with status=ACTIVE and a defaults dict that omits token_expires_at (services.py:120-130); the field is null=True with no default (models.py:46), so it is stored NULL. (3) The task query selects ACTIVE rows where Q(token_expires_at__isnull=True) (tasks.py:32-35), matching every reddit integration. (4) get_provider_client_credentials(reddit) returns empty strings because reddit is absent from PROVIDER_GROUP (services.py:302-305, 338-344). (5) The guard if not client_id or not client_secret (tasks.py:43) is therefore always true for reddit, so the row is set to ERROR and errored is incremented (tasks.py:44-46). There is NO is_composio_provider() guard in the task, even though that helper exists (services.py:33) and is applied elsewhere (views.py:225,384). Composio providers do not use OAuth client-credential refresh at all; their tokens are retrieved live from Composio (_get_composio_access_token, services.py:174-219). User impact: a user who connects Reddit silently has the integration flipped to ERROR on the next refresh cron tick, breaking it until manual reconnect, and reconnecting only resets the clock until the next run. The existing test test_marks_error_when_oauth_credentials_missing demonstrates this exact ERROR mechanism; no test covers the reddit/Composio case.
suggested_fix: Skip Composio-managed providers in refresh_expiring_integrations_task. Cleanest is to exclude them at the query level by adding .exclude(provider__in=COMPOSIO_MANAGED_PROVIDERS) to the queryset (import the set from services), or add an early-continue guard inside the loop: if is_composio_provider(integration.provider): continue. Composio tokens are managed/refreshed by Composio itself and fetched live via _get_composio_access_token, so they never need this OAuth client-credential refresh path. Add a regression test that a reddit ACTIVE integration with token_expires_at=None is left untouched (not counted in errored).
evidence: apps/integrations/services.py:338-344 (get_provider_client_credentials returns empty for unknown group)
apps/integrations/services.py:28,33 (COMPOSIO_MANAGED_PROVIDERS={reddit}, is_composio_provider helper exists, unused by task)
apps/integrations/models.py:20,46 (REDDIT provider; token_expires_at null=True no default)
expected_behaviour: QStash calls the task endpoint that invokes refresh_expiring_integrations_task(). Queries all ACTIVE integrations where token_expires_at is null or within 15 minutes. For each: loads refresh_token from Key Vault; if missing, marks EXPIRED. Calls refresh_integration_tokens which POSTs to provider token_url with grant_type=refresh_token, then calls connect_integration to persist updated tokens. On HTTP 400/401, marks EXPIRED; on other errors, marks ERROR. Returns dict {checked, refreshed, expired, errored}. Composio-managed integrations (reddit) are skipped from this task as Composio handles their own refresh.

detail: Confirmed. regenerate_tenant_crons treats any gateway cron that is neither _sync:-prefixed nor schedule-kind 'at' as managed. Fuel session crons are _fuel:{8-hex} with schedule kind 'cron' (fuel_cron.py:84,88), so they pass both guards (_UNMANAGED_PREFIXES at line 44 contains only _sync:; _UNMANAGED_SCHEDULE_KINDS at line 50 contains only 'at') and are classified managed at line 319. These jobs have NO managed=True CronJob row: regenerate_fuel_crons writes them straight to the gateway via cron.add (fuel_cron.py:159-161) and never creates rows, and build_cron_seed_jobs suppresses fuel emission when use_session_scheduling=True (config_generator.py:1638). So desired_by_name (line 247, from CronJob.objects.filter(managed=True)) lacks them; they land in current_managed but not desired_by_name, fall into to_remove (line 322), and cron.remove deletes them (line 381). Both reconcilers co-occur on the same tenant: postgres_cron_canonical=True for all tenants (migrations 0058/0067); regenerate_tenant_crons fires hourly (tasks.py:1095), on every CronJob save/delete signal, and on container-start, independently of the fuel signal that runs regenerate_fuel_crons. Result: a destructive flapping race where the tenant reconciler silently wipes the per-workout prep crons the fuel reconciler just added, so users miss scheduled workout notifications. The FUEL_SESSION classification in _classify_source (postgres_canonical.py:71-75) only runs on dashboard-create/backfill, not the live regen path, so it does not protect these crons.
suggested_fix: Add '_fuel:' to _UNMANAGED_PREFIXES in apps/orchestrator/cron_reconcile.py:44 so the tenant reconciler never owns or removes live fuel-session crons. The dedup pre-pass (line 272) and at-cron janitor already route through _is_unmanaged_cron, so they inherit the exclusion. This leaves regenerate_fuel_crons as the sole writer of _fuel:* jobs, matching the comment-stated design. Add a regression test asserting a _fuel:{8-hex} kind=cron gateway job with no CronJob row survives a reconcile.
expected_behaviour: Called from `regenerate_tenant_crons_task` (QStash, debounced 30s via signal) and `reconcile_tenant_crons_task` (hourly fleet-wide). No-op if `tenant.postgres_cron_canonical=False`. Calls `cron.list` against gateway, extracts jobs. Skips unmanaged crons: `_sync:` prefix, `kind='at'` schedule. Diff by name: for managed CronJob rows not in gateway → `cron.add`. For gateway jobs not in managed rows and not unmanaged → `cron.remove`. Janitor pass: reaps `kind='at'` jobs whose fire time is > 1h in the past (gateway auto-delete may have failed). Enforces `_at` cron caps: >50 → PlatformIssueLog MEDIUM; >200 → reaps newest-first back to 200 + PlatformIssueLog HIGH.

detail: Confirmed real. In frontend/components/schedule-builder.tsx the WEEKDAY_LABELS (lines 30-38) and WEEKDAY_NAMES (lines 40-48) arrays are ordered Monday=index0 ... Sunday=index6. For the weekly frequency the day-picker writes the array index directly into state.weekdays (lines 364-380), and buildCronFromState joins those indices into the 5th cron field unchanged (lines 119-123). But the resulting cron string is consumed with STANDARD cron weekday semantics (0=Sunday, 1=Monday ... 6=Saturday): croniter computes next-fire in apps/cron/gateway_client.py:243-254, and the expr is submitted verbatim from createForm.expr to the cron API / OpenClaw engine with no remapping (frontend/app/settings/cron-jobs/page.tsx:600-603). So picking Mon emits 0 which fires Sunday; Tue emits 1 which fires Monday; ... Sun emits 6 which fires Saturday. Every weekly task built via the easy builder fires one day earlier than intended. The bug is self-confirming because the hardcoded presets in the SAME file use correct standard convention: weekdays maps to 1-5 (line 116) and weekends maps to 0,6 (line 118), while only the weekly day-picker is off by one. formatHumanWeekdays (lines 209-215) and parseCronExpr (lines 173-189) use the same off-by-one arrays, so the job-list label (page.tsx:857) and round-trip edits stay internally consistent and the user never sees the mismatch in the UI -- they only find out when the task fires on the wrong day. User impact: silent wrong-day execution of every user-created weekly scheduled task.
suggested_fix: Make the builder's weekday indices use standard cron numbering. Cleanest fix: reorder WEEKDAY_LABELS and WEEKDAY_NAMES to standard cron order (Sunday=index0, Monday=1, ... Saturday=6). Because buildCronFromState, parseCronExpr, the day-picker, and formatHumanWeekdays all key off the same array index, reordering both arrays fixes the emit, parse, and display paths at once. Then update defaultBuilderState.weekdays / handleFrequencyChange weekly default (currently [1]) and the weekly fallback day in buildCronFromState (currently '1') to whatever default day is intended under the new numbering. Add a unit test asserting that selecting Monday emits cron value 1 (not 0) and that '0' round-trips to Sunday.
evidence: frontend/components/schedule-builder.tsx:116-123 (presets weekdays=1-5, weekends=0,6 use standard cron convention; weekly joins state.weekdays indices verbatim)
frontend/components/schedule-builder.tsx:364-380 (weekly day-picker writes array index into state.weekdays)
frontend/components/schedule-builder.tsx:173-189,209-215 (parseCronExpr and formatHumanWeekdays use same off-by-one arrays, hiding the discrepancy in UI)
frontend/app/settings/cron-jobs/page.tsx:600-603,857 (expr submitted verbatim; cronToHuman label shown in job list)
apps/cron/gateway_client.py:243-254 (croniter consumes expr with standard 0=Sunday weekday semantics)
expected_behaviour: The ScheduleBuilder component starts in easy mode when the initial expr is parseable by parseCronExpr. Easy mode shows a Frequency select (Every day / Weekdays / Weekends / Weekly / Monthly). Weekly shows day-of-week multi-select (Mon–Sun). Monthly shows a day-of-month select (1st–28th, no 29th–31st). Hour select (00–23) and Minute select (00, 15, 30, 45). State changes immediately rebuild the cron expression via buildCronFromState and propagate via onChange. A human-readable label ('Runs: Every day at 09:00') appears at the top. The builder also shows a toggle 'Use cron expression' to switch to advanced mode.

detail: Confirmed real. In MarkdownRenderer's custom input component (markdown-renderer.tsx:30-48), the checkbox onChange handler does NOT know which checkbox was clicked. It only reads props.checked (the clicked box's current state), then linearly scans content.split by newline and fires onCheckboxToggle(i, !checked) on the FIRST line whose source checked-state equals props.checked, returning immediately. There is no use of ordinal position (e.g. the react-markdown node.position AST data, which is available, or a per-render checkbox counter) to map the DOM checkbox back to its source line. Consequence: in any document with two or more checkboxes sharing the same state (two unchecked, or two checked), clicking the 2nd/3rd/etc. always toggles the FIRST same-state one. DocumentView.handleCheckboxToggle (document-view.tsx:344-365) then line-patches that wrong line index and PATCHes it, so the wrong task is completed/uncompleted and the intended one is left untouched. The path is live for all non-typed-managed journal docs (document-view.tsx:523-525 passes handleCheckboxToggle unless typedManaged is true). Multi-item checklists in journal entries are common, so a normal subscriber hits this routinely. There is also no optimistic update, so after the wrong PATCH the refetch visibly flips the wrong checkbox — the corruption is visible but not self-correcting.
suggested_fix: Disambiguate the clicked checkbox by source order rather than by checked-state matching. Easiest: in the custom input component, use the AST node prop that react-markdown passes (it carries position.start.line for the source line), map that 1-based source line to the 0-based index in content split by newline, and call onCheckboxToggle(sourceLineIndex, !checked). Alternative: maintain a per-render counter of how many checkbox inputs have rendered (the Nth checkbox) and walk lines to the Nth checkbox-matching line regardless of its checked state.
evidence: frontend/components/markdown-renderer.tsx:30-48 (handleChange scans for first line matching props.checked, returns on first match — no ordinal disambiguation)
frontend/components/markdown-renderer.tsx:27 (input handler only receives InputHTMLAttributes; reads only props.checked at line 34)
frontend/components/journal/document-view.tsx:344-365 (handleCheckboxToggle line-patches lines[lineIndex] using the passed index)
frontend/components/journal/document-view.tsx:523-525 (MarkdownRenderer wired with onCheckboxToggle for non-typedManaged docs — path is live)
expected_behaviour: When not editing and doc.markdown is non-empty, DocumentView renders MarkdownRenderer with the document's markdown and an onCheckboxToggle handler. Clicking a rendered checkbox ([ ] or [x]) calls handleCheckboxToggle(lineIndex, checked) which line-patches the markdown string, replacing [ ] with [x] or [x] with [ ], then calls updateMutation.mutate(PATCH) without entering edit mode. onCheckboxToggle is not passed (undefined) when typedManaged is true, making checkboxes non-interactive.

detail: Confirmed real. In markdown-renderer.tsx the checkbox onChange handler (handleChange) does NOT know which checkbox was clicked. It ignores the clicked input's identity/position and instead scans the raw markdown top-to-bottom for the FIRST line whose task-list state matches props.checked (regex match comparing isChecked === checked at lines 37-47), then reports that line's index via onCheckboxToggle(i, !checked). For any document with two or more checkboxes sharing the same state in document order (e.g. two unchecked '- [ ]' items), clicking any of them after the first always resolves to the FIRST one's line index. The receiving handler handleCheckboxToggle in document-view.tsx:344-365 rewrites that line ([ ]->[x] or [x]->[ ]) and persists it via updateMutation.mutate PATCH. Net effect: the user clicks checkbox B, but checkbox A is toggled and saved — a silent wrong-item mutation on journal task lists. It is wired into the live journal read path (document-view.tsx:523-525) for all editable (non-typedManaged) documents, so it affects real subscriber daily-note/task-list interactions, not a dead path. Same-state checkbox runs are the common case for to-do lists, so this fires routinely rather than as an edge case.
suggested_fix: Give each checkbox a stable identity tied to its source line rather than matching by state. react-markdown exposes AST node position data: read the node's source line (node.position.start.line, available via a custom input/li component) and forward that exact line index to onCheckboxToggle. Alternatively, keep a per-render running ordinal counter of checkboxes as react-markdown emits inputs in document order, map the nth checkbox to its markdown line index by scanning all checkbox lines once, and toggle by ordinal position instead of by state-match.
evidence: frontend/components/markdown-renderer.tsx:30-48 (handleChange: first-match-by-state loop, calls onCheckboxToggle with first matching line index)
frontend/components/journal/document-view.tsx:344-365 (handleCheckboxToggle rewrites lines[lineIndex] and persists via PATCH)
frontend/components/journal/document-view.tsx:523-525 (MarkdownRenderer wired with onCheckboxToggle for editable, non-typedManaged docs)
expected_behaviour:
`MarkdownRenderer` uses `react-markdown` with `remark-gfm` (GitHub Flavored Markdown: tables, strikethrough, task lists) and `remark-breaks` (newlines as `
`). Prose styling is applied via Tailwind typography classes. When an `onCheckboxToggle` callback is provided, GFM task-list checkboxes are rendered as interactive inputs: clicking scans the raw markdown for the matching checkbox by state (checked/unchecked) and calls `onCheckboxToggle(lineIndex, newChecked)`. Without the callback, checkboxes are rendered as disabled. Tables overflow horizontally (`overflow-x-auto`). The component is `React.memo`'d — it only re-runs the remark pipeline when `content` or `onCheckboxToggle` changes.

detail: Confirmed. redact_user_message (and its dict wrapper redact_telegram_update) is the designated seam for stripping PII from a user's inbound message before forwarding to OpenClaw / third-party LLM providers (redactor.py docstring: PII redaction for outgoing LLM provider traffic), but it has ZERO production call sites. Grep across all non-test Python shows redact_user_message is invoked only inside redactor.py itself, by redact_telegram_update and redact_tool_response; redact_telegram_update itself has no caller at all. All three inbound channels forward raw user text unredacted: (1) Telegram poller._forward_to_container (poller.py:1281-1380) builds message_text = proactive+datetime+chat-marker + raw user text and enqueues it; (2) LINE line_webhook.py:1249 enqueues raw user text; (3) iOS chat_views.py:217 uses raw text directly. The drain path (pending_queue.py:1186,1266) POSTs payload.message_text verbatim to /v1/chat/completions. The only PII calls on these paths are OUTBOUND rehydration (poller.py:329, line_webhook.py:657, pending_queue.py:1514/1561), the reverse operation. The OpenClaw redactPatterns config (config_generator.py:1758+) only masks values in tool-error stderr/log lines, not the LLM message body. Note the irony: tool responses ARE redacted at 4 sites (FA-0915), and pending_queue.py:361's own docstring claims webhooks do "PII redaction... BEFORE enqueuing" — but user-typed message content is not redacted. User impact: every name, email, phone, or other PII a subscriber types in Telegram/LINE/iOS is sent unredacted to third-party model providers, defeating the stated privacy guarantee that providers never see real personal data. The placeholder/rehydrate machinery is only ever fed by tool-response redaction, never by inbound user text.
suggested_fix: Wire the existing inbound seam into each channel's enqueue path before message_text is assembled. In poller._forward_to_container, run the raw user text through redact_user_message(raw_user_text, tenant) (or pass the update dict to redact_telegram_update) and substitute the redacted text into message_text; do the same in line_webhook (before line 1249) and chat_views (before line 217 on the raw text). Keep user_text_excerpt as the redacted form too so persisted ConversationTurn/AppChatMessage rows do not retain raw PII. Add a test asserting an inbound email/name produces a [PERSON_N]/[EMAIL_ADDRESS_N] placeholder in the enqueued payload for all three channels. Because outbound rehydration is already wired, the placeholders will round-trip correctly once inbound redaction mints them.

apps/pii/redactor.py:248-441 (redact_user_message/_redact_user_message/redact_telegram_update defined; redact_telegram_update calls redact_user_message at 429/436)

apps/router/poller.py:1281-1380 (_forward_to_container assembles message_text from raw user text, enqueues, no inbound redaction)
apps/router/line_webhook.py:1249-1261 (message_text from raw user text, enqueued unredacted)
apps/router/chat_views.py:200-231 (iOS: raw text stored + enqueued unredacted)

apps/orchestrator/config_generator.py:1758-1802 (redactPatterns only masks tool-error stderr/log values, not LLM message body)

expected_behaviour: redact_user_message(text, tenant, allow_user_name=True) runs a two-pass redaction. Pass 1: replaces known entities from the existing pii_entity_map using case-insensitive regex (longest names first, skipping empty originals, skipping denylisted entries). Pass 2: runs NER + Presidio on the partially-redacted output to detect new entities, drops hits that fall inside existing placeholder ranges to prevent nested placeholder corruption, resolves new entities against the CI-inverted map, and mints new numbered placeholders for genuinely new entities. New entities are persisted to the DB via Tenant.objects.filter(pk=tenant.pk).update(pii_entity_map=merged) and also updated in-memory. allow_user_name=True (default) excludes the tenant's own display name, first name, and last name from redaction; set False for tool responses so user's surname is not mixed with contact placeholders. On any exception logs and returns original text.

detail: Confirmed real. redact_telegram_update (apps/pii/redactor.py:421-438) has zero production call sites: grep finds it only in its own definition and apps/pii/tests.py. The Telegram poller inbound path (apps/router/poller.py:_forward_to_container, lines 1281-1370) accumulates context markers onto the raw message_text and enqueues it via enqueue_message_for_tenant with NO redaction; the drain task in pending_queue just POSTs the prepared payload to /v1/chat/completions. The defect is actually broader than the claim: redact_user_message AND redact_text also have ZERO production callers (verified by grep over apps/ excluding apps/pii/ and tests). The entire INBOUND user-message PII redaction layer is unwired across ALL chat channels (Telegram, LINE, iOS), not just Telegram. The system is asymmetric: outbound rehydration (rehydrate_text/rehydrate_for_tenant) IS wired everywhere (poller.py:329, pending_queue.py:1517/1564, line_webhook.py:657), tool responses ARE redacted (redact_tool_response at integrations/runtime_views.py:929/1009/1091/3494), and memory_sync/lessons redact via RedactionSession — but nothing mints [PERSON_N] placeholders from the user's own typed chat messages. User impact: raw personal data (names, emails, phone numbers, addresses) a subscriber types into chat is forwarded verbatim to the third-party LLM provider, defeating the stated purpose of the PII subsystem ('PII redaction for outgoing LLM provider traffic', commit #117). The pending_queue.py:361 docstring claiming callers do 'PII redaction ... BEFORE enqueuing' is stale/aspirational — no caller does. The inventory note ('Currently not called') corroborates the gap.
suggested_fix: Wire inbound redaction into the chat ingestion path. Call redact_user_message(raw_user_text, tenant) on the user's typed text inside apps/router/poller.py:_forward_to_container before the proactive/datetime/chat-context markers are prepended (operate on raw_user_text, then build message_text from the redacted result), and apply the same in the LINE webhook and iOS chat ingestion entrypoints so all channels are covered. redact_telegram_update can be used for the webhook-dict shape, but the poller forwards extracted text, so redact_user_message is the right seam there. Also delete or correct the stale 'PII redaction ... BEFORE enqueuing' comment in pending_queue.py once redaction is actually performed.
evidence: apps/router/poller.py:1281-1370 (_forward_to_container enqueues raw message_text with no redaction)
apps/router/poller.py:326-331 (only rehydration on outbound, no inbound redaction)
apps/router/pending_queue.py:343-372 (drain POSTs prepared payload; line 361 stale 'PII redaction BEFORE enqueuing' comment)
grep over apps/ (excl apps/pii/ + tests): zero production callers of redact_user_message/redact_text/redact_telegram_update
expected_behaviour: redact_telegram_update(update_dict, tenant) modifies the update dict in place and returns it. Redacts text from update['message']['text'], update['edited_message']['text'], and update['callback_query']['message']['text'] if those keys are present. Each string is processed via redact_user_message(text, tenant). Non-text fields (IDs, from, chat objects, etc.) are not touched. Returns the mutated dict.

detail: Confirmed real. GoalCard renders an unconditional link to /journal/goal/<slug> (frontend/components/goal-card.tsx:55). Slugs are typed:<uuid> for migrated goals (apps/dashboard/views.py:222) or a legacy Document slug. No /journal/goal/[slug] route exists: frontend/app/journal/ contains only memory/, today/, templates/, layout.tsx and page.tsx (no goal/ subdirectory), and there is NO dynamic-segment route anywhere in app/ (a find for bracketed dirs returns nothing). The app is a static export (output:'export', no trailingSlash, no not-found.tsx), so the route is never generated. GoalCard is the sole entry point for the Active Goals section on Horizons (app/horizons/page.tsx:103), rendered for every goal, with a 'View in Journal' CTA. Clicking it never opens the goal; the entire Active Goals list is a dead end. CAVEAT on the report wording: the literal 'Next.js 404' claim is imprecise. Azure Static Web Apps navigationFallback (frontend/public/staticwebapp.config.json:22-24) rewrites the unmatched path to /index.html, so the user is bounced onto the SPA-bootstrapped landing/index page in an odd shell (usePathname becomes /journal/goal/...) rather than a clean 404 page. The failure surface differs from a literal 404, but the user-facing defect is real and equivalent in impact: the click never shows the goal.
suggested_fix: Either (a) add a client-rendered frontend/app/journal/goal/[slug]/page.tsx that reads the slug via useParams/usePathname and fetches the goal client-side (viable under static export only because navigationFallback already serves index.html, so it must be fully client-fetched; no generateStaticParams since slugs are per-tenant/dynamic); or (b) lower-effort: stop deep-linking and make the card a non-navigating in-place disclosure (expand the preview) or point it at an existing surface such as the journal index, so the CTA does not dead-end.

detail: Confirmed real. The /finance dashboard endpoint returns only active accounts (apps/finance/views.py:203 filters is_active=True), so when a subscriber archives every account, data.accounts is empty so hasAccounts is false (page.tsx:126) and the empty-state early return fires (page.tsx:131-150), rendering "No accounts yet" and returning before the main block. ArchivedAccountsSection is only mounted in the main return (page.tsx:278), so the toggle/list/Restore UI never renders in that state. The archived data is NOT lost — it persists server-side and GET /api/v1/finance/accounts/?archived=true still returns it — but the web UI offers no path to reach the Restore button. The empty-state copy directs the user to "Tell your assistant about your debts and savings", i.e. create a NEW account, not restore; the assistant's finance services also resolve accounts only among is_active=True records (apps/finance/services.py:277-286), so there is no clean assistant-side unarchive either. Net user impact: after archiving all accounts, a subscriber is stranded — the only recovery is to create a brand-new active account (which re-renders the main block and makes the Archived section reappear) and then restore the old ones. Severity is medium rather than high because it requires the all-accounts-archived edge case, no data is lost, and a clumsy recovery path exists.
suggested_fix: Render ArchivedAccountsSection even in the zero-active-accounts state. Simplest: include the ArchivedAccountsSection JSX in the empty-state branch as well (below the "No accounts yet" card at page.tsx:131-150), or hoist it out so it always renders after the conditional content regardless of hasAccounts. Keep the existing showArchived/archivedQuery wiring unchanged.

detail: Confirmed real. NumericInput seeds its internal text state from the value prop only once at mount (useState, workout-detail.tsx:187) with no sync effect and no key-based remount. When the user toggles km<->mi via the STATS unit button (line 1163), setUnit mutates FuelProfile.distance_unit; on mutation success the ["fuel-profile"] query is invalidated and refetched (queries.ts:1505-1513), so useDistanceUnit returns the new unit and CardioStatsEditor recomputes distanceDisplay/elevationDisplay via kmToDisplay/metersToDisplay (lines 1151,1154). The DISTANCE and ELEVATION input boxes receive the new value prop but keep showing the stale pre-toggle number because their internal text state never re-syncs. Two real impacts: (1) visible UX wrongness - the box shows e.g. "5.0" next to a "mi" unit label after toggling from km; (2) silent data corruption - if the user then blurs that field without re-typing, commit() parses the stale text and pushes it through the NEW unit converter (displayToKm/displayToMeters, lines 1183-1185, 1244-1246): a stored 5.0 km still shown as "5.0" commits as displayToKm(5.0,"mi")=8.045 km, silently writing an incorrect canonical distance/elevation into detail_json with no error surfaced. Non-editing display (StatBox displayValue) is correct; only the live edit input is affected.
suggested_fix: Force the DISTANCE and ELEVATION NumericInputs to remount when the unit changes so useState re-seeds from the recomputed value prop: add key={`distance-${unit}`} and key={`elevation-${unit}`} to those two inputs. Alternatively, add a useEffect inside NumericInput that re-syncs text to String(value) when the value prop changes and the input is not currently focused (document.activeElement guard) to avoid clobbering in-progress typing.

detail: Confirmed real, same root-cause class as FA-0317. NumericInput (workout-detail.tsx:172-218) seeds its internal text state once at mount via useState(value == null ? "" : String(value)) and has NO useEffect, and the distance/elevation instances (lines 1181 and 1242) have no key prop, so nothing re-syncs the text state when the value prop changes. When the user clicks the km/mi pill toggle (line 1163), setUnit PATCHes the profile; on success the fuel-profile query is invalidated and refetched, unit flips, and distanceDisplay/elevationDisplay recompute in the new unit (e.g. a stored 5 km becomes kmToDisplay(5,'mi')=3.11 mi). But CardioStatsEditor only re-renders (it does NOT remount; the workout draft detail.distance_km is untouched), so each NumericInput keeps its stale text "5". Two failures result: (1) the input visibly still shows "5" while the field is now labeled miles, contradicting the expected_behaviour that "all distance and elevation inputs display in the selected unit"; (2) if the user blurs the field without retyping, commit() parses the stale "5" and calls onCommit(5), which fires onChange({ distance_km: displayToKm(5,'mi') }). displayToKm(5,'mi') = round(5 * (1/0.621371) * 1000)/1000 = 8.047 km, so a workout the user entered as 5 km is silently re-stored as 8.047 km (elevation is corrupted the same way via displayToMeters). This is silent corruption of logged fitness data triggered by an ordinary "switch me to miles" toggle on an already-populated workout. Severity medium: it requires an existing populated distance/elevation plus a blur without retype, and the toggle is only visible in edit mode, but when those conditions hold the stored value is silently inflated with no user-visible warning.
suggested_fix: Make NumericInput's display state re-sync when the underlying unit/value changes. Quickest fix: give the distance and elevation NumericInputs a unit-derived key so React remounts them on toggle and re-seeds text from the freshly converted value, e.g. key={`distance-${unit}`} at line 1181 and key={`elevation-${unit}`} at line 1242. A more general fix (also covers FA-0317) is to add a useEffect inside NumericInput that resets text from value when value changes and the input is not focused, e.g. track a ref to the input and on value change, if document.activeElement !== inputRef.current, setText(value == null ? "" : String(value)). Either approach ensures the visible number and the committed-on-blur number both reflect the active unit.

detail: Confirmed. Task lists are a supported feature (TaskList + TaskItem nested:true; toolbar Task-list button; the feature record lists Task list and "Tab indents list items; Shift+Tab outdents" as expected). But every indent/outdent path is hardwired to listItem. Toolbar Indent/Outdent buttons call sinkListItem('listItem')/liftListItem('listItem') (markdown-editor.tsx:135,142); the Tab/Shift+Tab handler in editorProps.handleKeyDown does the same (markdown-editor.tsx:254,256). TipTap's TaskItem node name is 'taskItem' (extension-list task-item/index.cjs:141; its own keymap correctly uses 'taskItem' at lines 226,234). Core sinkListItem/liftListItem (core index.cjs:2783,1214) resolve the name to a node type and call prosemirror-schema-list, whose sinkListItem requires firstChild.type==itemType and nodeBefore.type==itemType (prosemirror-schema-list index.cjs:225,232). With a taskItem selected but 'listItem' passed, blockRange finds no matching range and the command returns false — a silent no-op. Worse, the custom handleKeyDown always calls preventDefault() and returns the failing result, so it intercepts Tab/Shift+Tab before TaskItem's own correct built-in keymap can fire, actively breaking nesting the extension would otherwise handle. User impact: inside a task list, the Indent/Outdent toolbar buttons appear active but do nothing, and Tab/Shift+Tab do nothing (and do not even insert a tab, since default is prevented). Bullet/ordered lists are unaffected because they use listItem.
suggested_fix: Make indent/outdent node-type aware: when editor.isActive('taskList') (or 'taskItem'), call sinkListItem('taskItem')/liftListItem('taskItem'); otherwise use 'listItem'. Apply this to both the toolbar Indent/Outdent buttons and the Tab/Shift+Tab handler. Alternatively, drop the custom Tab/Shift+Tab handleKeyDown branch entirely and let listItem's (StarterKit) and taskItem's built-in keymaps handle nesting natively.
expected_behaviour: On desktop, when editing is true and isMobile is not true, DocumentView renders MarkdownEditor inside a rounded card surface. MarkdownEditor is dynamically imported (lazy, shows a skeleton loading placeholder). TipTap is initialized with StarterKit, TaskList, TaskItem (nested), and the tiptap-markdown extension (html:false, transformCopiedText/PastedText). The toolbar (Bold, Italic, Heading 2, Bullet list, Task list, Indent, Outdent) is shown above the editor, sticky at top. Cmd/Ctrl+S triggers handleSave. Tab indents list items; Shift+Tab outdents. A 400ms debounce serializes markdown and calls onChange. Cursor position is saved to localStorage under cursorKey on blur and restored on open. On unmount, any pending debounced change is flushed.

detail: Confirmed. The language save flow (handleLanguageSave -> handleSaveProfileField("language", ...)) runs through the shared handler whose catch block (page.tsx:334) writes the error string into saveMessage: setSaveMessage(error instanceof Error ? error.message : "Failed to save. Please try again."). That state has exactly ONE render site (page.tsx:789-793), styled as a success banner: border-signal/30 bg-signal-faint text-signal (signal/teal is the success token). There is no error-styling branch and no separate error state. So when a language-change API request fails, the failure text is shown inside a teal success banner — a real user reads a failed save as a confirmed save and may believe their language preference persisted when it did not. Same root cause as FA-0440 (display_name) since both flow through the single shared handler and single banner; it equally affects timezone and location saves.
suggested_fix: Distinguish error vs success state. Either add a separate saveError state set in the catch block and render it in a danger-styled banner (e.g. border-destructive/30 bg-destructive-faint text-destructive), or carry a status flag (success|error) alongside saveMessage and choose the banner classes conditionally. Apply once in the shared handler/banner so all fields (display_name, language, timezone, location) are covered.

detail: Confirmed real. For resource=accounts, the order_by allowlist (_ALLOWED_FIELDS, query_views.py:80-81) includes is_debt and payoff_progress, and the group_by allowlist (_ALLOWED_GROUP_BY, query_views.py:123) includes is_debt. On FinanceAccount these are Python @property methods (models.py:81-91), not DB columns. The validators only test membership in those allowlists and do not distinguish properties from real columns. So order_by=is_debt or order_by=payoff_progress passes _order's check (line 318) and reaches qs.order_by(...) at line 323; group_by=is_debt passes _validate_group_by (lines 355-356) and reaches qs.values(is_debt) in _aggregate at line 300. Django's ORM raises FieldError for a non-field name in either order_by() or values(). The handler BaseQueryView.post (apps/common/query_view.py:148-155) catches only QueryExecutionError, so FieldError is uncaught and DRF returns HTTP 500 (no custom EXCEPTION_HANDLER configured in config/). The is_debt FILTER path is correctly translated to account_type__in (lines 189-193) and is unaffected. Existing tests cover order_by=magic (unknown -> 400, tests.py:2302) but never exercise the property-named cases, so the gap is untested. User impact: the AI assistant (nbhd_gravity_query) can emit a syntactically valid query (e.g. order_by=payoff_progress for accounts closest to payoff, or group_by=is_debt for debt vs non-debt totals) and receive a 500 instead of the endpoint's intended strict-400 contract. Agent-only internal read surface; no data corruption.
suggested_fix: Separate serializable fields from orderable/groupable DB columns for accounts. Drop is_debt and payoff_progress from the order-by-eligible set, and drop is_debt from _ALLOWED_GROUP_BY[accounts], so those values return the existing clean 400s (invalid_order_by / unknown_group_by) instead of reaching the ORM. Keep them in the serialization field list so they remain requestable via fields=. Add tests asserting order_by=is_debt, order_by=payoff_progress, and group_by=is_debt all return 400. As a belt-and-suspenders defense, wrap the ORM execution in BaseQueryView.post (or _order/_aggregate) to translate django.core.exceptions.FieldError into a QueryExecutionError(400).

Filters are validated against per-resource allowlists; unknown filter keys return 400. Supports sum/count/avg/min/max aggregates with optional group_by. detail: Confirmed. SleepDetailView.patch (apps/fuel/views.py:1126-1128) calls serializer.is_valid() then serializer.save() with no IntegrityError/conflict guard. SleepLog has unique_together=["tenant","date"] (models.py:499-502), but SleepLogSerializer's fields are ["id","date","duration_hours","quality","notes","created_at"] (serializers.py:235-239) and exclude 'tenant'. DRF's ModelSerializer only auto-generates a UniqueTogetherValidator when every column of the unique_together is a writable serializer field; since 'tenant' is absent, no validator is produced, and 'date' is writable on a partial PATCH. So a PATCH that moves a sleep entry onto a date already occupied by another row for the same tenant passes validation, hits the DB unique constraint, and raises IntegrityError. IntegrityError is not in DRF's handled-exception set, so the response is a 500. BodyWeightDetailView.patch (views.py:621-633) guards the identical case (same unique_together, same tenant-excluded serializer) with an explicit pre-save existence check returning 409 — SleepDetailView is simply missing the mirrored guard. User impact: editing a sleep entry's date onto an already-occupied day returns an opaque 500 and a logged unhandled exception/alert instead of a clean 409. Integrity is preserved by the DB constraint; only the error surface is wrong.
suggested_fix: Mirror the BodyWeightDetailView guard into SleepDetailView.patch: after is_valid(), compute new_date = serializer.validated_data.get("date"); if new_date and new_date != entry.date and SleepLog.objects.filter(tenant=tenant, date=new_date).exclude(id=entry.id).exists(): return Response({"error":"date_conflict","detail":f"An entry already exists for {new_date}."}, status=409) before calling serializer.save().

apps/fuel/serializers.py:235-243 (SleepLogSerializer fields exclude tenant → no auto UniqueTogetherValidator)

detail: Real non-atomicity gap. In RuntimeWorkoutPlanCreateView.post the WorkoutPlan.objects.create() commits in autocommit, then _expand_plan_workouts() runs outside any transaction creating up to ~364 PlanSlot+Workout rows one at a time. A mid-loop failure leaves the plan plus partial workout rows committed. The 500 triggers an assistant retry, and the idempotency check at lines 1256-1262 matches the orphan by (tenant, name, start_date, status=ACTIVE) and returns 200 deduped=True BEFORE re-expanding, so the partial plan is silently locked in. The sibling swap view at line 481 already uses transaction.atomic(), so this view is inconsistent with the team's own pattern. Caveat: the verdict overstates recovery - the PATCH path (lines 1387-1410) runs reconcile_plan_state/apply_reconciliation which backfills missing rows, but only for dates on or after today (services.py:134-136 in_scope); future rows recover, past-dated rows stay gone, and the deduped=True response gives the assistant no signal to even attempt a PATCH. Net impact: a tenant can end up with a structurally incomplete active plan the create endpoint refuses to rebuild. Real, but requires a mid-loop write failure, hence medium not high.
suggested_fix: Wrap the WorkoutPlan.objects.create() plus the _expand_plan_workouts() call in a single transaction.atomic() block (transaction is already imported in the swap view at line 461; import it in this method too) so any mid-loop failure rolls back both the plan row and any partial slot/workout rows, letting the retry recreate the plan cleanly. Optionally also wrap the per-row creates inside _expand_plan_workouts.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/fuel/runtime/{tenant_id}/plans/ authenticates with internal headers. Requires name, schedule_json (non-empty dict keyed by weekday 0-6), weeks (1-52), days_per_week (1-7). Optionally accepts start_date (resolved via resolve_relative_date; defaults to next Monday in tenant tz), objective (max 200 chars), notes, week_overrides (dict of per-week schedule overrides, null values = rest day). Validates and normalizes the entire schedule atomically via _validate_normalize_schedule and _validate_normalize_week_overrides before persisting anything. Idempotent on (tenant, name, start_date, status=active): returns 200 with deduped=true if a duplicate exists. On success, expands schedule into Workout rows + PlanSlot rows via _expand_plan_workouts; creates a background fuel cron (best-effort). Returns 201 with {plan fields, workouts_created}.

detail: Confirmed. The runtime plan PATCH handler stores schedule_json without validation. At runtime_views.py:1358-1361 it only does an isinstance(dict) check then assigns plan.schedule_json = data['schedule_json'], with no call to _validate_normalize_schedule. The POST path (lines 1231-1237) deliberately runs _validate_normalize_schedule FIRST, which invokes set_contract.validate_detail and returns an LLMValidationError envelope (400) so the assistant self-corrects malformed strength/calisthenics sets before anything persists. PATCH skips this. After save, needs_regeneration triggers reconcile_plan_state + apply_reconciliation (lines 1387-1404), which read plan.schedule_json via _parse_schedule_template (services.py:76-93), build WorkoutSpec rows (services.py:208-228), and create Workout rows (services.py:362-377) with detail_json passed straight through; no validate_detail on this path. Malformed sets supplied to a PATCH thus land directly in Workout.detail_json, bypassing the normalization/validation contract POST enforces and never firing the self-correction loop. Corroborated by the codebase's own convention: the single-workout PATCH path (lines 296-304) DOES call normalize_detail+validate_detail on detail_json writes, so the plan-PATCH omission is an inconsistency, not intentional design. Impact is bounded: bad data originates from the assistant LLM over internal auth (not an external user); reconcile silently DROPS structurally-invalid weekday entries (non-int keys, non-dict values) so it cannot crash; but semantically-bad set contents persist into future planned workouts, degrade downstream rendering, and defeat the self-correcting validation guarantee POST provides.
suggested_fix: In RuntimeWorkoutPlanDetailView.patch, mirror the POST path: when 'schedule_json' is present, run normalized, sched_err = _validate_normalize_schedule(data['schedule_json']); if sched_err is not None return it (the LLMValidationError 400 envelope), else assign plan.schedule_json = normalized. For full parity also validate week_overrides via _validate_normalize_week_overrides on PATCH (currently not handled at all). This routes every PATCH-driven set through the same self-correcting validation that POST already enforces.

PATCH partially updates name, status, notes, weeks, schedule_json, days_per_week. If schedule_json or weeks change, triggers plan reconciliation via reconcile_plan_state + apply_reconciliation (not DELETE+INSERT regen — preserves workout UUIDs). Locked workouts are skipped in reconciliation (workouts_locked_skip count). Manages fuel cron: active plan → cron.update; paused/completed/archived → cron.remove. DELETE removes planned workouts, orphans completed ones (plan=null), removes cron (best-effort), returns 204. 404 on plan not found.

detail: Real validation asymmetry. The GET branch of RuntimeDocumentView enforces a YYYY-MM-DD regex for kind=daily (runtime_views.py:1873-1881) before the general validate_kind_slug() check. The PUT branch (runtime_views.py:1940) applies only validate_kind_slug() and omits the daily-date check. validate_kind_slug (apps/journal/path_validation.py:42-64) accepts any slug matching RUNTIME_SLUG_RE (leading alphanumeric, then alphanumeric/dot/underscore/slash/dash) for a valid kind, so a PUT with kind=daily and slug=my-notes succeeds and creates a Document(kind=daily, slug=my-notes) row. RuntimeJournalContextView (runtime_views.py:1394) selects recent daily notes with slug__gte=str(cutoff), a plain Postgres text comparison: 'm' (0x6d) sorts above '2' (0x32) in ASCII, so 'my-notes' >= '2026-06-15' is true and the garbage row passes the date-cutoff filter and is included in the journal context bundle that seeds every agent session; ordered by slug it sorts after all real ISO-date slugs and surfaces as the apparent most-recent daily note. The feature's expected_behaviour explicitly states daily notes must have a YYYY-MM-DD slug and that PUT uses the same validation, so the PUT branch not enforcing it is a genuine gap, not intended behaviour. Impact is bounded: the caller is the trusted-internal OpenClaw container/LLM agent (internal auth, not an external attack surface), so it requires the agent to PUT a non-date daily slug (a typo or generatively-chosen label); when it does, the daily-note context bundle is silently corrupted and can mislead subsequent agent sessions. The same gap also exists in the daily-note set-section endpoint near line 2286, which only defaults an empty slug to today and otherwise relies solely on validate_kind_slug.
suggested_fix: Extract the GET branch's daily-date guard (lines 1873-1881) into a shared helper such as validate_daily_slug(kind, slug) in apps/journal/path_validation.py and call it in the PUT handler before line 1940, and in the set-section endpoint near line 2286. Alternatively, fold the date check directly into validate_kind_slug when kind == 'daily' so all write paths inherit it uniformly.
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/integrations/runtime/<tenant_id>/document/?kind=<kind>&slug=<slug> — internal auth. Validates kind+slug via validate_kind_slug (path-component safety, NTFS-hostile names rejected). Daily notes must have YYYY-MM-DD slug. Creates Document via get_or_create if missing. Returns {tenant_id, id, kind, slug, title, markdown, updated_at}. PUT body: {kind, slug, markdown, title?}; same validation; creates or fully replaces. Returns 200 if existed, 201 if created.

detail: Confirmed real. SessionDetailView (apps/journal/session_views.py:185) sets permission_classes = [HasSessionsReadScope] at the class level, so it applies to BOTH its get and delete handlers. The delete handler (lines 195-204) permanently destroys a session via session.delete(). Because the guard only requires the sessions:read scope, a PAT issued with scopes ["sessions:read"] only can permanently delete sessions despite being a token the user provisioned as read-only. The PAT create endpoint explicitly allows an arbitrary subset of scopes (apps/tenants/pat_views.py:18-25 validates only against ALLOWED_PAT_SCOPES = {sessions:write, sessions:read}), so a read-only-only PAT is genuinely user-provisionable. The codebase itself treats the two scopes as distinct write/read tiers: the write path SessionCreateView correctly uses HasSessionsWriteScope (line 81), confirming the inconsistency is a bug, not intended behaviour. Impact is bounded: the delete is filtered by tenant=tenant (line 200), so a token can only delete its own tenant's sessions (no cross-tenant exposure), and JWT console requests bypass scope checks entirely -- read-only PATs are the only affected principal. Hence medium, not high. A user who shares or stores a deliberately read-only PAT unknowingly grants it destructive delete capability over their own session data.
suggested_fix: Move the destructive verb off the class-level read permission. Either override get_permissions() in SessionDetailView to return [HasSessionsWriteScope()] for DELETE and [HasSessionsReadScope()] for GET, or split DELETE into a separate view/handler guarded by HasSessionsWriteScope, mirroring SessionCreateView. Add a regression test asserting a sessions:read-only PAT receives 403 on DELETE while a sessions:write PAT succeeds.

apps/tenants/permissions.py:12,34-39 (distinct sessions:write vs sessions:read scope classes)

feature: Templates.md (Daily Note Template Reference) in Workspace
detail: Confirmed real. render_workspace_files() populates NBHD_SKILL_TEMPLATES_MD (personas.py:519-520, rendered from the tenant's live NoteTemplate via render_templates_md at personas.py:322-356). Both the config-refresh path (services.py:599) and provision path (services.py:337) call it, but in update_tenant_config() the upload step only writes keys enumerated in file_map_overwrite (services.py:602-614) and file_map_seed_once (services.py:621-624); NBHD_SKILL_TEMPLATES_MD is in NEITHER dict, so workspace/skills/nbhd-managed/daily-journal/references/templates.md is never re-uploaded on a config refresh. That file is only written from the env var at container boot by entrypoint.sh:29-35. Crucially, when a user edits their default template, TemplateDetailView.patch (views.py:495-513) explicitly fires update_tenant_config_task with the comment 'Push updated skill config to the agent's container when the default template changes' — but that refresh writes to the share without redeploying the container and silently skips templates.md, so the stated intent no-ops. The running container keeps its provision-time env var and the share keeps the stale slug list. After a section rename/add/remove, the agent's templates.md reference (the canonical slug source the prompt points it at, personas.py:311 and 354) is stale, so nbhd_daily_note_set_section calls using those slugs can write to the wrong section or error. Impact is real but bounded: it requires a post-provision default-template edit (uncommon) and the failure is a wrong-section write or tool error, not data loss — hence medium, not high.
suggested_fix: In apps/orchestrator/services.py, add an entry mapping NBHD_SKILL_TEMPLATES_MD to workspace/skills/nbhd-managed/daily-journal/references/templates.md inside the file_map_overwrite dict (services.py:602-614) so config refreshes overwrite templates.md on the file share. The destination path must match entrypoint.sh:31. The directory must already exist (uploaded skills are copied from the image at boot), so guarding for directory existence as entrypoint.sh does is not needed for the share write; upload_workspace_file already creates parent paths.

detail: Confirmed. In CronDeliveryView.post the 20-messages/hour cap is CHECKED for every channel at line 156 (if not _check_rate_limit(tid)), but the counter is only INCREMENTED via _record_send(tenant_id) inside the Telegram branch (line 301, in _send_via_telegram) and the LINE branch (line 405, in _send_via_line). The app-channel branch (lines 190-196) builds its success Response inline (status=sent, channel=app) and never calls _record_send. The counter is keyed by tenant_id and shared across channels; record_proactive_outbound (apps/router/proactive_context.py:73) does NO rate limiting, it only persists the ProactiveOutbound row and dispatches the APNs push. Net effect: for iOS-only tenants (the app channel, the common App Store case per resolve_user_channel), _check_rate_limit always returns True regardless of how many proactive/cron messages were sent in the last hour, so the runaway-cron-loop safety throttle (purpose stated at line 23) is silently ineffective for that channel. The feature spec lists 'Rate limit: 20 messages/hour per tenant' as an endpoint-wide invariant, not a Telegram/LINE-only one, so this is unintended. Impact is bounded: it only bites under a runaway/looping cron, and the iOS push leg has its own notified_at idempotency, but a malfunctioning or looping cron on an iOS-only tenant could emit unlimited ?since= feed rows + APNs pushes with no backstop, exactly what this cap exists to prevent. Hence medium, not high. The existing test (apps/router/test_cron_delivery.py:77 test_rate_limit) pre-fills _rate_counts manually and uses the default Telegram channel, so it never exercises the app-channel increment path and did not catch this gap.
suggested_fix: Increment the counter for the app channel. Cleanest fix: since _check_rate_limit at line 156 already runs for all channels, move the _record_send(tid) call to the shared post-routing success block (lines 209-218) where 200<=resp.status_code<300, so every 2xx response increments the counter exactly once for all channels, and drop the per-branch _record_send calls in _send_via_telegram and _send_via_line. Minimal alternative: add _record_send(tid) inside the app-channel branch right after building the Response (after line 196). Also add an app-channel rate-limit test that drives sends through the endpoint.
evidence: apps/router/cron_delivery.py:154-220 (rate check at 156 runs for all channels; app branch 190-196 builds inline Response without _record_send)
apps/router/test_cron_delivery.py:77-84 (test_rate_limit pre-fills counter, Telegram-only, misses app channel)
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/internal/runtime/<tenant_id>/send/ (mounted via integrations/urls.py). Auth: X-NBHD-Internal-Key + X-NBHD-Tenant-Id. tenant.status != ACTIVE→200 {status:'blocked'} (no retry). Channel resolution: prefers user.preferred_channel if linked; fallbacks: any linked messaging channel; iOS app (DeviceToken exists) as last resort. No delivery surface→422. Rate limit: 20 messages/hour per tenant (in-process memory, resets on restart). Body: {message, parse_mode (Markdown|HTML|plain, default Markdown)}. PII rehydration before send. Telegram: markdown→Telegram HTML (render_telegram_html), HTML fallback to plain text on 400; LINE: push API. channel='app': only APNs + ?since= feed (no chat message sent). On success records ProactiveOutbound row, dispatches iOS APNs push. X-NBHD-Job-Name header is captured in ProactiveOutbound.job_name.

feature: Telegram message forwarding to OpenClaw (poller path)
detail: Confirmed real. Production Telegram runs the central long-poller, not the webhook: startup.sh (lines 14, 61) launches the poll_telegram management command, and TelegramPoller.start() deletes any registered webhook on startup (poller.py:70-71), so the webhook view receives no live Telegram traffic. The poller subscribes to callback_query updates (allowed_updates at poller.py:154) and routes them in _handle_update's callback block (poller.py:726-837), which has explicit prefix cases for tz_, lesson:, extract:, gate_approve:/gate_deny:, container_update:, and agent: but NO case for task_action:. The nightly extraction summary sends Remove/Undo buttons with callback_data task_action:undo:ID (extraction.py:353). When a Telegram subscriber taps that button, the callback matches none of the poller's prefixes, falls through the callback block unhandled, is never answered (no answerCallbackQuery), and the undo is never performed (undo_task_action never called). The working handler handle_task_action_callback (task_action_callbacks.py:69-111) is wired only into the webhook view (views.py:234-237) and the LINE webhook (line_webhook.py:1301), both bypassed by the Telegram production path. User impact: every Telegram user who taps the morning reconciliation Undo button gets a spinning/no-op button and the reconciled change is not reverted. Recoverable (the user can ask the assistant in chat to revert), so degraded-feature rather than data loss, hence medium not high.
suggested_fix: Add a task_action: prefix case to the poller's callback block in poller.py (alongside the extract:/lesson: cases) that delegates to apps.router.task_action_callbacks.handle_task_action_callback(update, tenant) and runs the returned JsonResponse through the poller's _execute_telegram_response helper, mirroring how _handle_extraction_callback already bridges a webhook-style handler into the poller. Add a poller-path test asserting the undo executes and the callback is answered.
expected_behaviour: _forward_to_container in the poller decorates message_text with proactive_context block + datetime marker + chat context marker, then calls enqueue_message_for_tenant (PendingMessage queue). The queue drain task POSTs to https://{container_fqdn}/v1/chat/completions with Authorization: Bearer {gateway_token}, X-Telegram-Chat-Id, X-Channel: telegram, X-User-Timezone headers. On a healthy response, relay_ai_response_to_telegram rehydrates PII, strips/records insights markers, renders [[chart:]] markers into images uploaded to Azure File Share, sends MEDIA: images via sendPhoto, and delivers the markdown response via _send_telegram_markdown (render_telegram_html → parse_mode=HTML with plain-text fallback on 400). Returns True on success. Records token usage via _record_usage_safe.

feature: Automation run rate limiting and idempotency
detail: Confirmed real. In execute_automation, RUNNING is committed at services.py:435-437, but dispatch (line 440) and the next_run_at advance (468-470) happen after it, outside the get_or_create atomic block. A hard process kill (OOM/eviction/SIGKILL) between the RUNNING save and dispatch leaves the AutomationRun permanently RUNNING and never advances next_run_at. The scheduled idempotency key is window-deterministic (services.py:234-236 derives epoch from scheduled_for; scheduler.py:48 always passes scheduled_for=automation.next_run_at). With next_run_at not advanced, the next tick re-selects the same automation (scheduler.py:22-26) and rebuilds the identical key; get_or_create returns the existing RUNNING row (created=False) and short-circuits at services.py:432-433 without dispatching or advancing. The automation is re-selected forever while doing nothing. No stale-RUNNING reaper exists for AutomationRun (one exists for router messages at cron/views.py:177). Impact: the automation is silently dead until an operator manually resolves the row; no error surfaces (run_due_automations counts it merely as processed) and it permanently holds a due-batch slot. Medium not high: requires a hard kill in a sub-ms window (normal dispatch exceptions are caught at line 447, marked FAILED, and still advance next_run_at), so per-occurrence probability is low, but Azure Container Apps do get evicted/restarted and the failure is permanent and unrecoverable without manual intervention.
suggested_fix: Add a stale-RUNNING reaper cron (mirroring reap_stuck_inbound_messages) that finds AutomationRun rows in RUNNING with started_at older than a threshold, marks them FAILED, and advances the automation's next_run_at. Alternatively, advance next_run_at inside the same atomic block as run creation (before dispatch) so a crash cannot strand the schedule, and/or treat a stale RUNNING record found by get_or_create as re-dispatchable based on started_at age.
expected_behaviour: _check_limits(automation, reference_utc) enforces: (1) MIN_RUN_INTERVAL=120 minutes between consecutive runs (compared against automation.last_run_at); (2) MAX_RUNS_PER_DAY=12 runs within the tenant's calendar day in the automation's own timezone (computed via _day_window_for_timezone). For manual trigger_source, a failed limit check raises AutomationLimitError (view returns 400). For schedule trigger_source, a failed limit check creates a SKIPPED run (with error_message) and advances next_run_at — no exception is raised to the caller. Idempotency is enforced via AutomationRun.idempotency_key (unique DB constraint). Scheduled keys are 'schedule:<automation_id>:<epoch>'; manual keys are 'manual:<automation_id>:<epoch>:<uuid4>'. get_or_create inside an atomic transaction ensures that concurrent duplicate scheduled invocations result in only one run being dispatched.

feature: Constellation Graph — Relationship Navigation (Jump to Neighbour)
detail: Confirmed real. The Inspector Relationships list (neighbors) is computed from graphData.edges (ALL edges) with no visibility filter (page.tsx:565), rendering a clickable button per neighbour (page.tsx:340-345). By default kindFilter excludes Evidence and Tag (page.tsx:387 = Lesson, Cluster) and relFilter excludes EVIDENCED_BY and TAGGED_WITH (page.tsx:388 = IN_CLUSTER, SIMILAR_TO, REFINES). So selecting a Lesson that has tags or a source_ref shows TAGGED_WITH / EVIDENCED_BY neighbour buttons even though those kinds are hidden by default. Each button onClick = onJump(other.id) = focusNode (wired at page.tsx:733). focusNode (page.tsx:564): const p = positions[String(id)]; if (!p) return;. positions derives from baseLayout = layoutGraph(visibleNodes, ...) (page.tsx:430), and layoutGraph writes position entries only for the nodes handed to it (page.tsx:131-132). A hidden-kind node has no position entry, so focusNode returns immediately and does nothing - not even the setSelectedId after the early return. The user clicks a normal-looking relationship button and gets zero feedback. Reachable under default filters; no crash or data loss, hence medium.
suggested_fix: In focusNode, when positions[String(id)] is missing, reveal the target instead of silently returning: enable the neighbour kind (setKindFilter add) and the connecting rel type (setRelFilter add) so the next layout pass includes it, then jump. Alternatively, in the neighbors useMemo (page.tsx:565) annotate each entry with visibleIds.has(String(other.id)) and render hidden neighbours as dimmed/disabled with a hint, or auto-toggle the relevant filters on click. Avoid dropping hidden neighbours entirely since the relationship info is useful.
evidence: frontend/app/constellation/page.tsx:565 neighbors uses graphData.edges, no visibility filter
frontend/app/constellation/page.tsx:564 focusNode early-returns when positions[id] missing
frontend/app/constellation/page.tsx:131-132 layoutGraph builds pos only from passed graphNodes
frontend/app/constellation/page.tsx:387-388 default kindFilter omits Evidence/Tag, relFilter omits EVIDENCED_BY/TAGGED_WITH
expected_behaviour: The Inspector lists all graph edges touching the selected node (filtered to visible edges). Each entry shows direction arrow, relationship type, similarity score if SIMILAR_TO, a colour dot, and the neighbour's label. Clicking a neighbour button calls focusNode(id): it pans to the node's world position scaled by baseScale*1.4, sets zoom to 1.4, and sets selectedId to that node. The Inspector immediately re-renders for the new node.

detail: Both sub-claims verified; the material one is the silent save failure. (2) document-view.tsx:313-327 handleSave awaits updateMutation.mutateAsync with NO try/catch, and the hook (lib/queries.ts:904-914 useUpdateDocumentMutation) defines only onSuccess, no onError, with no toast/error UI anywhere in the component. If the desktop Save PATCH fails (network error, 409, 5xx), the promise rejects: the await throws, execution stops before setEditing(false)/setSavedIndicator, so the editor stays open (no data loss, which is good) but the user sees NO error AND no Saved confirmation, leaving them unsure whether the save landed. The contrast is telling: the sibling handleDone (mobile path, lines 295-311) deliberately wraps the identical mutateAsync call in try/catch with a comment about not silently losing keystrokes, so the failure path was considered for mobile but omitted for desktop handleSave (and even handleDone only swallows the error to idle without surfacing it). This is a real UX gap. (1) The desktop Save/Cancel/Edit buttons at document-header.tsx:193/200/209 do use min-h-[40px], 4px under the WCAG 2.1 AA 44px target named in CLAUDE.md/DESIGN.md, and are reachable on mobile when editing (the line-185 guard renders them when editing===true). But this is a trivial spec deviation, and the codebase uses min-h-[40px] widely as a de-facto size (document-header:99/140/153, document-view:400/445), so it is a low-priority nit, not the driver. Net: real ux defect, medium, driven by the silent save failure.
suggested_fix: Wrap handleSave's mutateAsync in try/catch (mirroring handleDone) and on failure surface a visible error, e.g. a saveStatus error state rendered in the header or a toast, keeping the editor open for retry; optionally add onError to useUpdateDocumentMutation as a global fallback. Separately (low priority), bump the three header buttons from min-h-[40px] to min-h-[44px] to meet the stated touch-target spec.

Clicking Save calls handleSave (mutateAsync PATCH /api/v1/journal/documents/{kind}/{slug}/), which then exits editing mode and shows the 2s 'Saved' indicator. Clicking Cancel exits editing without saving, resetting draft state. While saving (updatePending), the Save button shows '...' and is disabled. The Edit button is hidden (null) for typed-managed documents (tasks/goal when typed_lifecycle is true). On mobile, the Edit button is hidden in the header — the floating pencil FAB handles that.

detail: Confirmed by source. (1) offsetLabel() (page.tsx:192-217) has a static map of only 18 zones and returns the literal "UTC" for every other zone (line 216: return map[tz] ?? "UTC"). findTimezoneLabel() (219-221) renders `${tz} (${offsetLabel(tz)})`. The TIMEZONE_GROUPS dropdown lists well over 100 IANA zones, so the large majority render misleading labels like "Africa/Lagos (UTC)" (actually UTC+1), "Europe/Brussels (UTC)" (UTC+1/+2), "Asia/Bangkok (UTC)" (UTC+7). The wrong label shows both in the read-only display (line 581: findTimezoneLabel(me.timezone || "UTC")) and in every edit-mode option (line 595). (2) Duplicate entries: Europe group has Europe/Zurich (lines 107 and 111) and Europe/Madrid (lines 101 and 112) twice; Africa group has Africa/Cairo (lines 171 and 186) twice. The React option key is `${group.region}-${tz}` (line 594), so these yield duplicate visible options and duplicate React keys (e.g. "Europe-Europe/Zurich"), triggering a console key warning and redundant dropdown rows. User impact: a user picking/reviewing their timezone sees a factually wrong UTC offset next to most zones and the same city listed twice. The saved value is the correct IANA string itself, so no data corruption — purely a misleading display plus duplicate render, hence medium not high.
suggested_fix: Replace the hand-rolled static map with Intl.DateTimeFormat('en-US', { timeZone: tz, timeZoneName: 'shortOffset' }) (or longOffset) to compute the real offset/abbreviation for any IANA zone at runtime, with a graceful fallback for invalid zones. Separately, de-duplicate the zone arrays — remove the second Europe/Zurich, Europe/Madrid, and Africa/Cairo entries (or pass each group's zones through a Set before rendering).
