Clicking Edit shows a grouped `<select optgroup>` covering Popular, North America, Europe, Asia, South America, Africa regions. Save calls `PATCH /api/v1/tenants/profile/` with `{ timezone }`. On success a contextual message is shown: if the timezone changed, "Saved! Agent timezone updated. Changes take effect on next message."; otherwise "Saved!". The message auto-clears after 3 s.

detail: Confirmed off-by-one in the weekly frequency path of the schedule builder. WEEKDAY_LABELS maps array index 0=Mon through 6=Sun (lines 30-38). In the weekly day picker (lines 364-379) the array index is stored directly into state.weekdays, and buildCronFromState joins those indices verbatim into the cron day-of-week field (lines 119-123). But the cron consumer uses standard Unix/QStash DOW semantics where 0=Sunday and 1=Monday, confirmed by the project's own system crons (apps/cron/management/commands/register_system_crons.py:78, "0 5 * * 0" documented as "Weekly Sunday 05:00"). Result: selecting Mon emits cron 0 (fires Sunday); Tue emits 1 (fires Monday); every weekly selection fires one day earlier than displayed. The default weekdays:[1] (line 252) shows "Tuesday" in the UI (WEEKDAY_NAMES[1]) but emits cron 1 = Monday. The bug is masked on round-trip because parseCronExpr reads DOW values back into the same index space (lines 173-189) and cronToHuman/WEEKDAY_NAMES (lines 209-215) map index->name with the identical offset, so the UI always re-displays the same wrong label and never surfaces the mismatch. The weekdays preset (1-5) and weekends preset (0,6) are correct because they are hardcoded to proper cron values; only the custom weekly path is affected. User impact: weekly assistant crons run one day earlier than the day the user picked, and Sun (index 6) collides with Saturday in cron (where 6=Saturday).
suggested_fix: Map the Monday-first label index to the correct cron DOW (Sunday=0). On emit in buildCronFromState, convert each stored index via ((index + 1) % 7) before joining; on parse in parseCronExpr, convert each cron DOW back to the Monday-first index via ((dow + 6) % 7) before populating state.weekdays; keep formatHumanWeekdays's lookup consistent. Alternatively, reorder WEEKDAY_LABELS/WEEKDAY_NAMES to start with Sunday so index == cron value, and update the default weekdays:[1].
evidence: frontend/components/schedule-builder.tsx:119-123 (buildCronFromState emits weekday indices verbatim as cron DOW)
frontend/components/schedule-builder.tsx:173-189 (parseCronExpr reads cron DOW back into same index space)
frontend/components/schedule-builder.tsx:248-254,364-390 (default weekdays:[1] and day-picker index click handler)
expected_behaviour: The `<ScheduleBuilder>` defaults to easy mode when the cron expression can be parsed. Easy mode offers Frequency select (Every day, Weekdays, Weekends, Weekly, Monthly), conditional weekday pills for weekly frequency, a day-of-month select (1st–28th) for monthly, and Hour / Minute selects (minute in 00/15/30/45 increments). The cron expression shown as a human label ("Every day at 09:00") updates live above the builder. Switching to "Use cron expression" reveals a mono text input. Switching back parses the raw expression; if it is an unsupported pattern, shows a rose parse-error message.

detail: Confirmed real. apply_single_tenant_config_task advances config_version to pending_config_version unconditionally (tasks.py:182-187) regardless of whether update_tenant_config actually wrote. update_tenant_config (services.py:570-581) guards: if status is not ACTIVE or container_id is empty, it logs a warning and returns silently WITHOUT raising. So if a tenant becomes SUSPENDED or loses container_id after the task version gate at tasks.py:171 but before the status re-read at services.py:574, the file-share write is skipped, yet the task still stamps config_version equal to pending_config_version. The race window is wider than the report frames it: it spans the whole QStash delivery latency between apply_pending_configs enqueuing the task (when tenant was ACTIVE, awake, idle; cron/views.py:441-446) and the services.py:574 re-read. Once config_version is wrongly advanced, no automated path recovers the dropped change: apply_pending_configs only enqueues when pending greater than config (cron/views.py:442); billing reactivation re-queues only if pending greater than config (billing/services.py:370-373), now false; idle-wake reconcile only force-bumps for version_synced or config_version zero (hibernation.py:544); even the operator manual force_apply_configs gates on pending greater than config (force_apply_configs.py:21), so a human force-apply also no-ops. The dropped change reaches the share only after some unrelated future config edit re-bumps pending or a deliberate bump_config_version. Impact: a config change (model swap, cron-prompt edit) silently never reaches the container; the dashboard reports it as applied (has_pending_update false, tenants/views.py:283); recovery needs a manual re-bump nothing prompts for. Genuine but low-frequency (suspensions are Stripe-webhook driven and must coincide with an in-flight apply) and self-limiting (next legitimate config edit re-applies). Medium because it silently drops persisted desired state and evades every recovery path including manual force-apply.
suggested_fix: Make update_tenant_config signal whether it actually wrote (return False, or raise a typed SkippedNoActiveContainer, when the status/container guard at services.py:574 short-circuits) and have apply_single_tenant_config_task only advance config_version/applied_model when a real write occurred. On the skip path, leave pending_config_version greater than config_version so apply_pending_configs and the billing reactivation re-queue path naturally retry once the tenant is ACTIVE again. Apply the same return-value check in force_apply_configs.py and byo_models/services.py which advance config_version in lockstep.
expected_behaviour: `apply_single_tenant_config_task(tenant_id, _is_followup_retry=False)`. Looks up tenant; if not found, returns. If `config_version >= pending_config_version`, returns (nothing pending). Calls `update_tenant_config(tenant_id)` — on exception, logs and returns without advancing version. After successful update, advances `config_version = pending_config_version`, stamps `config_refreshed_at`, `applied_model`, `applied_model_at`. For hibernated tenants, logs that the file write is ready for wake but does not wake. The `_is_followup_retry` flag is accepted for compatibility but no longer drives retry behavior.

detail: Real. Password is rotated and committed at line 107 (set_unusable_password bumps password_last_changed_at via the model override at models.py:109-118, invalidating all of that user's JWTs) BEFORE the reset email is attempted at line 115. The rotated counter increments only on email success (line 116); on email failure only email_failed increments (line 118) and the user is left locked out. Because the idempotency key IS password_last_changed_at, re-running with the same --since (the documented recovery, docstring lines 12-13) sees the bumped stamp at line 96 and SKIPS that user, so the reset email is never auto-resent. The user is locked out (no session, no reset link) with no automated recovery; manual only, which the code itself admits is unbuilt (line 132: --resend-only is a future enhancement). OVERSTATED sub-claim: the 'summary undercounts/misleads the operator' part is weaker than stated -- email_failed IS printed with a loud warning and an explicit 'check logs and manually resend' instruction (lines 128-134), so the operator is told the follow-up count and is not actually misled. Also note the QStash task path (tasks.py rotate_all_passwords_task) passes no --since, so an automatic re-fire uses a fresh now() cutoff that would re-rotate/re-email the failed user; the silent-skip lockout specifically bites the documented manual same---since retry. No test covers the email-failure-then-retry path (test_idempotent_on_rerun is happy-path only). Net: genuine lockout/data-integrity gap for users hit by a transient email failure during the campaign.
suggested_fix: Decouple 'rotated' from 'emailed' so a same---since retry can recover email-failed users. Options: (a) add the --resend-only flag the code already references at line 132, which re-sends to users flagged as rotated-but-not-emailed; (b) track a separate reset_email_sent_at field and key the idempotency skip (line 96) on that rather than password_last_changed_at; or (c) reorder to send the email before bumping the stamp (mind the token-vs-new-hash dependency at lines 109-113).
expected_behaviour: python manage.py rotate_all_passwords --reason=<string> [--dry-run] [--since=<ISO datetime>]. Excludes the PLATFORM_OWNER_EMAIL user. Iterates all users skipping: those with no email, those whose password_last_changed_at >= --since (idempotency). For each eligible user: calls user.set_unusable_password() (bumps password_last_changed_at, invalidates all JWTs), saves with update_fields, generates a uid+token, sends a reset email using privacy_rotation_2026/email_1_* templates. Email send failures are logged but do not stop iteration. Prints rotated/skipped/failed counts. Idempotent: re-running with the same --since skips already-rotated users.

feature: Confirmation message dispatch — send gate prompt to user's platform
detail: Confirmed real. send_gate_confirmation reads tenant.user.preferred_channel directly and indexes _SENDERS, which only maps 'telegram' and 'line'. It does NOT call resolve_user_channel, the function that knows to fall back to the 'app' channel for iOS-only users. An iOS-only user has no telegram_chat_id/line_user_id but does have a DeviceToken, and preferred_channel defaults to 'telegram' (the model only allows 'telegram'/'line' as choices; 'app' is not a valid stored value). So the Telegram sender runs, finds chat_id is None, logs 'Tenant X has no Telegram chat_id' and returns None. The PendingAction is still created but platform_message_id/platform_channel are never set, so the user receives no approve/deny prompt. There is no in-app gate surface: approval handlers exist only in the Telegram poller and LINE webhook; nothing consumes a gate via APNs/DeviceToken. By default gating is ON (Tenant.gate_all_actions=True, gate_acknowledged_risk=False, GatePreference.require_confirmation defaults True), so destructive actions (gmail_delete, calendar_delete, drive_delete, task_delete, gmail_send) require confirmation and silently expire after 5 minutes for iOS-only users, returning 'expired' to the container with no explanation. Blast radius is limited because all ActionTypes are Google Workspace / task deletes, so an iOS-only App Store user must have GWS connected AND trigger such an action to hit it -- hence medium, not high.
suggested_fix: In send_gate_confirmation, resolve the channel via resolve_user_channel(tenant.user) instead of reading preferred_channel directly. When it returns 'app' (or None while a DeviceToken exists), deliver the confirmation as an APNs push plus a ?since= feed row, and add an app-side approve/deny path (a gate respond endpoint the iOS app can call against GateRespondView's logic) so iOS-only users can act on it. Minimum interim guard: when no usable messaging channel exists, do not leave the action to silently expire -- surface a clear failure back to the container/user instead.
evidence: apps/actions/messaging.py:37-40 (telegram sender returns None when chat_id is None)
apps/router/cron_delivery.py:48-83 (resolve_user_channel falls back to 'app' for DeviceToken users -- not used here)
apps/tenants/models.py:569-576 (gate_all_actions default True, gate_acknowledged_risk default False)
apps/actions/views.py:142-188 (action created + send_gate_confirmation called; gating not auto-approved by default)
apps/router/poller.py:751 + apps/router/line_webhook.py:1290-1370 (approve/deny handlers exist only for Telegram/LINE, none for app)
expected_behaviour: send_gate_confirmation(tenant, action) reads tenant.user.preferred_channel (defaults to 'telegram'). For Telegram: calls Telegram sendMessage with MarkdownV2 text including the rehydrated display_summary (PII tokens expanded) and an inline keyboard with callback_data 'gate_approve:<id>' and 'gate_deny:<id>'. Falls back to plain-text sendMessage if MarkdownV2 fails. Stores the returned message_id in action.platform_message_id and 'telegram' in action.platform_channel. For LINE: sends a Flex Message bubble with postback buttons, stores 'line-push-<action_id>' as the message_id (LINE push API does not return a message ID). If no suitable sender is found for the channel, logs a warning and does nothing.

detail: Confirmed at the code level. AutomationManualRunView.post (views.py:112-119) only catches AutomationLimitError (returns HTTP 400). When _dispatch_to_openclaw raises AutomationExecutionError -- tenant not ACTIVE (services.py:204-205), no container_fqdn (services.py:206-207), or missing telegram_chat_id (services.py:177-179) -- that exception is swallowed by the broad except Exception in execute_automation (services.py:447-449), which marks run.status=FAILED, records error_message, persists the run, and returns it normally with no re-raise. The view then unconditionally returns HTTP 201 Created with the FAILED run serialized. AutomationRunSerializer exposes status and error_message, so the failure IS surfaced -- but only in the response body, with an HTTP-level success (201) signal. A subscriber clicking Run now gets a success status code even when the automation could not be dispatched/delivered; a client must inspect body.status == FAILED to detect it. This is a real REST-semantics/UX inconsistency. Severity is low because the failure is reported in the body (not silently dropped), the run record is persisted and queryable via the runs-list endpoint, and the frontend can branch on the status field. It is not a data-integrity or security issue -- purely a mismatch between HTTP status and actual outcome on the manual-run path.
suggested_fix: In AutomationManualRunView.post, after execute_automation returns, inspect run.status: if it equals AutomationRun.Status.FAILED, return the serialized run with HTTP 502 (or 409) plus error_message instead of 201. Alternatively, have execute_automation re-raise AutomationExecutionError for MANUAL trigger_source (mirroring the existing MANUAL-specific AutomationLimitError re-raise at services.py:397-398) and let the view map it to a 4xx/5xx with a detail message, while keeping SCHEDULE-triggered runs swallowing the exception so the cron path stays resilient.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/automations/<uuid:automation_id>/run/ requires IsAuthenticated. Calls execute_automation(automation, trigger_source='manual'). Enforces two rate limits before dispatching: (1) MIN_RUN_INTERVAL=120 minutes since last_run_at; (2) MAX_RUNS_PER_DAY=12 runs within the tenant's local calendar day. If either limit is breached, raises AutomationLimitError, view returns 400 with {'detail': reason}. If limits pass, creates an AutomationRun with idempotency_key='manual:<automation_id>:<timestamp>:<uuid4>' (always unique for manual runs). Builds a synthetic Telegram update with a PKM-prompt text for the automation kind, then calls forward_to_openclaw(tenant.container_fqdn, synthetic_update, user_timezone=...) in a new asyncio event loop. Requires tenant.status=ACTIVE and tenant.container_fqdn set — otherwise raises AutomationExecutionError and the run is marked FAILED. On success, run.status=succeeded and automation.last_run_at is updated. Manual trigger does NOT advance next_run_at. Returns 201 with AutomationRunSerializer JSON. If the run was a duplicate idempotency_key (get_or_create returned existing), returns the existing run without re-dispatching.

detail: Confirmed asymmetry in the webhook dispatcher (apps/billing/views.py:161-172). Both checkout.session.completed and checkout.session.async_payment_succeeded enter the same match arm. Branch logic: (1) credit top-up (mode==payment, kind==credit_topup) routes to handle_credit_topup_completed, which handles BOTH events including async; (2) elif event_type == checkout.session.completed routes to handle_checkout_completed; (3) else, log only. The else branch is reached exactly when the event is async_payment_succeeded AND it is NOT a credit top-up, i.e. a SUBSCRIPTION whose payment cleared asynchronously. In that case handle_checkout_completed is never called, so the tenant is never provisioned/reactivated and tier/customer/subscription ids are never written. The feature expected_behaviour explicitly lists async_payment_succeeded as a trigger for subscription OR credit top-up, so the missing subscription leg is a genuine gap, not intended behaviour. Compounding it, handle_checkout_completed (services.py:296) does not gate on payment_status, so for a true async subscription the synchronous completed event arrives with payment_status unpaid yet the only confirmation event (async_payment_succeeded) is dropped. WHY LOW SEVERITY: reachability depends on Stripe Dashboard config. The subscription Checkout session is created at views.py:288 with mode=subscription and NO payment_method_types argument, defaulting to whatever methods are enabled in the Stripe Dashboard. With card-only (Stripe default) the async event never fires for subscriptions, so no real subscriber is affected today, and nothing in the repo enables ACH/SEPA/bank-debit. It is a real but latent, config-conditional logic bug that only bites if an async/delayed payment method is enabled in the Dashboard.
suggested_fix: Make the subscription leg symmetric with the credit-topup leg. After the credit-topup branch, route any non-credit session of EITHER event type to handle_checkout_completed, e.g. replace the elif/else with: else: handle_checkout_completed(data). To avoid double-provisioning on the common card path (where both completed fires paid and no async event occurs) this is fine since handle_checkout_completed is already idempotent (it short-circuits duplicate active tenants at services.py:321). Optionally gate the completed path on payment_status==paid and let async_payment_succeeded be the trigger when payment is delayed, so an unpaid completed event does not prematurely provision.
evidence: apps/billing/views.py:161-172 (dispatch match arm; else branch only logs for non-credit async_payment_succeeded)
apps/billing/views.py:288-302 (subscription checkout created with mode=subscription, no payment_method_types)
apps/billing/credits.py:269-277 (credit topup handler gates on payment_status==paid and handles both events)
apps/billing/services.py:296-323 (handle_checkout_completed; no payment_status check; idempotent on active tenant)

expected_behaviour: POST /api/billing/webhook/ is csrf_exempt and require_POST. Verifies Stripe signature using DJSTRIPE_WEBHOOK_SECRET; returns 400 on invalid. Sets service-role RLS context for cross-tenant DB writes. Dispatches on event type: checkout.session.completed/async_payment_succeeded (subscription or credit top-up), charge.refunded (credit clawback), charge.dispute.created (log + manual review), customer.subscription.deleted (deprovision or hard-delete), customer.subscription.updated (logged, no-op), invoice.payment_failed (suspend tenant). Uses stripe-py to_dict() on the StripeObject to work around stripe-py 15.x dict-like removal. Returns 200 on all handled and unhandled events.

detail: Confirmed. MonthlyBudgetAdmin puts is_capped in both list_display and list_filter (apps/billing/admin.py:23-24), but the is_capped BooleanField (apps/billing/models.py:128-131) is never written anywhere in the codebase. A repo-wide grep finds is_capped only in the model definition, its migration (0003_reconcile_refactor.py:41), and the admin registration — never assigned True and never read for enforcement. The actual budget-cap mechanism is entirely value-based: cap_budget sets spent_dollars = budget_dollars (cap_budget.py:55-56), and enforcement reads the is_over_budget property (spent_dollars >= budget_dollars, models.py:146-147), used by tenants/serializers.py:167 and usage_services.py. Because is_capped keeps default=False forever, the admin column always renders False and the list_filter Yes option returns zero rows even during a genuine cap. Impact: an operator auditing capped months via the Django admin gets a permanently misleading column/filter. Real, but strictly admin-internal cosmetic — it does not affect end users and does not break the cap itself (which works via spent_dollars). Hence low severity.
suggested_fix: Make the admin reflect the real cap state instead of the dead field: drop is_capped from list_display/list_filter and surface the is_over_budget property — add a boolean-styled method (def capped(self, obj): return obj.is_over_budget; capped.boolean = True) and a custom SimpleListFilter keyed on spent_dollars >= budget_dollars. Preferred over resurrecting the field, since is_over_budget is already the fleet-wide source of truth. Alternatively, have cap_budget set is_capped alongside spent_dollars and make enforcement honor it.
evidence: apps/billing/migrations/0003_reconcile_refactor.py:41 (only other is_capped reference; grep -rn is_capped finds no setter)
expected_behaviour: UsageRecordAdmin is registered with list_display (event_type, tenant, input/output tokens, model_used, cost_estimate, created_at), list_filter by event_type, and date_hierarchy on created_at. MonthlyBudgetAdmin shows month/budget_dollars/spent_dollars/is_capped with is_capped filter. Standard Django admin CRUD — read + (superuser) write access.

detail: Confirmed asymmetry between the runtime endpoint's serializer and its validator. RuntimePreferredModelView._state() (runtime_views.py:208-221) builds allowed_models from only {**TIER_MODEL_CONFIGS[tier], **_byo_model_extras(tenant)} and never calls offer_model_entry(). But the POST validation path uses _get_allowed_models(tenant) (views.py:793-805), whose base is {**offer_model_entry(), **TIER_MODEL_CONFIGS[tier], ...}, so it WILL accept the live free-offer model id. resolve_tenant_models (config_generator.py:1047-1051) likewise leads the real allowlist with the offer model, confirming it is genuinely selectable. The mismatch: GET state and the model_not_allowed 400 error body (which embeds **self._state(tenant) and tells the agent "Available models are listed in allowed_models") both return a list that OMITS the live offer model. The internal assistant agent — the only consumer of this endpoint — cannot learn the free-offer model id from this surface, so it may report that model as unavailable or omit it when listing selectable models, even though a POST for it would succeed. Impact is bounded: the offer is the rolling default (preferred_model == ''), so tenants on the default are already running it; the harm is only in the agent's representation of which models are available, not in actual model switching. Hence low severity.
suggested_fix: In _state(), reuse the validator's source of truth: replace the locally rebuilt allowed dict with allowed = _get_allowed_models(tenant) (imported from apps.tenants.views). That dict already merges offer_model_entry() and matches both the POST validation and resolve_tenant_models, eliminating the drift class.

apps/tenants/runtime_views.py:249-260 — POST error body embeds **self._state(tenant) and tells agent models are 'listed in allowed_models'
apps/tenants/views.py:793-805 — _get_allowed_models includes offer_model_entry() in its base, so POST accepts the offer model
apps/billing/model_offers.py:80-86 — offer_model_entry() returns the live offer model fragment when active

expected_behaviour: GET/POST /api/v1/tenants/runtime/<tenant_id>/preferred-model/ with internal auth. GET returns `{preferred_model, applied_model, model_tier, allowed_models: [{model_id, alias}]}` where `allowed_models` includes tier models + BYO extras. POST body `{model_id}`: if non-empty, validates against `_get_allowed_models` (same allowlist as consumer API — tier + BYO); model not in allowlist returns 400 with full state payload so the runtime can surface the reason. On success, saves `preferred_model`, bumps pending config, enqueues immediate apply. An empty `model_id` clears to tier default. Returns `{updated: True, ...state}` on success.

feature: BYO-aware allowed models set for tier gating
detail: Confirmed inconsistency. RuntimePreferredModelView._state() (runtime_views.py:208-213) builds its allowed_models advisory list from only TIER_MODEL_CONFIGS + _byo_model_extras(tenant). The POST write gate at line 250 instead calls _get_allowed_models(tenant) (views.py:793-805), which additionally merges offer_model_entry() (the limited-time free model). offer_model_entry() (apps/billing/model_offers.py:80-86) returns the offer model entry whenever offer_is_active(), else {}. Consequence: during an active free offer, the offer model IS accepted by POST but is NOT listed in the allowed_models array returned by GET state, the 200 POST success body, or the 400 rejection body — even though the 400 detail string tells the assistant "Available models are listed in 'allowed_models'." This is an assistant-facing advisory surface (internal AllowAny endpoint hit by the in-container OpenClaw runtime), not a direct end-user surface, and only diverges during the active-offer window. Impact is bounded: the in-container assistant reading its own state could fail to surface the free model or claim it is unavailable, contradicting the write gate that actually accepts it. No security/data-integrity/write-path correctness is affected — switching TO the offer model still succeeds. The feature record's own expected_behaviour explicitly lists "the active free offer (if any)" in the allowed-models union, so _state()'s omission diverges from the documented contract.
suggested_fix: Make _state() reuse the single source of truth rather than re-deriving the allowlist. Import _get_allowed_models from apps.tenants.views and build the allowed_models list from its result, so GET state, the 200 success body, and the 400 rejection body all reflect the same set the POST gate enforces (including offer_model_entry() during an active offer, with the same merge/precedence order).
evidence: apps/tenants/runtime_views.py:235-272 (POST uses _get_allowed_models gate but _state for 400/200 bodies)
expected_behaviour: `_get_allowed_models(tenant)` in `apps/tenants/views.py` merges the active free offer (if any), the tier's `TIER_MODEL_CONFIGS` dict, and BYO extras from `_byo_model_extras(tenant)`. This combined dict is used by both `PreferredModelView.patch` (subscriber API) and `RuntimePreferredModelView.post` (container API) to gate model selection. A model not in the union dict gets a 400 rejection. This ensures BYO models are selectable only when the credential is non-ERROR.

detail: The code asymmetry is genuine: in apps/common/cache_signals.py the _profile_changed handler (lines 86-88) is decorated only with @receiver(post_save, sender=FuelProfile), whereas all 7 other fuel models (Workout, WorkoutPlan, BodyWeightLog, PersonalRecord, RestingHeartRateLog, SleepLog, FuelGoal) and the journal/document models carry both post_save and post_delete. The feature record's expected_behaviour says signals register "for every data model" and lists FuelProfile -> ["fuel"] among mutation-bumping models, so the missing post_delete is a real deviation from the documented contract. However, real-world impact is effectively nil: FuelProfile is a OneToOneField(Tenant, on_delete=CASCADE) singleton (apps/fuel/models.py:321) that no application code ever deletes — it is only ever created via get_or_create and updated (apps/fuel/runtime_views.py:651, apps/fuel/views.py:156). The only delete paths are (a) cascade when the Tenant itself is deleted during deprovisioning, where a stale "fuel" tag belongs to a now-gone tenant with no consumer, and (b) a manual Django-admin delete (registered apps/fuel/admin.py:31), after which the next read/write re-creates the profile via get_or_create and fires post_save, bumping the tag. The cached fuel views also carry DEFAULT_TTL = 60s (apps/common/cache.py:25), bounding any stale window to under a minute. So no real user or operator would observe stale fuel-profile data in practice; this is a consistency / defense-in-depth gap, not a live defect.
suggested_fix: Add @receiver(post_delete, sender=FuelProfile) above the existing _profile_changed handler at cache_signals.py line 86, so deletion also bumps the "fuel" tag, matching every other model in the file and the documented contract.
evidence: apps/common/cache_signals.py:51-84 (every other fuel model has post_save + post_delete)
apps/fuel/runtime_views.py:651 + apps/fuel/views.py:156 (created via get_or_create, never deleted)
apps/fuel/admin.py:31 (registered in admin — only realistic manual-delete path)
expected_behaviour: The `apps/common/cache_signals.py` module registers `post_save` and `post_delete` Django signal receivers for every data model that backs a cached API view. On any save or delete, `_tenant_id(instance)` resolves the tenant via `instance.tenant` or `instance.tenant_id`, then calls `bump_tags(tenant_id, tags)` which increments each affected tag version in Redis. Affected tag mappings: Workout/BodyWeightLog → `["fuel", "dashboard"]`; WorkoutPlan/PersonalRecord/RestingHeartRateLog/SleepLog/FuelGoal/FuelProfile → `["fuel"]`; JournalEntry/DailyNote → `["journal", "dashboard"]`; Document → `["journal", "sidebar"]`; Tenant → `["dashboard", "tenant"]`. Exceptions are caught and logged; signal handlers never raise into the request that triggered the save.

detail: Real but low-impact mass-assignment hygiene gap, NOT a security defect. CoreProfileSerializer (apps/core/serializers.py:8-23) lists onboarding_status in fields but omits it from read_only_fields (only id/created_at/updated_at are locked), so CoreProfileView.patch (apps/core/views.py:155-163) accepts and persists it from a JWT-authenticated request. This is a genuine spec deviation: the feature record's expected_behaviour says status fields should be read-only, the model docstring says CoreProfile is populated via assistant-led onboarding, and the runtime (internal-auth) endpoint is the intended writer (apps/core/runtime_views.py:94-109, _PROFILE_FIELDS). However the security framing is overstated. The write is entirely self-scoped (request.user.tenant only — no cross-tenant access, no privilege escalation, no data exposure), and DRF's choices validator limits values to the 4 valid enum members (pending/in_progress/completed/declined). The only behavioral consequence in core is CoreRestartView (views.py:133) skipping the user's OWN welcome-meditation scheduling when status != pending; views.py:85 merely echoes the value back. No core cron/scheduler is gated on this field (unlike apps/fuel). Worst case: a user perturbs their own onboarding/welcome state — a self-inflicted UX/consistency nuisance, no trust boundary crossed. Hence data-integrity/low, not security.
suggested_fix: Add "onboarding_status" to read_only_fields in CoreProfileSerializer (apps/core/serializers.py:23), mirroring MeditationSessionSerializer which locks its status field. The runtime endpoint writes the field via the model directly (not this serializer), so it is unaffected. Optionally add a regression test asserting a web PATCH cannot change onboarding_status.

apps/core/views.py:155-163 (CoreProfileView.patch saves serializer with partial data, no field guard)

detail: Confirmed. In the fleet fan-out branch of dedup_crons (apps/cron/views.py:1344-1350), the response hardcodes "failed": 0. When publish_batch raises (e.g. QStash outage, since the prod batch_json path re-raises on any error at publish.py:208-210), the except block sets enqueued = 0 (views.py:1346-1348) and the endpoint returns {"enqueued": 0, "failed": 0}. That payload is indistinguishable from the legitimate "no active tenants matched" case (the empty-tasks early return at publish.py:155-156 also yields enqueued=0), so an operator reads it as "nothing needed processing" rather than "every dedup task was silently dropped." The sibling broadcast endpoint immediately above (views.py:1284-1291) handles this correctly: failed = len(batch_tasks) - enqueued. The dedup endpoint is simply missing that line. Additionally, in the sync-fallback path (QStash unconfigured, dev/test), publish_batch returns a partial count < len(tasks) when some tasks throw (publish.py:176-185); the hardcoded failed:0 masks those partial failures too. Impact is operator-facing telemetry only (admin/QStash-triggered cron maintenance endpoint), not stored-data corruption — hence low severity.
suggested_fix: Mirror the sibling broadcast endpoint: replace the hardcoded line at views.py:1350 with `failed = len(batch_tasks) - enqueued` followed by `return JsonResponse({"enqueued": enqueued, "failed": failed})`. Optionally, to disambiguate total failure from an empty fleet, set failed = len(batch_tasks) inside the except block before returning.

feature: Register system QStash crons management command
detail: Confirmed. In the --dry-run path, the new-entry (would-register) branch at lines 203-205 prints "[dry-run] would register: ..." then immediately continues WITHOUT incrementing the `registered` counter. The parallel dry-run update branch (lines 178-181) correctly increments `updated` before continuing. The final summary at line 217 reports "{registered} registered", so a --dry-run preview of a deploy that includes brand-new crons (ones not yet present in QStash) always reports "0 registered, X updated, Y unchanged" even when new crons would in fact be created. Impact is limited and cosmetic: this is an operator-only management command, and each would-be-registered cron is still printed individually on its own line, so the operator still sees every new cron in the per-line output. Only the summary tally undercounts. No functional effect on actual registration (the non-dry-run path increments correctly at line 213).
suggested_fix: Add `registered += 1` inside the dry-run new-entry branch (lines 203-205) before the `continue`, mirroring the dry-run update branch at lines 178-181.
evidence: apps/cron/management/commands/register_system_crons.py:203-205 (dry-run register branch, no increment before continue)
expected_behaviour: python manage.py register_system_crons [--base-url URL] [--dry-run]. Requires QSTASH_TOKEN and base_url. Fetches existing QStash schedules. For each of 25 SYSTEM_CRONS entries: skips if cron_expr unchanged, deletes+recreates if cron_expr changed, creates if new. Dry-run prints intended changes without API calls. Outputs registered/updated/unchanged counts.

feature: Dashboard Cache Invalidation via Signal Receivers
detail: Real but lower-impact than claimed. HorizonsView.get is cached with @tenant_cache(ttl=60, tag="dashboard") and surfaces pending_extractions (apps/dashboard/views.py:192, 251-268). apps/dashboard/receivers.py registers bump-on-save receivers for AssistantInsight, UserVoicePref, Goal, and Document only — PendingExtraction and Task have NO receiver (the docstring at receivers.py:27-29 deliberately excludes Task). Two paths leave the dashboard cache tag un-bumped: (1) ExtractionDismissView writes only PendingExtraction.status=DISMISSED with no covered-model write (apps/journal/views.py:588-590); (2) the typed-task approve path Task.objects.create + pending.save (apps/router/extraction_callbacks.py:153-158), which only runs for tenants with experimental_typed_journal_lifecycle=True (default False, canary-gated). In both cases the dashboard tag version is not advanced, so a GET /horizons/ refetch returns the stale cached pending_extractions list for up to 60s. NOTE: approve-goal (typed Goal AND legacy Document) and legacy-task approve DO bump the tag via the Goal/Document receivers, so they are correctly covered. IMPACT IS TEMPERED by the frontend: PendingGoal (frontend/components/pending-goal.tsx:21-33) holds local resolved useState that hides the card on dismiss/approve success, so during the live session the user does not see a revert — the stale refetch is masked. The glitch only surfaces on a hard page reload within the 60s window (local state lost), where an already-resolved extraction transiently reappears. DB state is always correct; this is a cosmetic stale-cache flicker, not a data-integrity or functional defect — hence low, not medium.
suggested_fix: Add a post_save/post_delete receiver for PendingExtraction in apps/dashboard/receivers.py calling _bump_for(instance) (PendingExtraction has tenant_id). That single receiver covers both the dismiss and typed-task-approve gaps and any future PendingExtraction status change. Alternatively, register a Task receiver too (and update the receivers.py docstring that says Task is intentionally excluded) since HorizonsView's dual-read could surface typed Task state later. The PendingExtraction receiver is the minimal, correct fix.
evidence: apps/dashboard/receivers.py:27-29,65-88 (receivers for AssistantInsight/UserVoicePref/Goal/Document; Task explicitly excluded; no PendingExtraction)
apps/router/extraction_callbacks.py:111-116,153-158 (typed Goal create -> Goal receiver fires; typed Task create -> no receiver)
frontend/components/pending-goal.tsx:21-33 (local resolved state hides card on success, masking the stale refetch until reload)
frontend/lib/queries.ts:277-285 (useDismissExtractionMutation invalidates [horizons] -> refetch hits stale cache)
expected_behaviour: Django post_save and post_delete signals on AssistantInsight, UserVoicePref, Goal, and Document all fire _bump_for(instance), which calls apps.common.cache.bump_tag(tenant_id, "dashboard"). This invalidates the cached responses for both DashboardView (TTL 30s) and HorizonsView (TTL 60s). The receiver swallows all exceptions so a Redis failure never propagates into the model save path. bump_tag already logs and returns 0 on Redis failures; the receiver adds a second try/except with logger.exception as belt-and-suspenders. Task model saves do NOT bump the tag (HorizonsView does not surface Task state). Receivers are registered in DashboardConfig.ready() via import of apps.dashboard.receivers.

feature: Scheduled Tasks page — initial load (skeleton / error / empty states)
detail: Confirmed. In the subscriber-facing console page frontend/app/settings/cron-jobs/page.tsx, the "Your Tasks" error-state card (shown when the cron jobs query fails) renders a literal operator-internal CLI instruction: "If this persists, run: python manage.py check_gateway_health" (line 717). This is a "use client" component under app/settings/ in the Next.js subscriber dashboard, so the audience is end-user subscribers with no shell/server CLI access. The guidance is confusing and useless to them since they cannot run a Django management command. Impact is purely cosmetic/UX and only surfaces on the error branch; the adjacent text "Could not load scheduled tasks" still conveys the real problem, and no secret/sensitive data is exposed (the command name is not a credential). Hence low severity, not a security/data-integrity concern.
suggested_fix: Replace the management-command line with subscriber-appropriate guidance, e.g. "If this keeps happening, please contact support" or a retry affordance. Keep any operator-facing diagnostic hint in server-side logs rather than the rendered UI.
expected_behaviour: On mount, useCronJobsQuery fires GET /api/v1/cron-jobs/ (staleTime 60s). While loading, a SectionCardSkeleton with 5 placeholder lines is shown. If the request fails, a rose-coloured error card appears with the error message and a hint to run check_gateway_health. If the response returns zero jobs, a 'No scheduled tasks configured yet.' message is shown. If jobs exist, the 'Your Tasks' SectionCard renders the full list. The page also simultaneously fires GET /api/v1/tenants/me/ (useMeQuery) to obtain the user timezone, and GET /api/v1/integrations/ (useIntegrationsQuery) to determine connected providers.

detail: Confirmed structurally. In handleCreate (page.tsx:357-359), on a successful create the code calls setShowCreate(false) BEFORE setSuccess(setCreateFeedback, ...). The Created emerald success state for the submit button is rendered only inside the form block, which is gated by showCreate===true (the form is the else branch of the !showCreate ternary at page.tsx:545-705; the success button text is at 682-687). Because the form is collapsed first, createFeedback.status flips to success while the button is already unmounted, so the Created affirmation never renders -- effectively dead code on the create path. There is also no toast on create success: the create mutation onSuccess (queries.ts:978-980) only invalidates the cron-jobs query, and handleCreate never calls showToast -- unlike the bulk-delete/bulk-enable paths which DO fire a confirmation toast (page.tsx:503,517), and unlike the edit path (handleUpdate, 396-397) which keeps its form open and shows its saved state. User impact is limited though: query invalidation refetches the list, so the newly created task does visibly appear in the Scheduled Tasks list below. So the create is not truly silent -- the user gets implicit confirmation via the new row -- but the explicit success affirmation the code clearly intends to show is lost and the form simply vanishes, inconsistent with the edit and bulk flows. Real but low-severity UX polish issue.
suggested_fix: Fire the existing toast on create success: add showToast("Task created", "success") in handleCreate after a successful mutateAsync, matching the bulk-delete/enable pattern at page.tsx:503,517 -- this surfaces confirmation regardless of the collapsed form. Alternatively, reorder so the success state shows briefly before collapsing: keep showCreate true, call setSuccess first, and collapse the form only after SAVE_CLEAR_DELAY_MS. The toast approach is cleaner and consistent with the rest of the page.
evidence: frontend/app/settings/cron-jobs/page.tsx:545-705 (form gated by showCreate; success button at 682-687)
frontend/app/settings/cron-jobs/page.tsx:503,517 (bulk ops fire showToast on success; create does not)
frontend/app/settings/cron-jobs/page.tsx:396-397 (edit path keeps form open and shows success)
frontend/lib/queries.ts:974-982 (useCreateCronJobMutation onSuccess only invalidates query, no toast)
expected_behaviour: The Create button is disabled while createMutation.isPending and shows 'Creating…'. On submit (form onSubmit), handleCreate calls createMutation.mutateAsync with the full payload including name (required, trimmed), schedule { kind:'cron', expr, tz }, foreground, payload { kind:'agentTurn', message (trimmed) }, delivery { mode, optional channel }, enabled:true. The name field has the HTML required attribute. On success: form resets to defaultCreateForm, showCreate closes, the Create button briefly shows '✓ Created' in emerald for 2 seconds (SAVE_CLEAR_DELAY_MS), then returns to idle. On error: the button turns rose, an error message appears below it for 4 seconds (ERROR_CLEAR_DELAY_MS). The ['cron-jobs'] query is invalidated on success.

feature: TimezoneSelector — timezone picker in create/edit forms
detail: Confirmed real. TimezoneSelector pairs a free-text search box (filter state) with a native select. The useEffect at lines 121-125 fires onChange(filteredTimezones[0]) whenever the currently-selected timezone is no longer in the filtered list. Because the filter re-applies on every keystroke, partial filter strings hide the current selection and commit an arbitrary alphabetically-first match into the parent form state (createForm.tz / editForm.tz). Crucially this transient is NOT reverted when the user clears the search: clearing makes all timezones visible again, so the guard at line 122 (currentValue not in filteredTimezones) becomes false and no re-selection occurs, so the silently-changed value persists. Real impact: a subscriber who starts typing to find a timezone but abandons the search (clears the box or submits/navigates) without explicitly clicking an option can save a wrong timezone, causing the scheduled task to fire at the wrong local time. Severity is low because (a) the select always displays currentValue so an attentive user can see the change, and (b) the dominant flow (type then click the matching option) ends with an explicit selection that overrides the transient. The footgun only bites the type-then-abandon path. It is intended-but-flawed behaviour: the comment at lines 119-120 states the sync intent but does not account for partial-typing transients.
suggested_fix: Decouple what is visible while searching from what is saved: remove the auto-select useEffect (lines 121-125) and keep currentValue always selectable in the select even when filtered out (e.g. prepend currentValue as a sticky option, or render it in its own optgroup), so the value only changes on an explicit user select onChange at line 145. The existing No-timezones-match branch (line 148) already covers empty results. This preserves the displayed-equals-saved intent without ever mutating form state from filter typing.
expected_behaviour: TimezoneSelector renders as a labelled select in the create and both edit forms. The value is initialised to the user's me.timezone (from useMeQuery) or the browser Intl TZ when the form opens. The subscriber can change the timezone via the select; the value is stored in form state (createForm.tz / editForm.tz) and sent as schedule.tz in the POST/PATCH body. No separate API call.

detail: Confirmed as dead code. `checkoutError` is set only inside `handleCheckout` (lines 36-53), and `handleCheckout` is invoked only by the Subscribe button at line 136, which lives entirely within the `hasTenant && tenant` branch (lines 79-174). The no-tenant empty-state else branch (lines 175-206) renders a `checkoutError` banner at lines 200-204 but offers no control that can call `handleCheckout` — only a Link to onboarding/integrations (lines 187-192). Therefore in that branch `checkoutError` is permanently the empty string and the banner can never render. Real user impact is essentially nil: a no-tenant user has no way to start checkout from this view, so there is no error condition that would otherwise need surfacing (the QA framing that a user "encounters a backend issue and sees no error" is overstated — there is no checkout path to fail). The genuine defect is purely maintainability: unreachable JSX that misleads future maintainers into thinking both branches wire up checkout.
suggested_fix: Delete the unreachable `{checkoutError && (...)}` block (lines 200-204) from the no-tenant empty-state branch, since no checkout action exists there.
evidence: frontend/app/settings/billing/page.tsx:134-141 (Subscribe button calling handleCheckout, inside hasTenant branch)
frontend/app/settings/billing/page.tsx:175-205 (no-tenant else branch with only a Link, no checkout trigger)
expected_behaviour: When tenant.has_active_subscription is false, a dashed CTA box renders with a 'Subscribe — $12/mo' button. On click it calls POST /api/v1/billing/checkout/ via useCheckoutMutation. While pending the button label becomes 'Redirecting...' and is disabled. On success window.location.assign(result.url) navigates the browser to Stripe Checkout. On error the JSON body.detail is parsed and displayed in a rose banner below; falls back to 'Checkout failed. Please try again.' The box styling varies: accent for active trial, rose for expired trial, amber for provisioned-but-never-trialed.

detail: Confirmed. The topup value is captured once on mount from window.location.search (credits-card.tsx:18-20) and the URL is never cleaned — there is no router.replace or history.replaceState anywhere (grep for "topup" finds only reads in this one component). Stripe's credit checkout success_url is ${FRONTEND_URL}/settings/billing?topup=success (apps/billing/views.py:377). So after a top-up the user lands on that URL, sees the "Payment received — your credit balance updates within a few seconds." banner (lines 64-68), and the three re-fetch timers (immediate + 2500ms + 6000ms, lines 26-36) fire — all intended. The defect: if the user manually refreshes or revisits that exact URL later with no pending payment, the param persists, the banner re-displays, and the three credit re-fetches fire again. User impact is genuine but minor: the re-fetches are idempotent read-only GETs (no data harm), and the displayed balance is the real server value; only the reassurance banner is misleading on a stale reload. Realistically uncommon because users normally navigate away rather than refresh on this URL, and it isn't a URL one would bookmark.
suggested_fix: In the existing success useEffect (after kicking off the refetches), strip the query param with window.history.replaceState({}, "", window.location.pathname) so a subsequent refresh shows no banner and triggers no refetch. Keep the topup state value for the current render so the banner still shows on the initial post-checkout load. Apply the same to the cancelled branch for consistency.
evidence: frontend/components/billing/credits-card.tsx:18-36 (topup read once via lazy useState, no URL cleanup, 3 refetch timers)
frontend/components/billing/credits-card.tsx:64-73 (success/cancelled banners conditioned purely on topup value)
expected_behaviour: When the page mounts with ?topup=success in the URL, CreditsCard reads it via useState lazy initializer (window.location.search, not useSearchParams — avoids SSR/Suspense constraint). A green-tinted banner reads 'Payment received — your credit balance updates within a few seconds.' The credits query is invalidated immediately, then again at 2500ms and 6000ms via setTimeout to catch the webhook-delayed balance update.

detail: Confirmed. The donation-percentage mutation optimistic update (queries.ts:332-334) does a shallow merge of {...old, ...newData} where newData = { donation_percentage: pct }. It updates donation_percentage in the cached TransparencyData but leaves donation_amount stale. donation_amount is server-derived: surplus * (donation_percentage / 100) (usage_services.py:248), so a percentage change should change it. On the page, donationAmount (page.tsx:32) drives the Community Impact StatCard value (line 207), the cost-split legend amount (line 255), the split-bar widths (lines 36-39), and mealsEstimate = floor(donationAmount/0.25) feeding the ~N meals estimate (lines 42, 358). Because donationPercentage (line 34) updates optimistically but donationAmount does not, the UI is transiently inconsistent: the selected % button highlights and the StatCard hint "X% of $surplus surplus" (line 209) update instantly, while the dollar amount and meals count still show the OLD percentage until onSettled invalidate (queries.ts:343) refetches. The PATCH response (usage_views.py:104-109) returns only donation_enabled/donation_percentage, NOT donation_amount, so the refetch is genuinely required. User impact: for one network round-trip a user who picks e.g. 50% sees "50%" selected and "50% of surplus" but the impact dollars and meals still reflect the prior figure, which is momentarily misleading. No data lost; self-corrects on refetch. Minor UX-only inconsistency.
suggested_fix: In useDonationPreferenceMutation onMutate, recompute donation_amount client-side from the cached surplus before writing the optimistic cache, mirroring the server formula: derive effectiveEnabled/effectivePct from old plus newData, then set donation_amount = (effectiveEnabled && old.surplus > 0) ? round(old.surplus * effectivePct / 100, 4) : 0 and merge it alongside newData. Alternatively, drop the optimistic write for the percentage buttons and rely solely on onSettled refetch (accepting a brief lag rather than an inconsistency). The first option keeps optimism and removes the mismatch.
evidence: frontend/lib/queries.ts:325-346 (optimistic shallow-merge of only donation_percentage; onSettled invalidate)
frontend/app/settings/usage/page.tsx:205-211 (Community Impact StatCard uses donationAmount; hint uses live donationPercentage)
apps/billing/usage_views.py:104-109 (PATCH returns only donation_enabled/donation_percentage, not donation_amount)
expected_behaviour: An emerald-tinted article below the transparency breakdown has a toggle switch (role=switch, aria-checked). Current state comes from transparency.donation_enabled. Clicking the toggle calls PATCH /api/v1/billing/donation-preference/ with {donation_enabled: !current} via useDonationPreferenceMutation. The mutation applies an optimistic update to the ['usage-transparency'] cache, reverts on error, and invalidates on settle. While pending the toggle renders at 50% opacity. When donation is enabled a percentage selector appears with options [25, 50, 75, 100]; clicking one calls the same mutation with {donation_percentage: pct}. When surplus > 0 and mealsEstimate (floor(donationAmount / 0.25)) > 0, a green message shows '~N meals for children' with a Feeding America attribution link.

detail: Confirmed at frontend/app/constellation/page.tsx:328. The copy button's onClick does `navigator.clipboard?.writeText(cypher); setCopied(true); ...` — the optional chaining means the write is skipped silently if `navigator.clipboard` is undefined, and `setCopied(true)` runs unconditionally with no `.then()`/`.catch()` on the (async) writeText promise. So the button flips to 'copied ✓' even when the copy never happened or the promise rejected. This diverges from the feature's documented expected_behaviour ('On success the button text switches to copied ✓'). However real-world impact is minimal: the frontend is an Azure Static Web App served over HTTPS, and local dev runs on localhost — both are secure contexts where `navigator.clipboard` is present and writeText succeeds in the overwhelming majority of cases. The remaining failure modes (promise rejection from an unfocused document or a rare permission denial) are edge cases; the copied content is a non-sensitive read-only Cypher string, and that exact string is rendered in the adjacent <pre> block (line 330) so a user can always select/copy it manually. Net: a real but low-severity optimistic-feedback gap — the indicator can lie in rare edge cases, with a visible manual fallback.
suggested_fix: Make the success state conditional on the actual write. Guard the API and chain the promise, e.g. `onClick={() => { const c = navigator.clipboard; if (!c) return; c.writeText(cypher).then(() => { setCopied(true); setTimeout(() => setCopied(false), 1400); }).catch(() => {}); }}`. Optionally add a textarea+execCommand fallback or a brief 'copy failed' state when the API is absent or the promise rejects.
evidence: frontend/app/constellation/page.tsx:328 — copy button onClick: unconditional setCopied(true) after optional-chained writeText, no promise .catch()
frontend/app/constellation/page.tsx:330 — cypher rendered in <pre>, providing a visible manual-copy fallback

expected_behaviour: Inside the Inspector panel, a pre-formatted Cypher query is shown for the selected node. A 'copy' button calls navigator.clipboard.writeText() with the query string. On success the button text switches to 'copied ✓' for 1400ms then reverts. The Cypher query is computed client-side by cypherPathFor() based on node kind (Lesson/Cluster/Evidence/Tag).

detail: Confirmed mechanism, but the claim overstates user impact. toggleKind (page.tsx:560) resets selectedId but never clears isolated. If a user is in cluster-isolation mode (entered by clicking a Cluster node, page.tsx:516) and then toggles the Cluster kind off via the legend, three things happen: (1) the cluster hub node is removed from the canvas because visibleNodes filters on kindFilter (page.tsx:412); (2) member lessons stay scoped to that cluster because isolated is still set and the isolation filter still runs (page.tsx:413-417); (3) the 'Focused' breadcrumb stays visible because it resolves isoNode from the full graphData.nodes set, not visibleNodes (page.tsx:633). So the user sees only the cluster's lessons with no hub node -- a genuine but mild inconsistency. The claim's 'stale breadcrumb with no way to understand the state' is inaccurate: the breadcrumb is a fully functional, labeled exit control -- it still shows the cluster's name (isoNode.constellation || isoNode.label, page.tsx:637) and clicking it clears isolation (page.tsx:634). Two more exits exist: 'Reset view' clears isolated (page.tsx:627) and clicking empty canvas clears it (page.tsx:512). The user can both understand which cluster they are focused on and exit easily. The scenario is also desktop-only: the legend toggle lives in a panel that is hidden sm:block (page.tsx:715), so phone users cannot trigger it. Real but low-severity edge case.
suggested_fix: Make toggleKind isolation-aware: when toggling off the Cluster kind while a cluster is isolated, also call setIsolated(null) so isolation and its breadcrumb clear together (one added line in toggleKind, page.tsx:560). Alternatively, keep the isolated hub node visible regardless of kindFilter so the focused view stays coherent.

Clicking a kind button toggles it in/out of kindFilter state. When a kind is off, its nodes disappear from the SVG and its label text greys out. The selectedId is reset to null on any kind toggle. The legend also lists five relationship types (IN_CLUSTER, SIMILAR_TO, EVIDENCED_BY, TAGGED_WITH, REFINES) with colour swatches; toggling a rel type hides/shows those edges.

detail: Confirmed by code reading. The 220ms fade-out (animateRemoval) is purely time-based and fully decoupled from the PATCH promise: the id is added to removingIds at call time and unconditionally cleared by a window.setTimeout(..., 220) regardless of mutation outcome (page.tsx:35-40, called at :45 and :57). On the success path this is clean — onSuccess invalidates ["pending-lessons"], the refetch drops the item from items, so clearing removingIds has nothing to reappear. On a PATCH failure, the mutations (useApproveLessonMutation/useDismissLessonMutation, queries.ts:1801-1820) have only onSuccess and no onError/optimistic revert, so the item stays in sortedItems; the timer still fires at 220ms and snaps the card back from opacity-0 to opacity-100 (article className, page.tsx:94-96), while the catch block sets the error banner rendered above the list (liveError, page.tsx:85). User-visible result, only on the error path: the card fades away, then flashes back to full opacity beneath a newly-appeared error banner. It is not data loss — the card returns fully actionable and processing resets in finally (page.tsx:49-51), so the user can retry; it is purely a cosmetic flash and only occurs on the uncommon request-failure path. Matches the QA verdict (ux, low).
suggested_fix: Gate the removal animation on mutation success instead of firing it optimistically. Option 1: await approveMutation.mutateAsync(id) first, then call animateRemoval(id) only after it resolves and let the refetch finish the removal (slightly later fade, no snap-back). Option 2 (preserves snappiness): keep the optimistic fade but in the catch block immediately clear the id from removingIds and cancel the pending timeout, so the card stays visible in-place with the error rather than completing a fade-then-snap-back.
evidence: /Users/michaeljones/Projects/nbhd-united.worktrees/feature-audit/frontend/app/constellation/pending/page.tsx:35-52 (animateRemoval time-based 220ms timer; handleApprove/handleDismiss call animateRemoval before awaiting the mutation)
/Users/michaeljones/Projects/nbhd-united.worktrees/feature-audit/frontend/lib/queries.ts:1801-1820 (approve/dismiss mutations have onSuccess only, no onError revert)
expected_behaviour: Clicking 'Approve' on a card sets processing to that lesson id and starts a 220ms fade-out animation (scale-[0.99] opacity-0). useApproveLessonMutation sends PATCH /api/v1/lessons/{id}/approve/. On success, the ['pending-lessons'] and ['constellation'] query caches are invalidated (refetches both). Only one lesson can be processed at a time (both buttons disabled while processing === lesson.id showing 'Working...'). On error, an error string is set and displayed in the rose panel at the top. After success or error, processing is reset to null.

detail: Both sub-claims are confirmed in frontend/app/constellation/pending/page.tsx. (1) Stale error banner: the `error` state (line 19) is only ever set in the catch branches of approve/dismiss (lines 48, 60) and is NEVER reset to empty — there is no setError("") on success or before the next attempt. `liveError` (line 25) is `error || queryError`, so once a single approve/dismiss fails, the rose banner (line 85) stays pinned at the top of the page indefinitely, even after the user successfully approves/dismisses other cards. The user is left staring at a failure message that no longer reflects reality, implying their successful action also failed. (2) Premature fade flicker: animateRemoval(lesson.id) is invoked BEFORE `await mutateAsync` (lines 45 and 57). The mutation hooks (queries.ts lines 1801-1819) only invalidate the pending-lessons query in onSuccess, so on a failure the list is NOT refetched and the card stays in `items`. The 220ms timer fades the card out (opacity-0/scale-0.99, lines 94-96) then clears removingIds restoring opacity-100, producing a brief disappear-then-reappear flicker on a failed operation. Both are genuine but low-impact (failure-path-only, cosmetic). The stale banner is the more meaningful of the two.
suggested_fix: Call setError("") at the start of handleApprove/handleDismiss (right after setProcessing, before the mutation) so each attempt clears any prior error and the banner only reflects the most recent operation. For the flicker, move animateRemoval(lesson.id) to run only after mutateAsync resolves successfully (after the await, or in the mutation's onSuccess), so the fade-out only plays when the card will actually be removed by the refetch; on failure the card stays put with no flicker.
expected_behaviour: Clicking 'Dismiss' on a card sets processing and triggers the same 220ms fade-out animation. useDismissLessonMutation sends PATCH /api/v1/lessons/{id}/dismiss/. On success, only ['pending-lessons'] is invalidated (dismissed lessons do not enter the constellation so ['constellation'] is not invalidated). Behaviour on error and in-flight state is identical to Approve. The dismiss button is styled with rose colours (border-rose-border, bg-rose-bg, text-rose-text).

feature: Galaxy Play — Go Deeper Tutoring Session
detail: Confirmed, but minor. In openTutor() (galaxy-scene.ts:1434-1465), tutorSessionId is set to null at line 1439 and only assigned on success (line 1455). On a tutorStart failure (network/500), the catch (1459-1461) clears thinking dots and renders the copilot message "I couldn't start just now — let's try again in a moment." The finally clears tutorBusy. Critically, resetTutorControls() ran at line 1446 BEFORE the try, so Send and Skip are left ENABLED, and the error path does NOT call lockTutorComposer(). The user thus sees an enabled Send button plus copy inviting a retry, but tutorSend() (1467-1468) and tutorSkipPhase() (1479-1480) both guard on !this.tutorSessionId and silently return because the session id is still null — typing a reply and clicking Send does nothing, with no feedback. The only working escape is "End session" (line 795 -> closeTutor(true)), which also nulls openStarId, dismissing the landing panel; to actually retry, the user must close, re-select the star, and click "Go deeper" again. The defect is real: the copy promises a retry and the Send affordance looks active, but neither an in-overlay retry nor Send works in this state. Impact is limited — it only occurs on the uncommon start-failure path, there is no data loss, and a working escape exists — hence low rather than the verdict's medium.
suggested_fix: In the openTutor catch block, call this.lockTutorComposer() after the error renderTutorMessage at line 1461 (mirroring the existing 404/session-expiry handling in tutorTurn at line 1511) so the dead Send/Skip buttons are disabled and End reads "Done", removing the false affordance. Better UX: render the failure with an explicit retry action that re-invokes tutorStart(entry.id), making the "try again" copy actionable without leaving the overlay.

detail: Confirmed. The finance page holds a single shared mutation instance (archiveMutation = useArchiveFinanceAccountMutation(), page.tsx:75) whose error state is never reset. The dialog's errorMessage is derived purely from archiveMutation.isError / archiveMutation.error (page.tsx:303-309). React Query v5 (@tanstack/react-query ^5.101.0) retains isError/error after a failed mutate until the next mutate call begins or reset() is called, and there is no reset() anywhere (grep found none in page.tsx or queries.ts). The cancel handler only does setConfirmArchive(null) (page.tsx:295), which unmounts the dialog but leaves mutation state untouched. So after: open dialog for account A, click Archive, request fails (isError=true, dialog stays open showing the rose-400 error at page.tsx:437-438), Cancel, then open dialog for account B (or even re-open A) — the freshly mounted ConfirmArchiveDialog immediately renders the stale error message from A's failed attempt before the user interacts. User impact: a confusing phantom error line on a just-opened dialog. It is non-blocking (clicking Archive re-triggers mutate and clears the error) and only surfaces after a prior archive failure, so impact is cosmetic/low.
suggested_fix: Reset the mutation when the dialog opens or closes so error state cannot leak across openings. Simplest: call archiveMutation.reset() in onCancel (onCancel={() => { archiveMutation.reset(); setConfirmArchive(null); }}) and before opening a new dialog (wrap setConfirmArchive in a handler that calls archiveMutation.reset() first). Alternatively gate the error on the current account by only showing errorMessage when archiveMutation.variables === confirmArchive.id, but reset() is cleaner and also fixes the same-account re-open case.
evidence: frontend/app/finance/page.tsx:73-76 (single confirmArchive state + shared archiveMutation instance)
frontend/app/finance/page.tsx:292-311 (errorMessage derived from archiveMutation.isError; onCancel only clears confirmArchive, no reset; onSuccess clears confirmArchive)
frontend/lib/queries.ts:1146-1155 (useArchiveFinanceAccountMutation has no reset/onMutate clearing of error)
frontend/package.json:14 (@tanstack/react-query ^5.101.0 — error persists until next mutate/reset)
expected_behaviour: Clicking the archive icon button on any `AccountCard` (either debt or savings) sets `confirmArchive` state to that `FinanceAccount`. This renders the `ConfirmArchiveDialog` as a fixed full-screen overlay (`z-50`, `role='dialog'`, `aria-modal='true'`). The dialog shows the account nickname in the heading, an explanation that it will be removed from Gravity totals but kept for records. Pressing Escape or clicking the backdrop dismisses the dialog (sets `confirmArchive` to null). Clicking 'Archive' fires `archiveMutation.mutate(account.id)` which calls `DELETE /api/v1/finance/accounts/{id}/`. While pending, the button shows 'Archiving…' and is disabled. On success, the dialog closes and both `finance-dashboard` and `finance-archived-accounts` query caches are invalidated. On error, a rose-coloured error message appears below the description text inside the dialog. No optimistic update.

detail: Confirmed: the two seed-weight defaults are transposed. '+ EXERCISE' (line 965) seeds a new exercise's first set with weight = unit==="lbs" ? 60 : 27, where 60 lbs is about 27 kg, a consistent ~27 kg physical default in either unit. '+ SET' (line 1077) falls back to unit==="lbs" ? 27 : 60 when the previous set has no weight (ex.sets.at(-1)?.weight is undefined/0, e.g. after a bodyweight or hold-time set). Those fallback values are the inverse pairing and are not physically equivalent: 27 lbs is about 12 kg, while 60 kg is about 132 lbs. User impact: when the fallback fires, a lbs user gets a too-light 27 lbs seed (half the new-exercise default) and a kg user gets a too-heavy 60 kg seed (more than double), inconsistent with the '+ EXERCISE' default and with each other. It is minor because the fallback only fires when the last set had no weight (the common path copies the previous set's weight via ex.sets.at(-1)?.weight), and the seeded value is a fully editable pre-fill the user overwrites anyway. Purely a cosmetic/ergonomic annoyance, no data corruption.
suggested_fix: Change the '+ SET' fallback on line 1077 to unit==="lbs" ? 60 : 27 so it matches the '+ EXERCISE' default and seeds the same ~27 kg physical weight in both units. Optionally extract a shared defaultSeedWeight(unit) helper to prevent future drift between the two paths.
evidence: frontend/components/fuel/workout-detail.tsx:1054-1068 (weight only seeded/shown for weighted_reps; BW/hold sets store no weight, so previous-set weight is falsy and the fallback fires)
expected_behaviour: In edit mode, shows an 'EXERCISES' section with '+ EXERCISE' button. Each exercise has a name input and a list of sets. Each set has a MetricToggle (WEIGHT / BODY / HOLD) to switch between weighted_reps, bodyweight_reps, and hold_time. Weighted: reps + weight (in user's unit, stored as kg). Bodyweight: reps only. Hold: hold_s (seconds). A trash button deletes individual sets or the whole exercise. '+ SET' adds a set defaulting to the last set's weight. Estimated 1RM is computed client-side (Epley formula) for weighted sets and shown as 'est 1RM X unit' if >0. Stored in detail_json.exercises. No server call per keystroke — all edits are staged in local draft state until 'Save changes' is clicked.

detail: Confirmed inconsistency for the 'expired' integration status. The backend sets status='expired' in four places (tasks.py:58 missing refresh token, tasks.py:79 refresh HTTP 400/401, services.py:527 missing refresh token, services.py:545 refresh HTTP 400/401). The frontend's isRevoked flag (page.tsx:587) only matches 'revoked'/'error', so an 'expired' record is isActive=false, isRevoked=false, isConnected=true. Result: the StatusPill correctly shows 'expired' in amber (status-pill.tsx:23), but the description falls through to the provider's generic blurb (for Google: 'Gmail, Calendar, Drive & Tasks', NOT the cited literal 'Not connected yet.' — that literal only renders when a provider has no description) and the action button reads 'Connect' instead of 'Reconnect' (page.tsx:629-631). So the badge says 'this lapsed' while the description and button frame it as a brand-new connection — a genuine user-visible inconsistency. Impact is real but minor: clicking 'Connect' triggers the same OAuth flow 'Reconnect' would (handleConnect), so the user can still recover; only the framing is misleading. Cosmetic/copy-level, not a logic or data defect — medium overstates it; low is accurate.
suggested_fix: Include 'expired' in the reconnect branch: change page.tsx line 587 to treat status in ('revoked','error','expired') as needing reconnection (e.g. rename to needsReconnect). This makes the description show 'Reconnection required' and the button show 'Reconnect', matching the amber 'expired' badge. No backend change needed.
expected_behaviour: The Google integration card shows StatusPill depending on data from useIntegrationsQuery (GET /api/v1/integrations/): 'active' if status=active, 'error' if status=revoked/error, 'pending' if not connected. When not active a 'Connect' button appears (or 'Reconnect' if revoked/error). Clicking calls getOAuthAuthorizeUrl('google') → GET /api/v1/integrations/authorize/google/, which returns {url}. The page then does window.location.assign(url) to begin the OAuth redirect. While the redirect is pending the button shows 'Redirecting...' and all connect buttons are disabled. On error a rose-tinted message appears below the cards. If already connected but not active, a 'Disconnect' button is also shown (see disconnect feature).

detail: Confirmed: the integrations settings page treats status="expired" inconsistently for any provider, including Reddit. The backend genuinely produces this status — refresh_expiring_integrations_task sets Integration.Status.EXPIRED when no refresh_token is found (tasks.py:58) and on token-refresh HTTP 400/401 (tasks.py:79), and "expired" is a declared model choice (models.py:24). On the frontend (page.tsx:587), isRevoked only matches "revoked" or "error" — it does NOT include "expired". So when a Reddit (or Google/Sautai) integration is "expired": isActive=false and isRevoked=false, so the description falls through to provider.description ("Browse your feeds and subreddits without the doom-scroll.") as if never connected (lines 591-595), and the CTA button reads "Connect" instead of "Reconnect" (lines 629-631). Meanwhile badgeStatus falls into the isConnected branch and renders the raw status "expired" (lines 602-603), which StatusPill displays as an amber "expired" pill (status-pill.tsx:23). Net user impact: the status badge says "expired" while the body copy and button imply the integration was never set up — a mild but genuine mismatch that mis-signals connection state and the required action. Impact is limited because the badge still surfaces "expired", so the user is not left fully unaware something is wrong; the inconsistency is confined to the description text and button label. Same root-cause class as FA-0362.
suggested_fix: Include "expired" in the reconnect-needed predicate at page.tsx:587, e.g. const isRevoked = ["revoked", "error", "expired"].includes(integration?.status ?? ""); (ideally renamed to needsReconnect). This one change makes the description show "Reconnection required", the button show "Reconnect", and keeps it consistent with the badge. Apply the same fix for FA-0362.
expected_behaviour: Identical flow to Google connect but for the reddit provider. Card shows 'Browse your feeds and subreddits without the doom-scroll.' as description when not connected. Clicking calls GET /api/v1/integrations/authorize/reddit/ → window.location.assign(url). Status badge and description update based on integration record from the integrations list.

detail: Confirmed but narrow. useDisconnectIntegrationMutation() is instantiated once (page.tsx:511) and reused inside providers.map(), so its disconnect.isPending is a single global boolean. When the user clicks Disconnect on one connected OAuth provider, every OTHER connected provider's Disconnect button is simultaneously disabled and relabelled 'Disconnecting...' (lines 634-643). The provider you actually clicked does NOT show this state: the mutation's onMutate optimistically removes that integration from the ['integrations'] cache (queries.ts:448-450), and because the Disconnect button is gated on isConnected = Boolean(integration) (page.tsx:588,634), the clicked card instantly re-renders to its 'Connect' state. So the only visible artifact is that the unrelated, still-connected card briefly flickers to 'Disconnecting...' and is disabled for the duration of the network round-trip. There are only two OAuth providers (google, reddit at page.tsx:30-41), so this requires both connected at once and is a brief transient flash on a fast API call. Real but cosmetic and low-impact; the spurious disable also harmlessly prevents concurrent disconnects.
suggested_fix: Make the pending state per-card. Smallest change: gate the disabled prop and label on disconnect.isPending && disconnect.variables === integration!.id (react-query exposes mutation.variables, which holds the integrationId passed to mutate()). Alternatively, extract each provider card into its own component that owns its own useDisconnectIntegrationMutation() instance.
expected_behaviour: A 'Disconnect' button is shown for any provider that has an integration record (isConnected=true), regardless of active/revoked state. Clicking calls disconnectIntegration(integration.id) → POST /api/v1/integrations/<id>/disconnect/. An optimistic update immediately removes the integration from the cache (the card reverts to 'Not connected'). On error the previous state is restored. On settled, the integrations cache is invalidated. The button shows 'Disconnecting...' and is disabled while the mutation is pending.

feature: Journal page initial render and hash-based routing
detail: Confirmed. handleNavigate (page.tsx:55-61) unconditionally calls setViewKey((k) => k + 1), and the content wrapper keys DocumentView on activeKind-activeSlug-viewKey (page.tsx:123). Because viewKey is part of the key, navigating to the already-active kind+slug still force-remounts DocumentView, resetting its local editing/draft state, which closes the editor and discards any unsaved draft. Crucially, DocumentView has NO desktop autosave: the autosave effect is gated by if(!(editing && isMobile === true)) return (document-view.tsx:232-233), so desktop drafts persist only via explicit Save/Cmd+S. The markdown editor's unmount flush (markdown-editor.tsx:272-279) only pushes pending keystrokes into the parent draft state via onChange; it never triggers a save mutation, so on a viewKey remount the flushed draft is thrown away with the rest of the state. Reachable triggers: a desktop subscriber editing today's Daily who clicks the (active-highlighted) Daily primary-nav button, or the New Entry button, both of which fire onNavigate('daily', todaySlug()) to the same destination (sidebar.tsx:252 and 272). The in-progress draft is silently lost with no confirmation. Severity is low: it requires clicking the already-active destination mid-edit, the lost text is re-typable, and the specific mobile case the claim cites is actually NOT reachable (the full-screen editing portal at z-[9999] covers the sidebar, and the mobile path autosaves anyway), so real exposure is desktop-only. The mechanism and the data-loss outcome are nonetheless verified.
suggested_fix: In handleNavigate, make a same-destination navigate a no-op for the remount: when kind === activeKind && slug === activeSlug, skip the setViewKey increment (and the redundant hash rewrite). Early-return at the top of handleNavigate, or wrap only the setViewKey call in that guard. This keeps the intentional remount-on-navigate for genuine document changes while preventing the self-remount that discards an in-progress draft. Optionally also add desktop autosave or a dirty-state confirm for defense-in-depth, but the guard alone closes the cited path.

frontend/components/journal/document-view.tsx:185-189 (effect resets editing/draft on kind/slug change; remount resets all local state)
frontend/components/journal/markdown-editor.tsx:272-279 (unmount flush calls onChange only, never a save mutation)
