If no hash is present, activeKind defaults to 'daily' and activeSlug defaults to todayISO() (YYYY-MM-DD computed client-side). Navigating between documents updates the hash so the URL is bookmarkable and the browser back/forward button reflects the active document. No loading state is shown by the outer page shell; the DocumentView handles per-document loading. The sidebar and document area are rendered side-by-side on desktop (lg breakpoint); only the document area is visible on mobile until the sidebar is opened.

detail: Verified: the expanded-sidebar collapse button (sidebar.tsx:238) renders with min-h-[36px] min-w-[36px], 8px under both the 44x44px target stated in FA-0379's expected_behaviour and the project's WCAG 2.1 AA / 44x44 touch-target standard (CLAUDE.md Frontend Conventions). This is a genuine spec deviation. However, the 'hard to hit on touch devices' framing overstates impact: the collapse button only renders when onToggle is passed, and the ONLY render site that passes onToggle is the desktop sidebar (page.tsx:66-75), wrapped in 'hidden lg:block' (visible only at >=1024px). The mobile sidebar overlay (page.tsx:110-115) does NOT pass onToggle, so this undersized button never appears on the mobile/touch-first path; that overlay has its own correctly-sized 44px close button (page.tsx:101). The sibling expand button (sidebar.tsx:202) and the collapsed-column expand button are both correctly 44px. So the affordance is desktop-gated, non-destructive (only toggles writing space, fully reversible via a 44px control), and desktop pointer precision is high. Real but low-impact: a legitimate inconsistency with the surrounding 44px buttons and the stated spec, not a meaningful tap-failure risk on the primary touch path.
suggested_fix: Change min-h-[36px] min-w-[36px] to min-h-[44px] min-w-[44px] on the collapse button at frontend/components/journal/sidebar.tsx:238 to match the expand button (line 202), the mobile close button (page.tsx:101), and the project's 44x44 touch-target standard. Keep p-1.5 (or adjust padding) so the icon stays visually balanced; the min-h/min-w only enforces the hit area.

frontend/app/journal/page.tsx:110-115 (mobile overlay Sidebar render, no onToggle -> collapse button never renders here)

When collapsed, the sidebar renders a minimal 48px-wide column with only an expand chevron button (aria-label='Expand sidebar'). When expanded, it renders the full 240px nav including header, new-entry button, primary nav, and scrollable sections. The toggle button has 44x44px minimum touch target.

feature: Document header — date navigation for daily notes
detail: Confirmed. The Previous-day chevron (line 99) and Next-day chevron (line 140) use min-h-[40px] min-w-[40px], and the Today button (line 153) uses min-h-[40px] — all 4px under the 44x44px touch-target minimum that CLAUDE.md/DESIGN.md mandate for WCAG 2.1 AA. These buttons sit in the always-rendered daily-note header (kind === "daily") and ARE touch targets on mobile. The codebase's own dominant pattern is 44px (140 min-h-[44px] occurrences vs 13 min-h-[40px] across frontend/components+app); the sibling sidebar-toggle button two elements up (line 82) correctly uses min-h-[44px] min-w-[44px], so the 40px values are an inconsistency rather than an intentional choice. User impact is real but marginal: a 40px square is slightly harder to tap precisely on a phone, especially given the small h-4 w-4 SVG chevrons. Note the feature's expected_behaviour does not itself state a 44px requirement — the standard comes from the project-wide accessibility convention — but that convention applies and is violated.
suggested_fix: Change min-h-[40px] min-w-[40px] to min-h-[44px] min-w-[44px] on the two chevron buttons (lines 99, 140) and min-h-[40px] to min-h-[44px] on the Today button (line 153). The buttons already use flex items-center justify-center so enlarging won't disrupt icon centering.

The next chevron is disabled (opacity-30, cursor-not-allowed) when slug >= todayISO() to prevent navigating to future dates. detail:
Confirmed real, but milder than the QA claim of a hard bounce loop. login/page.tsx:35 computes isOnboardingNeeded = !me.tenant || me.tenant.status !== "active" || !me.tenant.user.telegram_chat_id. The third clause only checks the Telegram channel, but LINE is a first-class onboarding channel (types.ts:206-208 expose line_user_id and preferred_channel "telegram"|"line"; onboarding/page.tsx:23 treats isLineLinked as valid linkage). A LINE-only subscriber has telegram_chat_id = null, so isOnboardingNeeded resolves to true and login pushes them to /onboarding instead of /journal on every login.

Correction to the QA description: the user is NOT permanently stuck. On landing at /onboarding the page recognizes LINE (onboarding/page.tsx:22-24, messagingLinked = isTelegramLinked || isLineLinked), so an active LINE-only user resolves to the "launch" scene which shows "Your universe is ready" with a manual "Start exploring" button routing to /journal (launch-sequence.tsx:23,66-70). Real impact: a redundant STEP-3-OF-3 launch screen plus a manual click on every login, never a lockout. It is also inconsistent with the root landing page (app/page.tsx:90-92), which redirects any logged-in user straight to /journal with no channel check. Net effect is recurring extra friction for LINE-only subscribers only. Hence ux (not logic) and low severity.
suggested_fix: Make the post-login onboarding-completion check channel-agnostic at login/page.tsx:35, mirroring onboarding/page.tsx. Replace the telegram-only clause with one that passes for any linked messaging channel, e.g. const messagingLinked = Boolean(me.tenant?.user.telegram_chat_id) || Boolean(me.line_user_id); const isOnboardingNeeded = !me.tenant || me.tenant.status !== "active" || !messagingLinked; (line_user_id lives on AuthUser per types.ts:206). Confirm /me returns line_user_id populated for LINE-linked users; if it is only on the LINE status endpoint, gate on tenant.status === "active" alone or fetch the LINE status as onboarding/page.tsx does.
expected_behaviour: The OnboardingShell wraps a glass-card form with email (type=email, required) and password (type=password, required) fields. On submit the button label changes to 'Signing in...' and is disabled. POST /api/v1/auth/login/ is called with {email, password}. On success, access and refresh tokens are stored in localStorage via setTokens(). If hasPendingAppAuthorize() is true, router.replace('/app/authorize') is called instead of the normal post-login flow. Otherwise fetchMe() is called (GET /api/v1/auth/me/) to check tenant status: if tenant is absent, status !== 'active', or telegram_chat_id is falsy, route to /onboarding; else route to /journal. On API error, a rose-coloured error box renders the error message plus a 'Reset your password' link to /forgot-password. On 401 with a prior session, tokens are cleared and the user is redirected to /login by the api client.

detail:
CONFIRMED, but lower impact than the QA claim implies. Post-signup routing (signup/page.tsx:47) and login routing (login/page.tsx:35) both compute isOnboardingNeeded using only the Telegram chat id (me.tenant.user.telegram_chat_id) and ignore LINE entirely. The TenantUser type (types.ts:11-19) carries telegram_chat_id but has NO line_user_id field, so this routing path literally cannot see LINE linkage. A LINE-only subscriber (telegram_chat_id null, line_user_id set, tenant active) is therefore routed to /onboarding on every login/signup instead of straight to /journal, even though fully provisioned and connected. That part of the claim is accurate, and it is identical between signup and login.

What the claim overstates: the user is NOT dead-ended. The onboarding page (onboarding/page.tsx:22-24) detects LINE via the async useLineStatusQuery; once lineStatus.linked resolves, messagingLinked is true, the page advances to the launch scene, and an active+provisioned tenant sees Your universe is ready with a Start exploring button to /journal (launch-sequence.tsx:23, 66-70). So the impact is a forced extra Step-3 Launching your universe screen plus one extra click on every sign-in, not a lockout. There is also a brief flash where lineStatus is still undefined, during which an already-LINE-connected user momentarily sees the Connect your messenger scene before the query resolves.

Net: a LINE subscriber never gets the clean direct-to-journal experience a Telegram subscriber gets; every login/signup detours them through onboarding/launch. Real and repeatable, but a UX friction/regression rather than a logic-breaking lockout, hence low (not medium) severity and category ux.
suggested_fix: Make the post-auth routing channel-agnostic. The top-level me (AuthUser, types.ts:204-208) already exposes line_user_id and preferred_channel. In both signup/page.tsx:47 and login/page.tsx:35, replace the telegram-only term with a combined check, e.g. const linked = me.tenant.user.telegram_chat_id || me.line_user_id; and use !linked in isOnboardingNeeded. (Either add line_user_id to the TenantUser type and have the backend serialize it on tenant.user, or read me.line_user_id from the top-level AuthUser which is already populated.) Apply identically to both files so login and signup stay consistent.
evidence: frontend/app/onboarding/page.tsx:22-24,38-44 (onboarding detects LINE and advances to launch)
expected_behaviour: Form has four fields: display name (optional text), email (required), password (required, client-validated ≥8 chars), confirm password (required). A PasswordStrengthMeter renders live feedback below the password field once any character is typed (scores: Weak/Fair/Good/Strong based on length ≥8, uppercase, digit, special-char). On submit, if passwords do not match the error 'Passwords do not match.' shows; if password < 8 chars the error 'Password must be at least 8 characters.' shows — both without API call. Otherwise POST /api/v1/auth/signup/ is called with {email, password, display_name}. On success tokens are stored and the same hasPendingAppAuthorize() branch logic as login applies. Legal links to /legal/terms and /legal/privacy appear below the submit button.

detail: Confirmed. The spec says: when prefers-reduced-motion is true, "stars are drawn once without animation." The code does not honor this. At line 79, draw() unconditionally calls rafRef.current = requestAnimationFrame(draw) at the end of every invocation. The branch at lines 82-86 (if (prefersReduced) draw(); else rafRef.current = requestAnimationFrame(draw);) was clearly intended to make draw() run a single time for reduced-motion users, but because draw() always re-schedules itself, the initial direct call kicks off the same infinite RAF loop. So reduced-motion users still get a continuous clear+repaint of all stars every frame. User impact is limited but real: the flicker sine-wave IS correctly suppressed (lines 69-72, flicker = star.opacity when prefersReduced), so the rendered image is visually static and there is no perceptible motion, which means the WCAG 2.3 vestibular-trigger concern is largely moot in practice. The genuine harm is wasted work: an idle full-canvas repaint loop runs forever on a static image, consuming CPU/GPU and battery for exactly the reduced-motion cohort the branch meant to spare, and it violates the documented draw-once contract. Real but low-severity logic defect, not a misread.
suggested_fix: Make the re-schedule conditional so draw() truly runs once for reduced-motion users. Inside draw(), replace the unconditional line 79 with: if (!prefersReduced) rafRef.current = requestAnimationFrame(draw);. The initial static paint still happens via the draw() call at line 82-83, but no further frames are scheduled. cleanup at line 95 already handles cancelAnimationFrame safely.

detail: Confirmed: the prefetchTabData switch in frontend/app/settings/layout.tsx (lines 125-155) has cases for all 7 other settings tabs but none for /settings/ai-provider, so hovering that sidebar tab (or touching it on mobile) falls through to default: break and prefetches nothing. The AI Provider page (frontend/app/settings/ai-provider/page.tsx:53-55) does have real data dependencies — useTenantQuery() (queryKey ["tenant"]) and useByoCredentialsQuery() (queryKey ["byo-credentials"]) — so there genuinely is data that could be warmed on hover. The expected_behaviour explicitly lists AI Provider among the 8 tabs and says navigation should feel instant via prefetch-on-mouseenter, making AI Provider the one tab omitted from the pattern. User impact is low: navigating to AI Provider still works correctly (its own useQuery hooks fetch on mount); the user just doesn't get the hover head-start the other 7 tabs get. Impact is further softened because the ["tenant"] query has a 5-minute staleTime and is typically already populated by the dashboard/Billing tab, so usually only ["byo-credentials"] would be cold — at most one short round-trip is not pre-warmed. A genuine omission in a perf/polish optimization, not a functional bug.
suggested_fix: Add a `case "/settings/ai-provider":` to the switch that prefetches both ["tenant"] (fetchTenant) and ["byo-credentials"] (fetchByoCredentials), importing fetchByoCredentials alongside the other fetch functions at the top of layout.tsx.

feature: Scheduled Tasks — enable/disable a cron job
detail: Confirmed at the code level. A single shared toggleMutation instance (page.tsx:214, from useToggleCronJobMutation) is reused by every row rendered in cronJobs.map(...) (page.tsx:820). The Enable/Disable button's disabled={toggleMutation.isPending} (page.tsx:884) reads the mutation's GLOBAL pending flag with no per-row scoping (no check against toggleMutation.variables to limit disabling to the in-flight job). So while ANY one job's toggle request is in flight, every other row's Enable/Disable button is disabled simultaneously. Notably the delete path directly beside it uses per-job state maps (singleDeletePending[jobIdentifier], page.tsx:824/905), confirming per-row guarding was a known pattern that simply was not applied to the toggle button — an inconsistency, not intended behaviour. User impact is real but minor: the optimistic update (queries.ts:1017-1026) flips the toggled row's label immediately and onSettled invalidates promptly (queries.ts:1032-1033), and a toggle is a single fast API call, so the all-buttons-disabled window is typically sub-second. Only a user rapidly toggling several jobs in succession would notice the other rows briefly frozen; in normal one-at-a-time use the list does not appear broken. Hence low severity, not a medium/high frozen/broken framing.
suggested_fix: Scope the disabled state to the in-flight row instead of the global flag. React Query exposes toggleMutation.variables while pending, so change line 884 to disabled={toggleMutation.isPending && toggleMutation.variables?.name === jobIdentifier}. This mirrors the per-job pattern already used for deletes (singleDeletePending[jobIdentifier]) and limits the disable to the row actually being updated.

detail: Confirmed. The active-tab dot at app-shell.tsx:247 is position:absolute (absolute bottom-1.5) with no left/right/inset, and no ancestor in the mobile tab bar is positioned: the parent Link (line 221: flex flex-1 flex-col items-center justify-center ... min-h-[44px]), the flex row div (line 212: flex items-center justify-around h-14), and the nav (line 207: z-[50] lg:hidden ... -- z-index without position is inert). Verified .mobile-tab-shadow (globals.css:474) only sets box-shadow and the only other nav rule (globals.css:553) is a print-media display:none. So the dot's containing block falls through to the root shell column at app-shell.tsx:336 (relative flex h-[100dvh] flex-col). Horizontally the dot keeps its static position (still under its tab), but bottom:6px resolves against the full 100dvh viewport, not the tab. Since MobileTabBar is the last flow child, the bar sits at viewport bottom with pb-[env(safe-area-inset-bottom)], so on notched iPhones (the primary target) the dot floats down into/below the safe-area inset at the physical screen bottom, detached from the active label rather than sitting just beneath it. Impact is purely cosmetic: a misplaced decorative glow dot on devices with a non-zero bottom inset. No functional/accessibility impact (aria-current conveys active state). The QA claim is correct in mechanism; the visible symptom is vertical detachment toward the screen bottom (horizontal static position is preserved).
suggested_fix: Add relative to the Link className at app-shell.tsx:221 so the dot's absolute bottom-1.5 anchors to the tab. Optionally add left-1/2 -translate-x-1/2 for deterministic horizontal centering.

detail: Verified. On mobile (below the sm breakpoint) AccountCard stacks into a column (line 58: flex flex-col sm:flex-row); the balance/monthly-payment row (line 70) carries pl-13 sm:pl-0 to indent it ~52px so the figures align under the account nickname, which begins after the 40px icon + 12px gap. But pl-13 is not a real Tailwind class: Tailwind v3.4.19 (installed) default spacing jumps 12 (3rem) to 14 (3.5rem) with no 13 step (confirmed via tailwindcss/defaultTheme), and tailwind.config.ts extend.spacing (lines 12-19) only adds fluid-* and page keys, never 13. The class is silently dropped by the JIT compiler, so the row gets 0 left padding on mobile and the Monthly/Balance figures sit at the card content's left edge instead of indented under the nickname -- a small left-misalignment on narrow screens, on every AccountCard. One nuance vs the report wording: the figures are NOT flush against the card's outer edge (the parent glass-card has p-6 sm:p-8 padding) and text-right still right-aligns text within each value box; the real symptom is the lost ~52px indent / misalignment, not edge-clipping. Cosmetic, mobile-only -- hence low severity.
suggested_fix: Either add a "13": "3.25rem" entry to extend.spacing in frontend/tailwind.config.ts, or replace pl-13 on account-card.tsx:70 with a valid class: pl-[3.25rem] (arbitrary value) to keep the exact intended indent, or pl-14/pl-12 if a default-scale step is acceptable.

feature: Runtime Payoff Plan Calculation & Save (OpenClaw Plugin)
detail: Confirmed in apps/finance/runtime_views.py (RuntimeFinancePayoffView.post). When strategy is a non-empty but unrecognised string (e.g. "waterfall"), the guard at line 410 `if strategy and strategy in ("snowball","avalanche","hybrid")` is False, so execution falls to the else-branch (lines 413-415) which populates `results` only with keys snowball/avalanche/hybrid from compare_strategies. The save guard at line 419 is `if save and strategy`, which is truthy for any non-empty string, so when save=true the code reaches line 420 `result_data = results[strategy]` -> `results["waterfall"]` -> KeyError. There is no custom DRF exception handler defined in config or apps, so a raw KeyError is not caught by DRF (which only converts APIException/Http404/PermissionDenied) and surfaces as HTTP 500. There is no upstream validation of the strategy value (`strategy = body.get("strategy")`, line 383). Real impact: the OpenClaw AI agent calling this internal runtime endpoint with a mistyped strategy plus save=true gets an internal server error instead of a clean 400. Impact is bounded -- internal agent-only endpoint (not public), the trigger requires both a mistyped strategy AND save=true, and a correct strategy or save=false works fine; hence low severity rather than medium.
suggested_fix: Validate strategy against the allowed set up front and return 400 on invalid values, e.g. after line 383: if strategy is not None and strategy not in ("snowball","avalanche","hybrid"): return Response({"error": f"Unknown strategy: {strategy}"}, status=400). Alternatively tighten the save guard to `if save and strategy in results:` so an invalid key cannot trigger the KeyError.
evidence: apps/finance/runtime_views.py:383 (strategy = body.get("strategy"), no validation of value)
apps/finance/runtime_views.py:410-415 (valid-strategy guard vs compare_strategies else-branch keyed snowball/avalanche/hybrid)
apps/finance/runtime_views.py:418-420 (save guard `if save and strategy` then `results[strategy]` -> KeyError on invalid key)
config/ and apps/ grep: no DRF EXCEPTION_HANDLER override, so KeyError -> HTTP 500
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/finance/runtime/<tenant_id>/payoff/calculate/ — internal auth required. Requires monthly_budget (Decimal). Optional strategy ('snowball', 'avalanche', or 'hybrid'); if absent or invalid, all three strategies are computed and returned. Loads all active non-savings accounts with balance > 0 as DebtInput objects. Calls services.calculate_payoff (single strategy) or services.compare_strategies (all three). If save=true and strategy is specified, deactivates all existing plans (bulk update is_active=False) and creates a new PayoffPlan row. Returns {results: {strategy: {total_debt, total_interest, payoff_months, payoff_date, schedule}}}. Returns empty message if no active debts exist; 400 if monthly_budget invalid.

detail: Confirmed real, low-severity. WorkoutDuplicateView (apps/fuel/views.py:730-732) sets the new workout's date with date_cls.today() — a plain datetime.date.today() that reads the container's system clock (UTC in Azure). The codebase already activates the authenticated user's IANA timezone per-request via UserTimezoneMiddleware (apps/tenants/middleware.py:100-111, dj_timezone.activate), so the timezone-aware date is available but unused here. For a tenant ahead of UTC (e.g. the Asia/Tokyo canary, UTC+9), duplicating a workout between local midnight and 09:00 yields a UTC date one day earlier, so the new 'planned' session lands on the previous calendar day in the schedule. Impact is mild and recoverable: the duplicate is created as status='planned' and the full payload is returned immediately, so a misplaced workout is visible and can be rescheduled; the trigger window is a few early-morning hours and only affects east-of-UTC tenants. The audit's expected_behaviour says 'server UTC date', but its own inventory_notes flags this as a 'potential off-by-one', so the spec documents current behavior rather than endorsing it — this is a genuine UX off-by-one, not intended design.
suggested_fix: Replace date=date_cls.today() at apps/fuel/views.py:732 with date=timezone.localdate() (from django.utils import timezone), which honors the per-request activated user timezone set by UserTimezoneMiddleware. The same pattern applies to the other plain date_cls.today() calls in this file (lines 865, 1229) if a broader tenant-local-date sweep is wanted.
evidence: apps/fuel/views.py:6 (from datetime import date as date_cls)
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/fuel/workouts/{workout_id}/duplicate/ requires JWT auth. Looks up the source workout by id+tenant (404 if not found). Creates a new Workout with status='planned', date=today (server UTC date), and copies category, activity, duration_minutes, detail_json. Clears plan, slot, notes, rpe, source. Returns 201 with full WorkoutSerializer payload.

detail: Confirmed. RestingHRDetailView.patch (views.py:1023-1034) calls serializer.is_valid() then serializer.save() with no date-conflict guard. The model declares unique_together = ["tenant","date"] (models.py:472), and the serializer fields are ["id","date","bpm","created_at"] (serializers.py:224-228) — 'tenant' is NOT a serializer field, so DRF does not auto-generate a UniqueTogetherValidator for (tenant, date). A PATCH that moves an entry onto a date already occupied by another entry for the same tenant therefore hits the DB unique constraint and raises an unhandled IntegrityError, surfacing as HTTP 500. The sibling BodyWeightDetailView.patch (views.py:613-634) handles the identical situation with an explicit pre-save check returning 409 date_conflict (lines 626-632), proving the RHR omission is an inconsistency, not intended behaviour. The URL is routed and reachable (urls.py:109). User impact: a 500 Internal Server Error instead of a clean 409 when a user edits a resting-HR entry's date onto a date that already has an entry for that tenant. Low severity: the collision is an uncommon edit case, the unique constraint still correctly blocks the duplicate write (no data corruption), and only the error presentation is wrong. The parallel Sleep detail view likely shares the same gap.
suggested_fix: Mirror the BodyWeight pattern: before serializer.save(), read new_date = serializer.validated_data.get("date"); if new_date and new_date != entry.date and RestingHeartRateLog.objects.filter(tenant=tenant, date=new_date).exclude(id=entry.id).exists(), return Response({"error": "date_conflict", "detail": f"An entry already exists for {new_date}."}, status=409). Apply the same guard to the Sleep detail view if it shares the shape.

apps/fuel/views.py:613-634 (BodyWeightDetailView.patch — explicit 409 date_conflict guard for identical case)

detail: Confirmed real, low severity. WorkoutPlanDetailView.patch (apps/fuel/views.py:1229) computes the regen boundary with bare _date.today() (UTC). The same file (views.py:290) and the runtime plan path (runtime_views.py:1248) already use the project tenant-local helper today_in_tenant_tz(tenant), and runtime_views.py:1241-1243 carries an explicit warning that bare date.today() is computed in UTC and drifts a day in the evening for tenants offset from UTC, propagating into every materialized workout date. So this line is a genuine inconsistency, not intended behaviour. Impact: for a tenant in a positive-UTC-offset zone (e.g. JST UTC+9) editing a plan schedule_json/weeks during early-morning local hours (00:00-09:00 JST, when UTC is still the prior calendar day), today resolves to the tenant yesterday. Line 1230 deletes future PLANNED workouts on date__gte=today (including the already-current-from-the-user-view day) and line 1235 sets regen_start = max(today, start_date) to that UTC-yesterday date, so re-expansion can insert a planned workout dated one day in the past from the tenant perspective. Only PLANNED rows are affected; completed workouts are excluded/untouched. The runtime PATCH path shares the defect: reconcile_plan_state (services.py:96, default at 125) defaults today to _date.today(), and runtime_views.py:1397 calls it without passing today=, so it also anchors on UTC. Blast radius is small (only a ~9h daily window, only positive-offset tenants, only a 1-day boundary skew on planned rows), which is why low not medium.
suggested_fix: In apps/fuel/views.py:1229 replace today = _date.today() with today = today_in_tenant_tz(tenant) (helper already imported at views.py:17; the local 'from datetime import date as _date' at 1225 becomes redundant). In apps/fuel/runtime_views.py:1397 pass the tenant-local date explicitly: reconcile_plan_state(plan, plan.schedule_json, plan.weeks, today=today_in_tenant_tz(tenant)) — the today= injection seam already exists in services.py:96. Both are one-liners; add a regression test patching today_in_tenant_tz to assert the regen boundary uses tenant-local date.

feature: Per-Session Fuel Cron Derivation and Regeneration
detail: RuntimeFuelAuditView.get() computes today = date.today() at apps/fuel/runtime_views.py:1510, which is the server-local (UTC in production Azure Container Apps) date rather than the tenant-local date. This today drives the daily-note document lookup (slug=str(today), line 1515), the 14-day planned-workout window (date__gte=today, lines 1526-1527), and orphan detection. Every other date-sensitive path in the SAME file uses today_in_tenant_tz(tenant) — lines 148-151, 742, 814, and 1248 — and the plan-create docstring at lines 1241-1243 explicitly warns "Never bare date.today(): that is computed in UTC and drifts a day in the evening for tenants offset from UTC." The helper today_in_tenant_tz is already imported at line 16. For east-of-UTC tenants (e.g. the canonical Asia/Tokyo tenant, UTC+9), between 00:00 and 09:00 local time the UTC date lags by one day, so the audit resolves 'today' as yesterday: it reads yesterday's daily Fuel section (which the guidance at lines 1657-1666 then instructs the agent to deliver "verbatim" as today's locked plan) and shifts the 14-day window one day early. Impact is a real but narrow morning-window advisory issue: the audit is a read-only tool that biases the agent's guidance, it does not mutate data, and the agent can still self-correct from other context — hence low severity. Confirmed at the code level: a single inconsistent call against an established same-file convention plus an explicit in-file warning against this exact pattern.
suggested_fix: Replace `today = date.today()` at line 1510 with `today = today_in_tenant_tz(tenant)` (already imported at line 16 and used throughout this file). Keep `now = datetime.now(tz=UTC)` as-is, since horizon_48h_end is an absolute instant compared against UTC-aware scheduled_at values.
evidence: apps/fuel/runtime_views.py:1515,1526-1527 (today drives daily-note slug lookup + 14d workout window)
apps/fuel/runtime_views.py:1241-1243 (plan-create docstring explicitly forbids bare date.today() for tz drift)
apps/fuel/runtime_views.py:1657-1666 (_audit_guidance tells agent to deliver today_plan.raw_section verbatim)
expected_behaviour: derive_fuel_cron_jobs() is a pure function that reads planned Workout rows with scheduled_at in the next 48h and returns one cron-job dict per session named '_fuel:{short_id}' (8-hex UUID prefix), using date-specific cron expressions (M H D MON *) in the user's timezone. regenerate_fuel_crons() diffs the desired set against the container's current _fuel:* crons (via cron.list), adds missing, removes stale. Only fires for tenants with FuelProfile.use_session_scheduling=True. Gated on tenant not hibernated/suspended. Called via QStash on Workout post_save/post_delete (30s debounce, idempotency_key='regen-fuel-{tenant_id}') and by the hourly fleet reconcile (reconcile_fuel_crons_task).

detail: Two sub-claims; only the second is real, and it is data-integrity, not a medium logic bug. CLAIM 1 (404 vs 400 for missing pillar) is NOT a defect: _validate_record_body returns 404 pillar_not_available whenever pillar is not in ALLOWED_PILLARS (only gravity), so a missing or empty pillar yields 404 by intentional, documented design. The module docstring (views.py lines 19-20) states only pillar=gravity is accepted and others 404 until snapshot pipelines ship; the same 404-pillar pattern is used across all endpoints (e.g. VoicePrefSet 572-576). The spec line about missing fields returning 400 is deliberately overridden for the pillar field by the gating design. The 404 body returns allowed=[gravity], fully actionable, so a caller learns exactly what to send whether pillar was absent or merely unsupported. The omitted-vs-unsupported status distinction is cosmetic when only one pillar value is ever valid; no real user/operator harm. CLAIM 2 (non-atomic topic+insight) is REAL but LOW: _record_insight_impl (views.py 355-365) calls resolve_topic then AssistantInsight.objects.create. resolve_topic is decorated transaction.atomic (topic_resolver.py 47) and commits a PROPOSED TopicRegistry row (78-85) when its atomic block exits. RecordInsightView.post (424-434) does not wrap the call, and there is no ATOMIC_REQUESTS setting in config, so Django autocommits the proposed topic before the insight create runs. If the create fails in the narrow inter-statement window, an orphaned proposed topic persists, and grep found no cleanup cron to reap it. Impact is benign (proposed topics are expected, curated registry churn and the orphan has no insight referencing it) and the trigger window is narrow, hence low severity.
suggested_fix: Wrap the body of _record_insight_impl in transaction.atomic() so resolve_topic and AssistantInsight.objects.create commit together. The nested atomic in resolve_topic then becomes a savepoint that rolls back with the outer block if the insight create fails, preventing the orphaned proposed topic.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/insights/insights/record/ requires JWT authentication. Body must include pillar (must be in ALLOWED_PILLARS), topic (string, required), statement (string, required), and optionally evidence_refs (dict) and confidence (float in [0.0, 1.0]). model_version is captured up to 128 chars. topic is resolved via resolve_topic: exact canonical slug → alias match → creates a proposed TopicRegistry row. Returns 201 with the serialized insight (status=open). Missing or invalid fields return 400.

detail: Confirmed, but narrow. search_lessons() (apps/lessons/services.py:106-119) returns a lazy, unevaluated QuerySet; the pgvector CosineDistance SQL only executes when the result is iterated. In RuntimeLessonSearchView.get (apps/integrations/runtime_views.py:1716-1741) that iteration happens at line 1738, OUTSIDE the try/except at lines 1724-1735. So the try/except only guards (a) QuerySet construction and (b) the eager generate_embedding(query) HTTP call to OpenAI inside search_lessons (services.py:108), which is the most likely failure point and IS correctly mapped to the spec'd {"error":"search_failed"} 500 envelope. But a failure during the actual DB execution (Postgres OperationalError, pgvector extension unavailable, network drop to DB) is raised at line 1738 and is NOT caught. No custom DRF EXCEPTION_HANDLER is configured (verified absent in config/), and a raw django.db error is not an APIException, so DRF's default handler will not convert it; the caller gets a generic unhandled 500 instead of the search_failed envelope. Impact is low: this is an internal /runtime/ endpoint consumed by the OpenClaw runtime (machine, not an end user); DB-execution failure is rare and usually coincides with a broader outage; and the runtime treats both a raw 500 and the search_failed envelope as failure. The only real effect is a violation of the documented error-envelope contract in this one DB-failure path, slightly degrading error consistency/observability.
suggested_fix: Force evaluation of the QuerySet inside the guarded region. Either return list(...) from search_lessons() (services.py:115-119), or materialize eagerly in the view: change line 1725 to lessons = list(search_lessons(tenant=tenant, query=query, limit=limit)) inside the existing try block. The existing except Exception at line 1731 then catches a DB OperationalError and maps it to the search_failed envelope.

apps/lessons/services.py:30-46 (generate_embedding is the eager OpenAI HTTP call already covered by the guard)

Calls search_lessons (pgvector semantic search). Returns {tenant_id, query, count, results: [{...lesson, similarity: float}]}. Returns 500 on search failure.

detail:
Confirmed real. In RuntimeJournalSearchView.get (apps/integrations/runtime_views.py:1995) the limit param is parsed as min(int(request.query_params.get('limit','20')),50) inside a method with no surrounding try/except. A non-numeric value such as ?limit=abc raises an uncaught ValueError. No custom DRF EXCEPTION_HANDLER is configured (grep over config/ and apps/integrations/ found none), so it propagates to Django's default handler and yields a 500 instead of a 400 validation error. This contradicts the pattern used everywhere else in the file: every other limit-bearing endpoint calls _parse_positive_int(...) wrapped in try/except ValueError returning HTTP 400 (canonical example lines 1716-1722). The identical bare-int bug also exists at line 2687. Impact is bounded: the route is internal-auth gated (_internal_auth_or_401) and called by the OpenClaw container runtime (nbhd_journal_search tool), not by end users or the public, and the caller normally passes a valid integer or omits the param. It is a genuine robustness/contract gap (wrong status code, inconsistent with sibling endpoints) with a narrow trigger surface, hence low severity rather than the claimed medium.</confirmed_detail>
<parameter name="suggested_fix">Replace the bare cast with the existing helper, mirroring lines 1716-1722: wrap limit = _parse_positive_int(request.query_params.get('limit'), default=20, max_value=50) in a try/except ValueError that returns a Response with status 400 and detail str(exc). Apply the identical change at line 2687.

detail: Confirmed in code. RuntimeProfileUpdateView.patch validates fields inconsistently. timezone (invalid name) and location_lat/location_lon (out-of-range or non-numeric) return explicit 400 errors (invalid_timezone, invalid_coordinates). But display_name (>100 chars), language (>10 chars), and location_city (>255 chars) — and any of these arriving empty after .strip() — are silently skipped: the guards (if name and len(name) <= 100, etc.) simply do not append to updated_fields, with no error path (lines 2376-2392). If every field the caller supplied falls into a silent-skip branch, updated_fields is empty and the endpoint returns 400 no_changes / "No valid fields to update." (lines 2413-2417), which misrepresents the failure: a value rejected for being too long is reported identically to sending no fields. The caller is the OpenClaw container runtime (an LLM agent under internal auth), not a human, so there is no UI to surface the dropped value. Impact: the agent cannot distinguish "no field sent" from "field rejected for length," so it may believe the profile is in sync when the update was actually dropped, or cannot explain to the user why a stated preference (e.g. a long display name) did not take. Real but genuinely low — these are edge-case lengths on user-confirmed conversational values, and the partial-success case (one valid field plus one too-long field) still saves the valid field, dropping only the bad one.
suggested_fix: Make length/empty validation symmetric with the timezone and coordinate paths: when a field is present in data but fails its length check (or is empty after strip), return an explicit 400 (e.g. invalid_display_name / invalid_language / invalid_location_city, with a detail naming the constraint) instead of silently skipping it. At minimum, treat a present-but-invalid field as an error rather than a no-op so the agent gets an actionable response distinct from no_changes.

apps/integrations/runtime_views.py:2361-2372 & 2394-2411 (timezone and coordinates DO return explicit 400 errors, confirming the asymmetry)

detail: Confirmed: the DELETE handler in WeeklyReviewDetailView (apps/journal/views.py:455-459) returns Response(status=status.HTTP_204_NO_CONTENT) without the headers=DEPRECATION_HEADERS argument, whereas every sibling handler passes it consistently — GET (line 445), PATCH (line 453), and the paired list/create view's GET (429) and POST (436). The feature record's expected_behaviour explicitly states "Deprecated headers on all responses." So DELETE alone omits the Deprecation: true + Warning: 299 headers (defined at lines 62-65). Real-world impact is minimal: the delete itself works correctly (returns 204, tenant-scoped), and these headers are purely informational deprecation telemetry that an end-user/subscriber never perceives. The only affected consumer is a developer/operator inspecting response headers to track deprecated-endpoint usage; for the DELETE method they get an incomplete signal. This is a genuine but cosmetic spec-vs-code consistency gap on a deprecated endpoint, not a functional or security defect — matching the low-severity ux concern.
suggested_fix: Add headers=DEPRECATION_HEADERS to the DELETE response: return Response(status=status.HTTP_204_NO_CONTENT, headers=DEPRECATION_HEADERS) to match the other methods on the view and satisfy the "all responses" spec.
evidence: docs/feature-audit/areas/journal-core__1.json:111-121 (FA-0690 expected_behaviour: Deprecated headers on all responses)
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/journal/reviews/<uuid>/ returns the review scoped to the tenant or 404. PATCH accepts partial updates. DELETE removes and returns 204. Deprecated headers on all responses. Requires IsAuthenticated.

detail: Confirmed asymmetry. TemplateDetailView.patch() fires update_tenant_config_task.delay(tenant.id) whenever the edited template is the default (apps/journal/views.py:504-511), regenerating the agent's templates.md skill reference. TemplateDetailView.delete() promotes an alternate to is_default=True when the current default is deleted (lines 520-525) but never fires update_tenant_config_task, so the change to the effective default is not pushed. The default template materially affects container config: apps/orchestrator/personas.py render_templates_md() (lines 320-345) calls apps.journal.services.get_default_template() (services.py:207, filters is_default=True) and emits templates.md describing that template's name/slug/sections to the agent. After a delete-and-promote, the container's templates.md keeps describing the now-deleted template until an independent config bump (image bump, wake, or another template PATCH) refreshes it. Impact: a transient staleness window where the assistant's daily-note template reference points at a template the user just deleted. No data loss, no crash, and the DB stays correct (a default always exists via the explicit promotion plus get_default_template's fallback). Low severity / low frequency (only when deleting the default template while alternates exist), but a genuine missing config-push relative to the PATCH path's own established pattern.
suggested_fix: Mirror the PATCH path in delete(): inside the existing `if template.is_default:` block, after `template.delete()`, fire the same best-effort push wrapped in try/except — import update_tenant_config_task from apps.orchestrator.tasks and call .delay(str(tenant.id)). This regenerates templates.md from the newly-promoted default. Only push when the deleted template was the default, since non-default deletes do not change templates.md.
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/journal/templates/<id>/ returns the template or 404. PATCH updates fields; if is_default is set to true after the PATCH, triggers an async update_tenant_config_task (best-effort, non-blocking). DELETE removes the template; if it was the default, promotes another template as default; returns 400 if it's the only remaining template. Requires IsAuthenticated.

detail: Confirmed code-level asymmetry. In SidebarTreeView.get (document_views.py:445-468) the queryset has no explicit order_by, so it inherits Document.Meta.ordering=['-updated_at'] (models.py:198-200). Only daily notes get an explicit chronological re-sort (lines 461-463: tree['daily'].sort by slug desc). Weekly reviews are passed straight to tree.get('weekly', [])[:12] with no slug sort, so the cap keeps the 12 most-recently-EDITED reviews, not the 12 most-recent weeks, and the 12 shown are ordered by edit time rather than week order. Weekly slugs are date strings (YYYY-MM-DD, the week's Monday — confirmed by dashboard/views.py:60-72 _WEEKLY_SLUG_RE), so they are lexicographically date-sortable exactly like daily slugs; the fix is identical to the daily path. User impact: a user with more than 12 weekly reviews (sustained ~3+ months of weekly use) who edits an old review bumps it into the visible top-12 and silently displaces a more-recent week from the sidebar; within the list the order is edit-recency, not chronological. The data is never lost (the document still exists and is reachable directly), so this is a visibility/ordering glitch, not data loss — low severity, and only manifests beyond the 12-item cap.
suggested_fix: Mirror the daily-note handling: after building the tree, add `if 'weekly' in tree: tree['weekly'].sort(key=lambda x: x['slug'], reverse=True)` (next to lines 461-463) so weekly reviews are sorted by slug (date) descending before the [:12] cap. Optionally apply the same to 'monthly' if present. This guarantees the 12 shown are the 12 most-recent weeks in chronological order regardless of edit time.
evidence: apps/journal/document_views.py:445-468 (no explicit order_by; daily sorted at 461-463, weekly not; [:12] cap at line 468)
apps/dashboard/views.py:60-72 (weekly slug is a parseable YYYY-MM-DD date, so it is lexicographically date-sortable like daily slugs)
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/journal/tree/ returns a list of {kind, label, items[]} where each item has slug, title, updated_at. Future daily notes (slug > today) are hidden. Daily notes are capped at 30, weekly reviews at 12. Cached via @tenant_cache(ttl=120, tag='sidebar'). Requires IsAuthenticated.

detail: Confirmed. In SessionListView.get (apps/journal/session_views.py:162-164) the `since` query param is read with no parsing and passed straight to qs.filter(session_start__gte=since). session_start is a Django DateTimeField (session_models.py:51), so a malformed value like ?since=not-a-date makes Django's DateTimeField.to_python/get_prep_value raise django.core.exceptions.ValidationError at query-evaluation time (when SessionDetailSerializer(qs, many=True).data forces the queryset). No custom EXCEPTION_HANDLER is configured (config/settings/base.py REST_FRAMEWORK block), so DRF's default handler applies, and it only converts Http404/PermissionDenied/APIException subclasses, NOT django.core.exceptions.ValidationError. The exception therefore propagates as an HTTP 500. The sibling TaskListView already validates dates via _parse_iso_date and returns 400 (lifecycle_views.py:27-36, 208-213), and the SAME view validates `limit` with a 400 (session_views.py:170-176), so `since` is an inconsistent omission. User impact: an authenticated API client passing a typo'd or wrong-format `since` gets an opaque 500 instead of an actionable 400, plus a noisy server error. Low severity: requires malformed input from an authenticated caller, no data-integrity or security impact, only a wrong status code and degraded error UX.
suggested_fix: Validate `since` before filtering, mirroring the existing pattern. Parse it (django.utils.dateparse.parse_datetime/parse_date, or a helper like _parse_iso_date) inside a try/except that returns Response({"detail": "since must be an ISO date/datetime."}, status=400) on failure, then filter with the parsed value. This matches the limit-validation block already present a few lines below.
evidence: apps/journal/session_views.py:162-164 (unparsed since passed directly to DateTimeField filter)
apps/journal/session_views.py:170-176 (limit IS validated with a 400 in the same view, showing the inconsistency)
apps/journal/lifecycle_views.py:27-36 and 208-213 (sibling endpoint validates dates and returns 400 on bad input)
config/settings/base.py:130 (REST_FRAMEWORK block has no EXCEPTION_HANDLER override, so DRF default applies)
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/sessions/ requires PAT 'sessions:read' scope or JWT auth. Query params: project_identity (takes priority over project for filtering), project (name filter), since (ISO datetime, filters session_start__gte), include_test (bool, default false — excludes test_mode=True sessions), limit (int, default 50, max 100). Returns 400 on invalid limit. Returns list of session detail objects ordered by -session_start (model Meta default). Returns 404 if no tenant found.

detail: Confirmed. galaxy_summary (apps/lessons/views.py:282) reads `tenant = self.request.user.tenant` with no guard, whereas its three sibling detail=False actions all guard with `if not hasattr(request.user, "tenant"): return 400 {"error": "tenant_required"}` — refresh (122-123), galaxy_insights (325-326), galaxy_reflect (357-358) — and the ViewSet's own get_queryset guards the same way (line 50). Tenant.user is a OneToOneField with related_name="tenant" (apps/tenants/models.py:142), so for an IsAuthenticated user with no associated Tenant, accessing .tenant raises RelatedObjectDoesNotExist (subclass of Django ObjectDoesNotExist). There is no custom DRF EXCEPTION_HANDLER, and DRF's default handler does not catch ObjectDoesNotExist, so it propagates to a 500 with traceback instead of the intended controlled 400. User impact: an authenticated-but-tenantless account (staff/superuser via createsuperuser, or any user created outside the atomic tenant-provisioning path) calling GET /lessons/galaxy/summary/ gets an unhandled 500 instead of the clean 400 every sibling endpoint returns. Severity is low because the galaxy/game surface targets subscriber tenants, who are always created atomically with a Tenant (apps/tenants/services.py:35-48); only staff/superusers or rare tenantless accounts can trigger it, and there is no data exposure or corruption — purely an inconsistent error contract (500 vs 400).
suggested_fix: Add the identical guard at the top of galaxy_summary, mirroring the three sibling actions: `if not hasattr(request.user, "tenant"): return Response({"error": "tenant_required"}, status=status.HTTP_400_BAD_REQUEST)` before reading request.user.tenant.
evidence: apps/lessons/views.py:50-51 (get_queryset hasattr guard — same handled case)
config/ + apps/ grep: no custom DRF EXCEPTION_HANDLER catching ObjectDoesNotExist
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/lessons/galaxy/summary/ — IsAuthenticated required. Returns {total_stars, proto_count, ignited_count, radiant_count, supernova_count, cluster_count, recent_activity (last 5 stars by last_visited_at with id, text[:80], visited ISO)}. All counts aggregate approved lessons only. Returns 200.

feature: Runtime constellation notes — assistant pull tool (nbhd_constellation_notes)
detail: Confirmed N+1 query inefficiency in the q (semantic search) mode of constellation_notes_payload. search_lessons() (apps/lessons/services.py:106-119) returns a Lesson queryset with NO prefetch_related, whereas the recent_active_stars() path (agent_context.py:88-99) explicitly prefetches journal_entries and tutoring_sessions. The search path (agent_context.py:207) passes its unprefetched stars into build_star_context(star, full=True), which lazily evaluates star.journal_entries.all() and star.tutoring_sessions.all() (agent_context.py:134-135) -- 2 extra queries per star. With limit capped at 25 (runtime_views.py:1809) that is up to ~50 additional simple FK queries per nbhd_constellation_notes pull-tool call; at the default limit of 5 it is ~10 extra. Impact is real but bounded and non-functional: this is an internal assistant pull-tool endpoint (RuntimeConstellationNotesView), not a hot user-facing path; the queries are indexed FK lookups, and overall latency in search mode is already dominated by generate_embedding(query). No correctness or UX impact -- only marginally slower tool responses, and only in search mode. The QA verdict (concern / logic / low) is accurate.
suggested_fix: Add .prefetch_related('journal_entries', 'tutoring_sessions') inside search_lessons() in apps/lessons/services.py, applied BEFORE the [:limit] slice (slicing first prevents adding the prefetch afterward via plain queryset chaining). This mirrors the existing prefetch on recent_active_stars(). Alternatively attach it at the call site in agent_context.py:207 using a non-sliced base query, but the cleanest fix is within search_lessons before the slice.
evidence: apps/lessons/services.py:106-119 (search_lessons returns queryset with no prefetch_related, slices [:limit])
apps/lessons/agent_context.py:134-135 (build_star_context full=True reads star.journal_entries.all() and star.tutoring_sessions.all())
apps/lessons/agent_context.py:207 (q-mode search path feeds unprefetched stars into build_star_context)
expected_behaviour: GET /integrations/runtime/{tenant_id}/constellation/notes/?q=&star_id=&limit=5&days=30 — internal-auth only. Three modes in precedence: ?star_id gives full context for one approved star; ?q runs semantic search (generate_embedding + pgvector); default returns recently active stars (tutored, visited, or journaled on within days window, OR has galaxy_note at any time). Each star context includes: id, text, stage label, galaxy_note, tags, cluster_label, tutoring counts, last_tutored_at/last_visited_at, last 2 (compact) or 5 (full) journal entries, last 2 (compact) or 5 (full) tutoring session honest-signal summaries. Returns 200 with {mode, count, stars, summary: {total_stars, by_stage}, tenant_id}. 400 on invalid params, 500 on failure.

detail: Confirmed: the --threshold argument's help text is wrong. DEDUP_THRESHOLD = 0.65 (line 20) is the actual default and is wired into the argument via default=DEDUP_THRESHOLD (line 30), but that same line's help string reads "Similarity threshold (default 0.75)". An operator who runs --help and trusts the text expects deduplication at 0.75 cosine similarity, but the command actually runs at 0.65 — a more aggressive setting that groups and deletes lessons at lower semantic similarity (deletion at line 121, keeping only the longest text per group). Impact is real but bounded: it only affects operators who run the command WITHOUT an explicit --threshold (an explicit value overrides the default cleanly), and the command ships a --dry-run flag (lines 28, 111-115) that previews every lesson it would remove with its exact similarity score, so a careful operator catches the discrepancy before data loss. The code is internally consistent at 0.65; this is a help-text/documentation bug, not a logic error, hence low severity.
suggested_fix: Make the help text reflect the constant — either change the string to "Similarity threshold (default 0.65)" or, better, interpolate it: help=f"Similarity threshold (default {DEDUP_THRESHOLD})" so it can never drift again.
expected_behaviour: python manage.py dedup_lessons [--tenant UUID] [--dry-run] [--threshold FLOAT] — Processes ACTIVE tenants (or specific one). For each tenant: (1) deletes lessons tagged 'goal' or with context starting 'Goal' (these don't belong in constellation), (2) computes O(n^2) pairwise cosine similarity matrix from embeddings, (3) union-finds groups above threshold (default=0.65), (4) keeps longest lesson text in each group, deletes rest. On non-dry-run, triggers refresh_constellation after deletion. Dry-run prints groups without deleting.

detail: Confirmed: a Telegram user whose hibernation-buffered message is permanently dropped after 3 failed delivery attempts receives no notification, while a LINE user in the identical scenario does. When a BufferedMessage reaches _MAX_DELIVERY_ATTEMPTS=3 (hibernation.py:1324), it is marked delivered=True/FAILED and _send_apology_for_dropped_message is called (hibernation.py:1343). Inside that function the LINE branch sends a localized apology via _send_line_text (lines 966-978), but the else/Telegram branch only emits logger.info and sends nothing to the user (lines 979-986). The user's message silently vanishes. Impact is real but narrow: it triggers only when a buffered message fails delivery 3 consecutive times (e.g. the container keeps erroring on /telegram-webhook after wake), and the user can still re-send. Both the code comment and the feature record document the Telegram apology as deliberately deferred, but the stated rationale (forward_to_openclaw has its own retry envelope, so head-of-line stalls are less acute) explains the retry behavior, not why the user is left uninformed when a message is genuinely dropped. The cross-channel inconsistency (same failure, different UX for no user-facing reason) is what makes it a defect rather than intended design. All scaffolding already exists: the error_msg framework with dropped_message / dropped_message_with_excerpt keys (English plus other languages), excerpt extraction, and strip_internal_framing are in place; only the Telegram send call is missing.
suggested_fix: Replace the log-only Telegram branch in _send_apology_for_dropped_message (hibernation.py:979-986) with an actual send mirroring the LINE branch: resolve the tenant's Telegram chat id (e.g. tenant.user.telegram_chat_id) and call the existing Telegram send helper used by the router/telegram_bot send paths with the already-computed `text`, wrapped in try/except + logger.exception so a send failure cannot break delivery accounting. The localized text/excerpt are already built above the channel branch, so no new message plumbing is needed. Guard for a missing chat id (early return, as the LINE branch does for a missing line_user_id).
evidence: apps/orchestrator/hibernation.py:943-986 (_send_apology_for_dropped_message — LINE sends, Telegram log-only at 979-986)
apps/orchestrator/hibernation.py:1324-1345 (drop-at-cap path marks FAILED and calls _send_apology_for_dropped_message for Telegram singleton)

expected_behaviour: QStash task `deliver_buffered_messages` — published with 45s delay after wake. Per-iteration: checks next undelivered BufferedMessage. LINE messages: `_claim_buffered_batch_for_tenant` picks up contiguous non-voice rows under the attempts cap (SELECT FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED, lease = timeout * 1.5). Batch > 1 rows are coalesced into one `/v1/chat/completions` POST with delineated content via `format_coalesced_user_content`. Response is relayed via `relay_ai_response_to_line`. Telegram messages: singleton claims via `_claim_next_buffered_message`, forwarded to `/telegram-webhook` via `forward_to_openclaw`. On success: marks `delivered=True`, `delivery_status=DELIVERED`, clears lease. On failure: increments `delivery_attempts`, clears lease, breaks (preserves queue order). At `_MAX_DELIVERY_ATTEMPTS=3`: marks `delivered=True/FAILED`, calls `_send_apology_for_dropped_message` (LINE only), increments `dropped`. Raises RuntimeError at end if any failed (signals QStash to retry).

detail: Confirmed at code level. The hourly fleet-wide reconcile_fuel_crons_task (tasks.py:1042-1045) only skips tenants lacking container_fqdn. Hibernated containers retain their container_fqdn (hibernation sets hibernated_at but never clears the FQDN), so the sweep proceeds and calls regenerate_fuel_crons(tenant) directly (line 1047), which itself has no hibernation/suspension guard (fuel_cron.py:107-131). Its first action is invoke_gateway_tool(tenant, cron.list, ...), which POSTs to a scaled-to-zero container, raising requests.RequestException then GatewayError, caught at fuel_cron.py:138 with logger.exception (a full stack trace) plus errors++. For any FuelProfile with use_session_scheduling=True whose tenant is hibernated/suspended but still has a FQDN, this emits a stack-trace log and inflated error telemetry every hour. The sibling per-tenant regenerate_fuel_crons_task (tasks.py:1024-1026) deliberately guards exactly this case with a comment about avoiding recurring GatewayError noise for idle tenants and notes reconcile-on-wake catches up; the fleet path is missing that identical guard, an intentional-looking inconsistency. Impact is bounded: log/observability noise only, no user-facing breakage and no data loss (reconcile-on-wake rebuilds the crons), and it requires the non-default cutover flag use_session_scheduling=True (default False) plus a hibernated/suspended tenant that still has an FQDN. Hence real but low severity.
suggested_fix: Add the same guard the per-tenant task uses to the fleet loop: after the container_fqdn check (tasks.py:1044), `continue` when tenant.hibernated_at is not None or tenant.status == Tenant.Status.SUSPENDED. Optionally also exclude these at the queryset level (filter tenant__hibernated_at__isnull=True and exclude SUSPENDED status) to avoid loading them. Best to centralize the guard so the per-tenant and fleet paths cannot drift again, e.g. push the hibernation/suspension check down into regenerate_fuel_crons itself.

apps/orchestrator/tasks.py:1024-1026 (per-tenant regenerate_fuel_crons_task DOES guard hibernated_at/SUSPENDED with the exact 'recurring GatewayError noise for idle tenants' rationale)
apps/orchestrator/fuel_cron.py:107-141 (regenerate_fuel_crons has no hibernation guard; cron.list GatewayError caught with logger.exception + errors++)
apps/cron/gateway_client.py:145-231 (invoke_gateway_tool POSTs to container_fqdn; RequestException to scaled-to-zero container raises GatewayError)

apps/fuel/models.py:367-374 (use_session_scheduling default=False cutover flag, limits prevalence)

Skips if `hibernated_at` is set or status=SUSPENDED (logs skip). Calls `regenerate_fuel_crons(tenant)` from `apps/orchestrator/fuel_cron.py`. detail: Real but cosmetic diagnostic bug in an operator-only management command. invoke_gateway_tool returns data.get('result', {}) (gateway_client.py:231). For cron.list, the codebase documents and overwhelmingly uses the nested shape {'details': {'jobs': [...]}} -- canonical comments at services.py:880 ('Gateway wraps in {details:{jobs:[...]}} or {jobs:[...]}') and services.py:1387, plus the dominant {'details':{'jobs':[...]}} test fixtures across orchestrator/fuel/cron tests. The robust consumers (_extract_cron_jobs at services.py:870-889, cron_exists at gateway_client.py:295) explicitly unwrap details. Check 5 at check_gateway_health.py:133 instead does result.get('jobs', []) directly on the raw result, returning [] for the nested shape, so it always prints 'returned 0 jobs' even when the container has live crons. Impact is limited: (1) the check still returns PASS correctly regardless of count; (2) the count is purely a diagnostic stdout line; (3) the 'masking the seeding step' worry is overstated because Check 6 _check_cron_jobs_seeded (lines 178-185) independently reads cron/jobs.json from the file share and prints the authoritative seeded job count WITH names, so an operator running the full health check is NOT actually misled about whether seeding happened -- the truth is available two checks down. Net: a single misleading 'returned 0 jobs' line in an internal diagnostic command. Real but low.
suggested_fix: Replace result.get('jobs', []) at check_gateway_health.py:133 with the existing helper: from apps.orchestrator.services import _extract_cron_jobs; job_count = len(_extract_cron_jobs(result) or []). This reuses the canonical unwrap that already handles both the flat {jobs:[...]} and nested {details:{jobs:[...]}} shapes, rather than duplicating shape logic.
evidence: apps/orchestrator/services.py:870-889 (_extract_cron_jobs unwraps details; comment line 880 documents both shapes)
apps/orchestrator/management/commands/check_gateway_health.py:178-190 (Check 6 reports authoritative seeded job count from cron/jobs.json, mitigating operator confusion)
expected_behaviour: Management command `check_gateway_health [--tenant-id UUID]`. Runs 7 sequential checks, stopping at first failure: (1) tenant exists and ACTIVE; (2) `socket.getaddrinfo` DNS resolution of container FQDN; (3) Key Vault secret `tenant-<uuid>-internal-key` readable via `read_key_vault_secret`; (4) GET `https://{fqdn}/health` → 200; (5) `invoke_gateway_tool(tenant, 'cron.list', {})` via `GatewayError`-catching wrapper; (6) Azure File Share read of `cron/jobs.json` → at least 1 job; (7) POST to `https://{fqdn}/api/v1/integrations/runtime/{id}/daily-note/append/` with internal key → 200/201. Prints PASS/FAIL per check with details.

detail: Confirmed. The hourly fleet-wide reconcile_fuel_crons_task (tasks.py:1030-1056) iterates every FuelProfile with use_session_scheduling=True and calls regenerate_fuel_crons(tenant) for any tenant whose container_fqdn is non-empty. Its only filter is 'if not tenant.container_fqdn: continue'. Hibernation does NOT clear container_fqdn: hibernate_idle_tenant (hibernation.py:108-110) only sets hibernated_at and scales the container to 0/0; container_fqdn is cleared only on deprovision/DELETE (services.py:741). So hibernated and suspended tenants pass the fqdn check, and the sweep issues a doomed gateway POST to a scaled-to-zero container. regenerate_fuel_crons (fuel_cron.py:107-141) has no hibernation/suspension guard of its own (it only checks use_session_scheduling), then calls invoke_gateway_tool 'cron.list', which after a 45s timeout plus one retry raises GatewayError, caught as summary['errors'] += 1. The signal-driven per-tenant path regenerate_fuel_crons_task (tasks.py:1018-1027) DOES skip hibernated/suspended tenants, with a comment (tasks.py:1021-1023) explicitly naming this exact failure mode (gateway-call noise on scale 0/0 containers). The fleet reconcile bypasses that reasoning. Operator impact: per hibernated/suspended tenant with an active session-scheduled fuel profile, every hourly sweep wastes up to ~90s on a failing gateway call, logs a GatewayError, and inflates totals['errors'] (masking real drift errors); the POST may also cold-start an idle container, partially undermining hibernation. No user-visible data loss (reconcile-on-wake catches up). Low severity given the small current fleet, but it scales with adoption.
suggested_fix: In reconcile_fuel_crons_task, add the same guard the per-tenant path uses: after the container_fqdn check, skip tenants where tenant.hibernated_at is not None or tenant.status == Tenant.Status.SUSPENDED (continue, not erroring). Cleaner still, filter at the queryset level: FuelProfile.objects.filter(use_session_scheduling=True, tenant__hibernated_at__isnull=True).exclude(tenant__status=Tenant.Status.SUSPENDED). Reconcile-on-wake already handles drift catch-up for skipped tenants.

Calls `regenerate_fuel_crons(tenant)` from `apps.orchestrator.fuel_cron`. detail: Confirmed spec deviation. handle() does a bare `return` at line 41 when a check fails instead of raising CommandError. In Django, run_from_argv only emits a non-zero exit (sys.exit(1)) when a CommandError propagates; a normal return from handle() lets the process exit 0. So check_gateway_health exits 0 even when a link in the chain fails, directly contradicting the spec's stated behavior "Exits non-zero at first failure." This is genuine, not a misread: _resolve_tenant already uses CommandError (lines 52, 55), so the import on line 7 is in active use; only the per-check failure path skips it. Impact is low: this is a hand-run operator diagnostic with NO programmatic consumers in the repo (grep across *.py/*.sh/*.yml/Makefile/*.md found zero callers besides the file itself — no CI workflow, shell script, Makefile target, or runbook invokes it and checks $?). An operator running it interactively reads the explicit color-coded "FAIL" / "Stopped at first failure." stderr output and discovers the failure regardless of exit code. The defect only bites if someone later chains it via && or wraps it in automation that trusts the exit code — a latent footgun rather than an active operator-facing failure.
suggested_fix: In handle(), on check failure raise CommandError("Gateway health check failed at: <check name>") instead of `return`, so the process exits non-zero per spec. CommandError is already imported (line 7) and used in _resolve_tenant, so the fix is a one-line change in the loop's failure branch. Keep the individual _check_* methods returning bool and printing their own FAIL lines; only the loop branch needs to raise.
evidence: apps/orchestrator/management/commands/check_gateway_health.py:37-43 (loop bare-returns on check failure instead of raising CommandError → process exits 0)
apps/orchestrator/management/commands/check_gateway_health.py:7,52,55 (CommandError imported and already used in _resolve_tenant, but not on the per-check failure path)

Exits non-zero at first failure. Defaults to first active tenant if `--tenant-id` not specified.

detail: Confirmed inconsistency. The backfill_welcomes management command gates the finance welcome on the raw tenant.finance_enabled DB flag (backfill_welcomes.py:68), while every other welcome path gates on tenant.finance_active, which ANDs the flag with the GRAVITY_ENABLED platform kill switch (tenants/models.py:707-718). I verified the underlying scheduler schedule_welcome (welcome_scheduler.py:48-141) has NO internal GRAVITY guard — it only short-circuits on welcomes_sent[feature] and pending-cron idempotency — so the looser gate is NOT compensated downstream. With GRAVITY_ENABLED=False (the production default, settings/base.py:465) and a tenant whose finance_enabled is still True from before Gravity was paused, the command would dispatch a finance welcome cron via the OpenClaw gateway for a feature that is platform-disabled and whose tools are absent from the agent manifest — a confusing, off-brand intro. Scope-limiting facts that lower severity: (1) the CI/CD deploy step does NOT run this command — it curls the HTTP endpoint (cron/views.py:830), which correctly uses finance_active (line 867); (2) the daily watchdog reconcile_welcomes_task also correctly uses finance_active (orchestrator/tasks.py:1146). The management command is documented (and referenced in ci-cd.yml:420) only as the MANUAL operator fallback when the deploy curl fails. So the defect only manifests when an operator hand-runs python manage.py backfill_welcomes against a DB containing finance_enabled=True tenants while GRAVITY is off. Real but narrow (manual-path plus specific DB-state preconditions), not a security or data-integrity issue.
suggested_fix: Change line 68 of apps/orchestrator/management/commands/backfill_welcomes.py from tenant.finance_enabled to tenant.finance_active so the manual command matches the HTTP-endpoint (cron/views.py:867) and watchdog (tasks.py:1146) paths, all respecting the GRAVITY_ENABLED kill switch.

apps/cron/views.py:867 (deploy HTTP endpoint uses finance_active — correct)
apps/finance/views.py:310 (toggle refuses enabling when GRAVITY_ENABLED off)

For each tenant: if fuel_enabled and feature in (fuel, both), calls _schedule_fuel_welcome; if finance_enabled and feature in (finance, both), calls _schedule_finance_welcome. Uses the same self-healing WelcomeStatus enum. Tallies per-feature outcome counts (scheduled, replaced_stale, skipped_pending, skipped_already_delivered, failed). Prints summary. Idempotent: re-running is safe because schedule_welcome skips already-delivered and still-pending welcomes.

detail: Confirmed. The agenda refresh_on tuple omits the typed Goal model (agenda_envelope.py:92) but _count_active_goals reads Goal.objects.filter(status=ACTIVE).count() (line 283). No post_save on Goal triggers a USER.md refresh: the Goal receiver (journal/signals.py:181) only auto-resolves PendingExtractions, and goal write paths (lifecycle_views.py serializer.save / goal.mark_achieved) never call push_user_md. So a Goal create/achieve/abandon via API does not refresh the agenda count. The same gap exists in the journal's own goals summary (journal/envelope.py:147, refresh_on=(Document,)) which also counts typed Goals, so this is a pre-existing class issue, not unique to the agenda section. There is also an internal inconsistency: the fleet-refresh docstring (tasks.py:831) claims signal-driven refreshes cover goal saves, but they do not for typed Goal rows. Impact is minor and bounded: staleness closes on the next Document/Workout/FuelGoal/PayoffPlan write or the hourly refresh_user_md_fleet cron (tasks.py:826, ~1h for active tenants). The count is only a synthesis hint; the rendered goals line directs the agent to call nbhd_goal_list for live state and the AGENTS.md reconcile gate forces nbhd_reconcile_scan before any goal claim, so the live source of truth is the tool path, not the bootstrap count. The AgendaEngagement sub-claim is weaker (engagement rows are written by the same proactive crons that push USER.md). Net: a real but minor freshness gap in the bootstrap envelope, not a user-felt correctness bug.
suggested_fix: Add Goal (and arguably Task) to the agenda section's refresh_on at agenda_envelope.py:92, and to the journal goals/tasks sections, so a typed Goal write debounce-pushes USER.md the same way Document/Workout/FuelGoal/PayoffPlan already do.
expected_behaviour: Registered via @register_section(key='agenda', order=5) in apps/orchestrator/agenda_envelope.py. Enabled for all tenants (lambda t: True). Renders: open task count, open goal count, planned workout count, active payoff plans, and 'untouched introductions' (features where fuel_enabled or finance_active but welcomes_sent[feature] is null). Includes agent guidance: 'read first, weave selectively, never enumerate'. Contributes to both USER.md and context_digest. Triggered by post_save/post_delete on Task, Goal, Workout, PayoffPlan, AgendaEngagement models.

feature: Entity registry CRUD API - Patch entry
detail: Confirmed. PATCH coerces the entry (views.py:1086), which preserves arbiter_judged_at via _KNOWN_FIELDS (entity_registry.py:45), then rebuilds the stored value via to_storage_value() (views.py:1122-1127). to_storage_value (entity_registry.py:98-116) only accepts name/relationship/notes/updated_at, so the stamp is dropped from the written entry. On the next hourly arbiter sweep, _entries_to_judge no longer sees the stamp (arbiter.py:118-119) and re-sends the entry to the LLM. The inventory note claiming the stamp survives the coerce/to_storage_value round-trip is incorrect. Impact is minor: it only fires when a user manually edits (via People settings) an entry the arbiter had confirmed is_pii=true; denied entries are skipped by the denylist check (arbiter.py:123) regardless of the stamp. Cost is one extra batched Haiku judgment for that entry on the next sweep (fractions of a cent, batched 50/call), and it self-corrects because the re-judgment re-stamps it (arbiter.py:274). No data corruption, no PII leak, no user-visible breakage. The redactor's to_storage_value use (redactor.py:388) only mints brand-new never-judged placeholders, so it is unaffected.
suggested_fix: Add an optional arbiter_judged_at parameter to to_storage_value() in apps/pii/entity_registry.py and emit it when set, then forward current.get('arbiter_judged_at') from the PATCH call site at views.py:1122-1127. Simpler alternative: in the PATCH path, write back the already-coerced current dict (which still carries the stamp) instead of rebuilding via to_storage_value.
expected_behaviour: PATCH /api/v1/tenants/settings/entity-registry/<placeholder>/ requires IsAuthenticated. Resolves tenant via request.user.tenant. Returns 404 if placeholder not in pii_entity_map or no tenant. Accepts body fields name (max 200 chars), relationship (max 80 chars), notes (max 500 chars) individually or in any combination; omitted fields are unchanged. Null value clears the field. Non-string values return 400. Oversized values return 400. On success, builds canonical storage value via to_storage_value() (drops empty optional fields to keep JSON compact), stamps updated_at with current ISO timestamp, writes back via Tenant.objects.filter(pk=pk).update(pii_entity_map=...) and updates in-memory. Returns 200 with {placeholder, name, relationship, notes, updated_at}.

feature: Span deduplication and adjacent merge in detection pipeline
detail: Confirmed but minor. DATE_OF_BIRTH is listed in TIER_POLICIES['starter']['entities'] (config.py:94) as a protected type, yet no detection pathway can ever emit it: DEBERTA_LABEL_MAP (config.py:16-82) maps no model label to DATE_OF_BIRTH (the model's DATE label is deliberately dropped, commented out at config.py:73-76), and get_pattern_recognizers (engine.py:104-114) only ever produces CREDIT_CARD, IBAN_CODE, and EMAIL_ADDRESS. In _detect_pii, model spans are only emitted when DEBERTA_LABEL_MAP.get(...) returns a value that is also in the entities list (redactor.py:545-546), and Presidio spans are gated the same way (redactor.py:570-572). So DATE_OF_BIRTH is unreachable. User impact is small: the entities list is a whitelist/filter, so a dangling entry is a no-op (no crash, no mis-redaction, no wrong behaviour). The only real effect is that birth dates are silently never redacted while the starter policy advertises DATE_OF_BIRTH coverage. Crucially, config.py:73-76 documents this as an intentional tradeoff (the model fires DATE on every yyyy-mm-dd journal heading, producing a false-positive flood), so the missing detection is by design; what remains is a stale/misleading config entry implying coverage that was deliberately removed. The merge/dedup logic (_merge_adjacent_spans, _deduplicate_overlapping) is correct, as the report itself notes.
suggested_fix: Remove DATE_OF_BIRTH from TIER_POLICIES['starter']['entities'] (config.py:94) so the policy no longer advertises coverage that cannot be produced — the cleanest option, consistent with the deliberate DATE omission documented at config.py:73-76. Alternatively, if birth-date redaction is genuinely wanted, add a narrowly-scoped context-anchored Presidio recognizer (keyed on "born"/"DOB"/"date of birth" rather than the noisy model DATE label) and map it; but removal is the right call given the documented false-positive rationale.
