apps/pii/engine.py:104-114 (Presidio recognizers: only CREDIT_CARD, IBAN_CODE, EMAIL_ADDRESS)
apps/pii/redactor.py:542-562 (model spans gated by DEBERTA_LABEL_MAP.get + entities membership)
apps/pii/redactor.py:568-581 (Presidio spans gated by entities membership)
expected_behaviour: _merge_adjacent_spans() sorts results by start position and merges consecutive spans of the same entity_type if they are separated by 0 or 1 characters (a space). The merged span uses the minimum score of its components. This handles GIVENNAME + LASTNAME both mapping to PERSON. _deduplicate_overlapping() then removes overlapping spans: keeps higher-score match; on score tie, keeps shorter (more specific) span. Applied before filtering in _detect_pii. The DeBERTa model's span boundaries may include trailing/leading whitespace from aggregation_strategy='simple'; these are trimmed before recording the span. Spans where start >= end after trimming are discarded.

detail: Real but minor UX inconsistency. In _handle_lesson_postback (apps/router/line_webhook.py:1501-1503), when a lesson:approve/lesson:dismiss button is tapped for a lesson no longer status=pending (already approved, dismissed, or gone), the handler returns silently with zero feedback. LINE lesson suggestion cards (apps/lessons/notifications.py:144-153) stay in chat scrollback and are never edited away, so a user can tap a stale button and get no response, which reads as a dead/broken button. The codebase itself shows silence is NOT the intended standard here: (1) the Telegram equivalent at extraction_callbacks.py:278-281 sends Already processed, and (2) the sibling LINE handler _handle_extraction_postback in the SAME file (lines 1427-1433) explicitly handles this with the comment 'Already resolved — send friendly acknowledgment instead of silence' and replies a thumbs-up 'That one is already added/skipped!'. So the lesson path is an oversight, not a deliberate design; the feature record's 'returns silently' merely describes current code. Impact is cosmetic only: no data corruption, no wrong action, and the status=pending guard correctly prevents double-processing. Only affects users tapping an old card after the lesson was already resolved.
suggested_fix: In _handle_lesson_postback, instead of the bare return at lines 1502-1503 when the pending lesson is not found, re-query the lesson without the status=pending filter; if it exists in a resolved state, send a friendly acknowledgment via _send_line_follow_up (e.g. already saved / already skipped), mirroring the sibling _handle_extraction_postback block at lines 1427-1433. A silent return remains fine only when the lesson truly does not exist.
evidence: apps/router/line_webhook.py:1306-1307 (dispatcher routes lesson: postbacks to this handler — live, reachable path)
apps/router/line_webhook.py:1427-1433 (sibling LINE handler explicitly acks already-resolved with comment 'send friendly acknowledgment instead of silence')
apps/lessons/notifications.py:144-153 (LINE cards emit lesson:approve/lesson:dismiss buttons that persist in scrollback)
expected_behaviour: Handles postback data format 'lesson:<action>:<lesson_id>'. Fetches Lesson scoped to tenant where status='pending'. If not found, returns silently. Rehydrates PII for the display text. On 'approve': sets status='approved', sets approved_at, saves, calls process_approved_lesson, sends '✅ Approved: <text[:100]>' via _send_line_follow_up. On 'dismiss': sets status='dismissed', saves, sends '❌ Dismissed: <text[:100]>'. process_approved_lesson errors are logged but don't block the approve response.

detail: Confirmed localization gap. In the Telegram poller path (apps/router/poller.py:846-853), the PENDING/PROVISIONING branch sends a hardcoded English string ("Your assistant is waking up! ... send your message again in a moment!") via self._send_message, instead of error_msg(lang, "waking_up"). The webhook path (apps/router/views.py:246-255) correctly localizes the identical scenario using lang = tenant.user.language or "en" and error_msg(lang, "waking_up"). The poller already imports error_msg (poller.py:27) and uses it with tenant.user.language for many nearby messages, including provisioning-related ones (e.g. poller.py:1257-1259: lang = getattr(tenant.user, "language", None) or "en"; error_msg(lang, "telegram_provisioning_almost_ready")). The "waking_up" key has real non-English translations in error_messages.py (Japanese at lines 130-134), so the localized output genuinely differs from English. User impact: a non-English-speaking subscriber whose tenant is still provisioning, messaging via the polling (single-revision Telegram) path, receives this transient (~1 minute) message in English regardless of language preference; the next post-wake message is localized, so impact is cosmetic and short-lived — hence low severity. The adjacent SUSPENDED branch (poller.py:855-865) shares the same hardcoded-English pattern but is outside this finding's scope.
suggested_fix: Replace the hardcoded string at poller.py:848-852 with the localized call, mirroring views.py:247-255 and the existing poller pattern at lines 1257-1259: derive lang = getattr(tenant.user, "language", None) or "en", then self._send_message(chat_id, error_msg(lang, "waking_up")). Optionally apply the same treatment to the adjacent SUSPENDED branch using error_msg(lang, "suspended", billing_url=...).

Returns a localized sendMessage response (error_msg(lang, 'waking_up')) telling the user to try again in a minute. No forwarding to OpenClaw occurs. Lang is taken from tenant.user.language or 'en'.

detail: Confirmed real localization inconsistency. The webhook path (apps/router/views.py:258-270) renders the suspended-tenant message via error_msg(lang, "suspended", billing_url=...), which has translations for ~12 locales (en/ja/es/fr/de/pt/zh/ko/it/nl/ru/ar). The poller path (apps/router/poller.py:857-865) instead sends a hardcoded English literal for the identical SUSPENDED + not-trial + no-subscription state. The poller is a live production path (run via the poll_telegram management command; Telegram delivery is poller-based per the single-revision setup). It is also an internal inconsistency, not just a missing feature: the same poller file already imports error_msg (line 27) and already localizes other unavailable-state messages with the tenant's language (telegram_provisioning_almost_ready / telegram_restarting / telegram_resend_after_failed_wait at lines 1257-1275, using getattr(tenant.user, 'language', None) or 'en'). Impact: a non-English subscriber whose account is paused (trial ended or payment lapsed with no active Stripe subscription) sees the pause/billing explanation in English instead of their language. Low severity — the message still contains the working billing URL and the user can recover; only the copy is un-localized for this one branch.

detail: Confirmed. When a Telegram photo's largest size exceeds 5 MB, _download_photo logs a warning and returns None (poller.py:531-533), so _upload_photo_to_workspace returns None (poller.py:570-571) and image_path stays None in the forward path (poller.py:939-943). The "if image_path:" guard is False (poller.py:946-948), so no "[Photo attached: ...]" prefix is added. Separately, _extract_message_text builds message_text as the caption or the literal "User sent a photo" (poller.py:1097-1099). Net result: the message is forwarded to the assistant with only that text, NO image, and NO explanation that the photo was dropped. The user gets a confident AI reply that appears to address a photo the assistant never saw. This is inconsistent with sibling media handlers in the same function: oversized documents get an explicit "too large to process" marker injected into the text (FA-1025), and failed voice transcription yields "[Voice message couldn't transcribe, please try sending as text]" (FA-1024). Only the photo path silently degrades. Severity is low: it triggers only above 5 MB (Telegram compresses typical inline photos, so it is not the common case), it is degraded behavior not a crash, and no data is lost, but the silent blind reply is real and user-visible.
suggested_fix: Mirror the document/voice pattern so the user is told the photo could not be processed. Simplest approach: in the forward path, when msg_data has a photo but _upload_photo_to_workspace returns None (image_path is None), append an explicit marker to message_text such as "[Photo too large to process - please send a smaller image under 5 MB]" so the assistant (and thus the user) knows the image was not seen. Optionally return a sentinel from _download_photo/_upload_photo_to_workspace to distinguish the over-5MB case from a generic download failure, so the marker can be size-specific.

expected_behaviour: In the poller's _handle_update, if message has photo field, _upload_photo_to_workspace downloads the largest photo (max 5MB) from Telegram getFile, re-encodes as base64, stores to workspace/media/inbound/{hash}.{ext} on the tenant's Azure File Share via upload_workspace_file_binary, and returns the container-local path /home/node/.openclaw/workspace/media/inbound/.... The message_text is then prepended with [Photo attached: {path}]. On outbound, _send_photo (poller) and _send_telegram_photo (drain) download a workspace image from Azure File Share and call Telegram sendPhoto with multipart data. Captions are rendered via markdown_to_plaintext (max 1024 chars).

detail: Verified discrepancy, but no live user/operator impact - latent code-consistency issue only. get_forwarding_timeout (services.py:259-274) classifies a tenant's model against REASONING_MODELS only; it omits BYO_SLOW_MODELS (ANTHROPIC_SONNET_MODEL/ANTHROPIC_OPUS_MODEL, constants.py:188-191). Its sibling _resolve_chat_timeout (pending_queue.py:299-331) correctly checks both sets. Concrete effect: in the webhook path (views.py:354-355) a BYO Claude tenant gets wh_timeout=30s instead of 120s. However: (1) the webhook handler telegram_webhook is INACTIVE in prod - poller.py calls deleteWebhook (poller.py:134-142) so getUpdates polling is the live path; (2) even in the ACTIVE poller path, get_forwarding_timeout's result (poller.py:1357) is used ONLY for typing/nudge timing - the per-attempt chat-completion timeout is re-resolved in the drain via _resolve_chat_timeout, which DOES include BYO_SLOW_MODELS (explicit comment at poller.py:1353-1356); (3) even if the webhook path were re-enabled, the agent replies directly via the bot token even if the forward times out (views.py:351-353), so a short timeout would only miss usage-tracking capture, not drop the user's reply. Net: no behavior any user or operator currently experiences is wrong; this is a maintainability/consistency gap between two timeout helpers that could only bite if the webhook path were ever reactivated as the primary delivery path.
suggested_fix: Make get_forwarding_timeout consistent with _resolve_chat_timeout by also checking BYO_SLOW_MODELS: import it (with the same defensive ImportError fallback used in pending_queue.py:320-326) and treat model-in-REASONING_MODELS-or-BYO_SLOW_MODELS as the slow case. Optionally extract one shared classifier to remove the duplication across services.py, pending_queue.py, and orchestrator/hibernation.py.
expected_behaviour: get_forwarding_timeout(tenant) returns (timeout_seconds, is_reasoning). Checks tenant.preferred_model against REASONING_MODELS and BYO_SLOW_MODELS sets. Reasoning/slow models get REASONING_MODEL_TIMEOUT (240s); others get DEFAULT_CHAT_TIMEOUT (120s). In the webhook path, is_reasoning drives wh_timeout=120.0 vs 30.0. In the drain path, _resolve_chat_timeout returns the timeout for httpx.post.

detail: Confirmed. SignupView reads the raw email (auth_views.py:188, no normalization) and checks duplicates with a case-sensitive exact filter User.objects.filter(email=email) (line 207), then passes the same raw value to create_user(username=email, email=email, ...) (lines 213-216). The custom User model (apps/tenants/models.py:23) inherits AbstractUser's default UserManager whose create_user runs Django normalize_email, which lowercases ONLY the domain, not the local part. The email field is not overridden so it has NO DB unique constraint; username (set to the email) is unique but case-sensitive in Postgres, so it does not block the insert either. Result: signing up as Test@x.com then test@x.com both succeed, creating two accounts for the same effective address. This is inconsistent with the password-reset path which correctly uses email__iexact (line 105) - that path would then iexact-match both duplicates and resolve ambiguously via .first(). Real but low-impact: the domain-case collision is already handled by normalize_email, and triggering it requires deliberate case variation in the email local part (most users/clients type or lowercase emails consistently). Blast radius is duplicate accounts plus reset/login ambiguity, not data loss or a security hole.
suggested_fix: Make the signup duplicate check and the persisted value case-insensitive, consistent with the reset path. Simplest: lowercase/strip the email on entry (email = (request.data.get('email') or '').strip().lower(), mirroring PasswordResetRequestView line 93) and/or change line 207 to filter(email__iexact=email). For a hard guarantee, add a case-insensitive unique constraint via UniqueConstraint(Lower('email')).
evidence: apps/tenants/models.py:23 (User extends AbstractUser, no custom UserManager, no email unique override)
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/auth/signup/ accepts {email, password, display_name (optional, defaults to 'Friend')}. If PREVIEW_ACCESS_KEY is set in settings, an invite_code field must also be supplied and must match exactly, otherwise returns 403. If the email is already registered returns 409. On success, creates the User via create_user (sets username=email) and returns {access, refresh} JWT pair with HTTP 201. The refresh token is minted via RefreshToken.for_user (no pw_iat claim — the user has never rotated their password, so pw_iat=0 is acceptable at this stage). No email verification step exists.

detail: Confirmed. SignupView.post() (apps/tenants/auth_views.py:187-227) goes from the required-fields check straight to User.objects.create_user(password=password, ...) and never calls validate_password(), even though validate_password is imported (line 7) and the project configures a full AUTH_PASSWORD_VALIDATORS set (config/settings/base.py:105-110: MinimumLength, CommonPassword, Numeric, UserAttributeSimilarity). The sibling PasswordResetConfirmView DOES enforce these validators (line 153) before set_password, creating a genuine inconsistency: an attacker/user POSTing directly to /api/v1/auth/signup/ can register with a password as weak as a single character, all-numeric, or a common word like 'password'. The only strength gate on signup is client-side (frontend/app/signup/page.tsx:30, password.length < 8), trivially bypassed by hitting the API directly. The dedup/token half of the claim is accurate but benign and intended: create_user routes through Django's _create_user -> make_password (not the custom set_password override), so password_last_changed_at stays null at signup; the JWT validator treats a null stamp as 'never rotated' and accepts the RefreshToken.for_user token (which lacks pw_iat) -- documented intended behaviour per the feature record and models.py:80-91, not a defect. Impact is low: weak passwords only weaken the account holder's own login (easier brute-force/credential-stuffing of one self-owned account), with no platform-wide blast radius, and the normal browser path is gated client-side. The real defect is the missing server-side strength enforcement on signup, inconsistent with the reset flow and the configured validators.
suggested_fix: In SignupView.post(), after the required-fields / invite-code / duplicate-email checks and before User.objects.create_user, call validate_password(password) inside a try/except DjangoValidationError, returning HTTP 400 with {"detail": " ".join(exc.messages)} on failure — mirroring exactly what PasswordResetConfirmView already does at lines 152-158. This makes the two password-entry paths consistent and enforces the AUTH_PASSWORD_VALIDATORS that are already configured.
evidence: apps/tenants/auth_views.py:187-227 (SignupView never calls validate_password; goes straight to create_user)
apps/tenants/models.py:96-107 (set_password override bumps stamp; create_user bypasses it)
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/auth/signup/ accepts {email, password, display_name?, invite_code?}. Email and password are required (400 if missing). If PREVIEW_ACCESS_KEY is set, invite_code must match exactly (403 otherwise). Duplicate email returns 409. On success, creates User with username=email, hashes password, sets display_name; returns {access, refresh} JWT pair with 201. No Tenant is created here — onboarding is a separate step. Password is hashed by Django's create_user; password_last_changed_at is NOT set at signup (only set by set_password/set_unusable_password).

detail: PreferredModelView.patch() (apps/tenants/views.py:769-770) accesses request.user.tenant with no guard. Tenant.user is a OneToOneField with related_name="tenant" (models.py:142), so a user with no tenant raises User.tenant.RelatedObjectDoesNotExist (a subclass of Tenant.DoesNotExist / ObjectDoesNotExist) on attribute access. DRF's default exception handler does NOT convert ObjectDoesNotExist to a 404 (only Http404/PermissionDenied/APIException subclasses are handled), so it propagates as an unhandled HTTP 500. Every sibling view in the same file that touches request.user.tenant wraps it in try/except Tenant.DoesNotExist returning a 404 (ProvisioningStatusView 137-139, RetryProvisioningView 170-172, RefreshConfigView 268-270, AvailableModelsView 860-861, plus the EntityRegistry/PIIDenylist views); OnboardTenantView (line 58) even gates on hasattr(request.user, "tenant"), proving tenant-less authenticated users are an explicitly expected state (pre-onboarding, or after tenant hard-delete). So an authenticated pre-onboarding user, or one whose tenant was deleted, who sends PATCH /settings/preferred-model/ receives a 500 instead of a clean 404. The defect is genuine but the trigger is narrow: the settings UI is normally only reachable post-onboarding, so realistic triggers are a direct API caller, an iOS client racing the onboarding/provision flow, or the deleted-tenant edge case. The identical unguarded pattern also exists at TaskModelPreferencesView.patch() (line 899), confirming this is a consistency miss rather than intended behaviour.
suggested_fix: Wrap the tenant access in PreferredModelView.patch() in try/except Tenant.DoesNotExist, returning Response({"detail": "No tenant found."}, status=status.HTTP_404_NOT_FOUND), matching the established pattern in the rest of the file. Apply the same guard to TaskModelPreferencesView.patch() (line 899) for consistency.
evidence: apps/tenants/views.py:137-139, 170-172, 268-270, 860-861 (sibling views guard with try/except Tenant.DoesNotExist -> 404)
apps/tenants/views.py:58 (OnboardTenantView gates on hasattr(request.user, 'tenant'), confirming tenant-less users are an expected state)
apps/tenants/views.py:899 (TaskModelPreferencesView.patch has the same unguarded pattern)
expected_behaviour: PATCH /api/v1/tenants/settings/preferred-model/ requires IsAuthenticated. Body: {preferred_model: string or ''}. Validates model_id against _get_allowed_models(tenant) which combines tier base models + live free offer + BYO extras (400 if not in set). Empty string clears to tier default. Saves preferred_model, calls bump_pending_config(), calls _enqueue_immediate_apply() (publishes 'apply_single_tenant_config' QStash task with idempotency key, skipped if hibernated). Returns {preferred_model, model_tier}.

detail: Confirmed. TaskModelPreferencesView.patch (apps/tenants/views.py:899) and PreferredModelView.patch (line 770) access request.user.tenant with no try/except guard. Every other tenant-scoped view in the file (TenantViewSet, ProvisioningStatusView, RefreshConfigView, HeartbeatConfigView, AvailableModelsView at 858-865, EntityRegistry and PIIDenylist views) wraps the same access in try/except Tenant.DoesNotExist and returns a clean 404 ("No tenant found. Complete onboarding first."). An authenticated-but-tenant-less user is a real, reachable state: Tenant has a OneToOneField to User (models.py:142, related_name="tenant"), and OnboardTenantView (lines 49-58) is the step that creates the tenant, explicitly handling the no-tenant case via hasattr(request.user,"tenant") — so a registered user holding a valid JWT before completing onboarding has no tenant. For that user, request.user.tenant raises RelatedObjectDoesNotExist (a subclass of Tenant.DoesNotExist), which is unhandled, so DRF maps it to HTTP 500 instead of the intended 404. User impact is narrow: a pre-onboarding user (or any client hitting these endpoints outside the normal post-onboarding flow) on the task-model / preferred-model write path gets a 500 instead of a graceful 404. No data corruption, no cross-tenant leakage, no security exposure — purely an inconsistent error-handling/status-code defect on an edge state the rest of the module already handles. Note: the claim names TaskModelPreferencesView; PreferredModelView.patch shares the identical unguarded access.
suggested_fix: Wrap the tenant lookup in both patch methods in the same guard used everywhere else in the file: try: tenant = request.user.tenant / except Tenant.DoesNotExist: return Response({"detail": "No tenant found. Complete onboarding first."}, status=status.HTTP_404_NOT_FOUND). Apply to both PreferredModelView.patch (line 770) and TaskModelPreferencesView.patch (line 899) for consistency.
evidence: apps/tenants/views.py:858-865 (AvailableModelsView.get shows the standard try/except Tenant.DoesNotExist -> 404 pattern)
apps/tenants/views.py:49-71 (OnboardTenantView creates the tenant; handles no-tenant via hasattr -> tenant-less authenticated state is real)
apps/tenants/models.py:142 (Tenant.user = OneToOneField(User, related_name='tenant'); missing related object raises RelatedObjectDoesNotExist/Tenant.DoesNotExist)
expected_behaviour: PATCH /api/v1/tenants/settings/task-model-preferences/ requires IsAuthenticated. Body: {task_model_preferences: {slug: model_id, ...}}. Valid slugs: heartbeat, morning_briefing, evening_checkin, week_review, background_tasks (400 for others). model_id validated against allowed models (400 if not allowed). Empty string clears a slug. Merges into existing preferences. Saves, bumps pending_config, enqueues immediate apply. Returns {task_model_preferences}.

detail: Real asymmetry confirmed. has_entitlement (apps/tenants/models.py:721-727) is positive logic: True only if stripe_subscription_id is set OR a valid unexpired trial exists. entitled_active() (apps/tenants/models.py:729-742) is negative logic: it returns ALL ACTIVE tenants with a container and only excludes the narrow tuple (is_trial=True AND trial_ends_at<=now AND empty stripe_subscription_id). A ghost tenant (is_trial=False, status=ACTIVE, no stripe_subscription_id, not budget-exempt) has is_trial=False, so the exclude clause does not match and it IS included in the queryset, even though has_entitlement returns False for it. The sibling sweep _unentitled_active_tenants() (apps/cron/views.py:651-674) was deliberately hardened against exactly this ghost class (its docstring cites ~17 production ghosts accruing LLM cost since 2026-04-15 after is_trial was flipped to False without a SUSPENDED transition), but entitled_active() was never updated to mirror that fix. entitled_active() gates cron seeding (apps/cron/views.py:523), force-reseed-crons (594), and admin broadcast (1268), so a ghost keeps receiving config-apply and cron-seeding cycles until the once-daily expire_trials sweep (apps/cron/views.py:741) suspends it. Real but minor and self-correcting: bounded by the daily sweep cadence (max ~1 day of wasted cron seeds/config applies for an unentitled tenant). One correction to the claim's framing: ghosts do NOT arise at account creation (new tenants start is_trial=True per apps/tenants/views.py:90); they only arise from a later is_trial flip without status moving to SUSPENDED. Net effect is a sub-1-day wasted-cost/correctness leak, not a security or access defect.
suggested_fix: Make entitled_active() the exact positive inverse of _unentitled_active_tenants() so the two queries cannot diverge. Replace the narrow .exclude(is_trial=True, trial_ends_at__lte=now, stripe_subscription_id="") with: filter status=ACTIVE and container present, then keep only positively-entitled rows via Q(stripe_subscription_id__gt="") | Q(is_trial=True, trial_ends_at__gt=now) | Q(is_budget_exempt=True). Add a regression test asserting a non-trial active ghost with no subscription is excluded from entitled_active().
expected_behaviour: Tenant.status transitions are driven by Stripe webhooks in apps/billing/views.py (not exposed directly in tenants app). Status choices: pending, provisioning, active, suspended, deprovisioning, deleted. Active tenants must have status=active. Tenant.has_entitlement property returns True if stripe_subscription_id is set OR is_trial is True and trial_ends_at > now. Tenant.entitled_active() classmethod returns the filtered queryset used by crons and reports.

detail:
Confirmed against the code. set_unusable_password() is saved unconditionally at line 107, locking the user out immediately. The rotated counter is incremented only at line 116 after a successful email send; on email failure (lines 117-118) only email_failed is bumped. The operator summary headline "Rotated + emailed: {rotated}" (line 125) therefore EXCLUDES users who were rotated-but-not-emailed. The true locked-out count is rotated + email_failed, which is never shown as a single number. During a partial Mailgun outage, an operator who reads only the green success line will under-count how many users are locked out without a reset link.

This is real but minor, not a silent failure: the command logs each failure per-user with "rotated, locked out until manual resend" (lines 119-122) and prints a distinct red ERROR line "Email failed (rotated but no reset link sent): {email_failed}" (lines 128-134). All the information is present; the defect is only that the blast radius is split across two counters and the headline "Rotated + emailed" can be misread as the total locked-out figure. Hence low severity / ux category.
suggested_fix: Report the total locked-out count explicitly. Either keep the headline scoped (e.g. "Rotated + emailed (got reset link): {rotated}") and add a line "Total passwords rotated / locked out: {rotated + email_failed}", or increment a single rotated counter immediately after the save at line 107 and track email success/failure as a separate sub-metric, so the rotated total always reflects the real blast radius regardless of email outcome.
evidence: apps/tenants/management/commands/rotate_all_passwords.py:104-122 — unconditional set_unusable_password()+save at 107; rotated++ only after _send_reset_email at 116; email_failed++ at 118 with no rotated++

apps/tenants/management/commands/rotate_all_passwords.py:124-134 — summary headline 'Rotated + emailed: {rotated}' (125) omits email_failed; separate ERROR line at 128-134 does flag 'rotated but no reset link sent: {email_failed}'
expected_behaviour: rotate_all_passwords management command: iterates all users except PLATFORM_OWNER_EMAIL. Skips users with no email or whose password_last_changed_at >= --since (idempotency). For each user: calls set_unusable_password() (bumps password_last_changed_at → invalidates all JWTs), saves, generates reset token, sends email (privacy_rotation_2026 template). Reports rotated/skipped/failed counts. --dry-run mode available. rotate_all_passwords_task() wraps the command for QStash dispatch.

detail: Confirmed code-level inconsistency. RuntimePreferredModelView.post() gates the incoming model_id against views._get_allowed_models(), which merges offer_model_entry() (the limited-time free-offer model, e.g. nemotron free, when the promo is active) into the allowed set. But the GET response and every POST response body are built by _state() (runtime_views.py:206-221), which only merges TIER_MODEL_CONFIGS + _byo_model_extras() and does NOT call offer_model_entry(). Net effect during an active promo: the runtime can successfully POST the free-offer model (switch applies, config bumps), yet that model never appears in any allowed_models list returned by GET or POST. Impact is minimal/cosmetic: the switch itself succeeds with no error, and the free-offer model is the auto-selected default during the promo, so the user can always reach it. The only consequence is a discovery gap — an agent that enumerates GET's allowed_models to decide options wouldn't list a model that is actually settable (and already running). Manifests only while the promo is live and self-resolves when it ends. Matches the auditor's own inventory note ('Minor inconsistency'); the consumer-side PreferredModelView's parity gate correctly uses _get_allowed_models.
suggested_fix: Make _state() source allowed_models from the same authoritative helper as the validation path: replace the inline {**TIER_MODEL_CONFIGS.get(...), **_byo_model_extras(tenant)} dict in RuntimePreferredModelView._state() with a call to apps.tenants.views._get_allowed_models(tenant), so GET/POST responses and the POST gate share one source of truth and the free-offer model is listed whenever it is settable.
evidence: apps/tenants/runtime_views.py:206-221 (_state builds allowed_models from TIER_MODEL_CONFIGS + _byo_model_extras only, no offer_model_entry)
apps/tenants/runtime_views.py:249-272 (POST gates model_id on _get_allowed_models, then returns _state() body)
apps/tenants/views.py:793-805 (_get_allowed_models merges offer_model_entry() + tier + byo extras)
apps/billing/model_offers.py:80-86 (offer_model_entry returns the free-offer model only while promo active, else {})
expected_behaviour: GET /api/v1/tenants/runtime/<tenant_id>/preferred-model/ — internal auth required. Returns {preferred_model, applied_model, model_tier, allowed_models: [{model_id, alias}]}. POST /api/v1/tenants/runtime/<tenant_id>/preferred-model/ — internal auth required. Body: {model_id: string}. If model_id not in _get_allowed_models, returns 400 with allowed_models in body. Saves preferred_model, bumps pending_config, enqueues immediate apply. Returns {updated: True, ...state}. Empty model_id clears to tier default.

detail: Confirmed dead code with a real but minor user-facing consequence. apps/actions/tasks.py imports celery (from celery import shared_task) and decorates expire_stale_pending_actions with @shared_task, yet (1) Celery is NOT a dependency: it appears only inside comments in requirements.in ("replaces Celery") and the removed django_celery_beat note in config/settings/base.py:37; importing this module would raise ImportError. (2) The task is never invoked: the only reference to expire_stale_pending_actions in the repo is its own definition (verified via grep) -- no QStash endpoint, no apps/cron registration, no .delay() call. So the proactive sweep never runs. Impact is real but contained. Pending actions are still expired lazily in GatePollView (apps/actions/views.py:213) and GateRespondView (:285), but the lazy GatePollView path only flips DB status and does NOT call update_gate_message(). The only place expiry triggers a platform-message button refresh is the dead Celery task (tasks.py:41). update_gate_message IS called on real user responses (poller.py:1524/1545, line_webhook.py:1348/1365, GateRespondView via views.py:330) but never on timeout-expiry. Result: when an action expires by timeout (user ignores it, or the container hibernates and stops polling), the Approve/Deny buttons on the Telegram/LINE confirmation message stay visible indefinitely, and abandoned actions leave stale PENDING rows. There is NO security/data-integrity risk: tapping a stale button hits GateRespondView, which returns 410 GONE and refuses to apply the action (views.py:285-298). Worst case is UX confusion -- a tap on an old button silently does nothing and the message looks unresolved. Severity is low.
suggested_fix: Two parts. (1) Make the sweep run project-idiomatically: remove the Celery import/decorator (the project uses QStash, not Celery) and re-home the logic as a plain function invoked by a QStash cron, mirroring the no-body endpoint pattern in apps/cron. (2) More importantly, fix the button-staleness gap regardless of the sweep: have the lazy-expiry branch in GatePollView (and any timeout-expiry path) also call update_gate_message(action) so the platform Approve/Deny buttons are cleared/replaced when an action expires, exactly as the user-response paths already do. Alternatively, just delete tasks.py entirely and rely on lazy expiry plus a button refresh in GatePollView, which is the lowest-effort correct fix.
evidence: apps/actions/tasks.py:1-50 (celery import + @shared_task + only definition of expire_stale_pending_actions; update_gate_message only called here on expiry)
requirements.in:13 (celery referenced only in a comment; not an actual dependency in requirements.txt)
grep: expire_stale_pending_actions has no caller/cron/endpoint anywhere in repo
apps/actions/views.py:191-231 (GatePollView lazy-expiry flips status only, no update_gate_message call)
apps/actions/views.py:284-298 (GateRespondView returns 410 GONE on expired action -- no apply, so no data/security risk)
apps/actions/views.py:328-330, apps/router/poller.py:1524/1545, apps/router/line_webhook.py:1348/1365 (update_gate_message called on user responses, never on timeout expiry)
expected_behaviour: expire_stale_pending_actions() queries all PendingAction records with status=PENDING and expires_at < now. For each: sets status=EXPIRED, creates ActionAuditLog with result=expired, calls update_gate_message to edit/follow-up the platform message. Returns a summary string 'Expired N actions'. Errors in update_gate_message are caught and logged per action but do not abort the sweep. Intended to run every 60 seconds via Celery Beat or QStash cron.

detail: Both prongs are factually confirmed in the code but narrow and edge-gated. Prong A (hardcoded display name): _transition_message (model_health.py:257-278) hardcodes NEMOTRON_FREE_DISPLAY in all three notification templates while reading the fallback name data-drivenly via display_name_for_model() at line 258. FreeModelOffer.model_id is operator-configurable (CharField with default, models.py:209-213), so if an operator changed the offer model the notifications would still say 'Nemotron 3 Ultra (Free)'. However the entire offer system is single-model and hardcoded around Nemotron: model_offers.py:168 uses NEMOTRON_FREE_DISPLAY for the offer's own display_name regardless of model_id, the allowlist alias is 'nemotron' (model_offers.py:86), and REASONING_MODELS hardcodes Nemotron. Pointing the offer at a different model is an unsupported operation that would mislabel many surfaces, not just this message; impact is cosmetic. Prong B (SUSPENDED tenants not reset): _apply_to_tenants (model_health.py:221-225) filters status=ACTIVE, hibernated_at__isnull=True, so a SUSPENDED tenant whose preferred_model equals the offer model is never reset to '' on deactivation. Real gap but narrow: suspended tenants run no container/inference, the resolver only substitutes the offer model while the offer is active (model_offers.py:70-76), and OpenClaw's runWithModelFallback absorbs a stranded dead slug at turn time, so the only concrete failure is an explicit pick of a now-retired model surfacing at reactivation. Net: genuine latent inconsistencies, both low-impact; the code facts hold so this is not a false positive, but it is not something a normal user/operator experiences today since the offer is single-model (Nemotron) and suspended tenants do not infer.
suggested_fix: Prong A: replace the three NEMOTRON_FREE_DISPLAY literals in _transition_message with display_name_for_model(offer.model_id) (the helper is already imported and used for the fallback name on line 258) so notifications always name the actual offer model. Prong B: widen the _apply_to_tenants queryset (or add a separate deactivation pass) so that on deactivation any tenant whose preferred_model == offer model — including SUSPENDED/hibernated ones — is reset to '' so they are not stranded on a retired slug when they reactivate.
expected_behaviour: model_health_check() runs every 30 minutes via QStash. Fetches OpenRouter /models pricing for MONITORED_MODELS. Records pricing/free-status in ModelHealth rows. Sends a 1-token completion ping to the offer model. Transitions FreeModelOffer singleton: activates if offer_free=True and reachable; deactivates if no longer free (immediate) or unreachable for >= 3 consecutive failures (OFFER_FAILURE_THRESHOLD). On transition, bumps all affected tenants' config (tenants on rolling default or explicitly set to offer model), publishes apply_single_tenant_config task for each, and sends an in-channel system notification (capped at 500 tenants). Auto-disables the offer if the slug is absent from a successful /models read and has never activated (retired slug).

detail: Confirmed admin observability gap. The MonthlyBudget.is_capped boolean field (models.py:128) is referenced only in the model definition, a migration, and the admin's list_display + list_filter (admin.py:23-24). A repo-wide grep shows NO code path ever writes it — it stays at default=False forever. The cap_budget command caps service by setting spent_dollars = budget_dollars (cap_budget.py:55-56) and uncaps via spent_dollars = 0 (lines 50-51), never touching is_capped. The real enforcement (check_budget in services.py:288) routes to 'global' purely on remaining <= 0, and reconciliation (reconcile_openrouter_spend.py) only updates spent_dollars. Impact: an operator using the admin filter is_capped=Yes to find manually-capped months always gets zero results, and the is_capped column always renders No even on a capped month — the admin field is permanently misleading. This is purely an admin-facing display defect; billing enforcement and end-user gating are unaffected and work correctly via the spent/remaining path. Low severity: operator-only blast radius, and the admin already surfaces the truthful spent_dollars/budget_dollars columns alongside the broken flag.
suggested_fix: Make is_capped reflect reality or remove it. Simplest: drop the unused field plus the admin list_filter, and add a computed method (e.g. is_over_budget) to list_display so operators can still see capped months. Alternatively, have cap_budget (and reconcile_openrouter_spend) include is_capped in their update_fields alongside spent_dollars, and replace the admin boolean list_filter with a custom SimpleListFilter keyed on spent_dollars >= budget_dollars. The drop-the-field route avoids two sources of truth.
evidence: apps/billing/services.py:288 (real cap uses remaining <= 0, not is_capped)
grep -rn is_capped --include=*.py: only model def, migration 0003, and admin.py — no writer

detail: CoreComposeView.post (apps/core/views.py:217-243) does a non-atomic read-then-create: it queries for an in-flight MeditationSession (status PENDING/RENDERING) and, if none exists, calls MeditationSession.objects.create(...) then enqueues the compose_meditation task, with no transaction.atomic, no select_for_update, and no DB unique constraint. The model (apps/core/models.py:82-142) has only plain non-unique indexes on (tenant,date) and (tenant,status). Two requests landing in the SELECT-to-INSERT gap can both see no in-flight session, both create a PENDING row, and both enqueue a compose task. The downstream task guard (apps/core/tasks.py:42,69) only re-checks the single session it is handed, so it cannot dedup two distinct rows; each renders and each spends quota. The claimed defect is structurally accurate. However severity is LOW, not medium: the frontend (frontend/app/core/page.tsx:147-180) already guards effectively the orb is inert while phase is loading or composing, and compose() flips React state to composing synchronously BEFORE the network await, absorbing the ordinary single-user double-tap client-side. The remaining race requires genuinely concurrent requests (multiple tabs/devices, a proxy/QStash/client retry, or direct API calls) hitting the narrow window. Impact is a duplicated billable render (cost/quota), not data corruption or broken UX; both rows remain valid and pollable.
suggested_fix: Add a DB-level backstop plus an atomic guard. Add a partial unique constraint so at most one active session per tenant can exist, e.g. UniqueConstraint(fields=["tenant"], condition=Q(status__in=[PENDING, RENDERING]), name="uniq_active_meditation_per_tenant") via a migration, then wrap the in-flight check and create in transaction.atomic and catch IntegrityError to fall back to returning the existing in-flight session. This makes the coalescing guarantee race-proof at the database layer regardless of request timing.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/core/compose/ requires JWT auth; 404 if no tenant; 403 if tenant.core_enabled is false. Coalesces duplicate taps: if a session already exists with status PENDING or RENDERING, returns that session's id and status (200) without creating a new one. Otherwise creates a MeditationSession (status=PENDING, date=tenant_today(tenant)) and enqueues QStash task 'compose_meditation'. Returns {meditation_id, status} with 201 on creation. Enqueue failure is logged but non-fatal (the row is still created).

feature: Atomic fleet bump (OpenClaw version + config + image)
detail: Confirmed real, low-impact. The lock TTL is 30 min, commented "covers slowest fleet rollout" (views.py:1682), implying intent to hold it for the rollout. But it is released in the finally block (views.py:1813-1815) right after publish_batch returns (line 1797). publish_batch is a near-instant QStash fan-out enqueue, not the rollout itself; the per-tenant bumps run later in bump_openclaw_atomic_per_tenant_task (tasks.py:33-60), each ~30s across the fleet. So the lock is held for milliseconds, not the 30-min window. A second rollout_atomic_bump call arriving after the first HTTP response re-acquires the lock (cache.add succeeds) and re-runs the eligibility query, whose idempotency exclude (line 1779) reads DB state that is only written at task execution time. Tenants whose first task has not run yet are still "eligible" and get duplicate tasks enqueued. Backstop: the per-task guard at tasks.py:53-54 re-checks DB state at execution time, so if the first task already finished, the duplicate no-ops. A genuine double-write only occurs when both deliveries for one tenant execute before either writes DB state; even then bump_openclaw_version_for_tenant snapshots-before-write and restores-on-failure, so the repeated write is idempotent in effect (redundant container/config churn, no corruption). This is an operator/CI-only endpoint gated by X-Deploy-Secret (manual or workflow_dispatch), not user-facing, and a concurrent double-trigger is uncommon, hence low severity. The lock semantics do not match the documented 30-min intent, but the per-task idempotency backstop keeps real-world harm to redundant work.
suggested_fix: Either make the lock cover the full rollout (do not delete it in finally; let it expire via the 30-min TTL, or release only after tasks confirm), or accept the per-task idempotency as the real guard and remove the misleading "covers slowest fleet rollout" TTL comment, documenting that double-bumps are prevented at the task layer (tasks.py:53-54), not by the lock. The lowest-risk fix is the latter plus optionally narrowing the TTL to a short enqueue mutex.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/cron/rollout-atomic-bump/. Auth: X-Deploy-Secret. Rejects image_tag=latest (400). Concurrency-locked via Django cache key (409 if another bump in flight). Reads oc_version (default OPENCLAW_CURRENT_VERSION), image_tag (default OPENCLAW_IMAGE_TAG), optional tenant_id filter, dry_run flag. Skips tenants already at target version AND image_tag. Enqueues bump_openclaw_atomic_per_tenant QStash task per eligible tenant. Returns {queued, oc_version, image_tag, tenant_ids, dry_run}.

detail: Confirmed, but narrow. The frontend poll loop only reacts to status=failed while it is actively polling (page.tsx:127-132). At the 4-minute POLL_TIMEOUT_MS it sets pollRef.active=false, calls setLongRunning(true), and returns WITHOUT scheduling another tick or changing phase (page.tsx:134-139) — phase stays composing and the UI shows "Still composing…". After that the page never polls again (no visibilitychange/focus re-poll handler exists), and onOrb is a hard no-op while phase===composing (page.tsx:180), so the orb offers no retry. If the backend marks the session failed AFTER the 4-minute timeout (plausible: a late transient render/persist failure or a tts_quota failure in the multi-minute TTS pipeline — apps/core/services.py:215-237 and 245-281), the web user is stranded on "Still composing…" with no in-page error and no retry path until a manual reload. The gap is sharpened by the backend: notify_meditation_ready is sent ONLY on status=ready (services.py:307-309); the _fail() path (services.py:314-317) sends NO channel ping on ANY channel. So the inventory-note mitigation ("backend sends a channel notification when rendering completes") holds only for the SUCCESS case — on failure-after-timeout there is zero out-of-band signal and the "Still composing…" copy actively misleads. For success-after-timeout the design degrades gracefully (channel ping + a reload surfaces the ready sit), so this is a low-frequency edge, not a broad break. The QA's "app-only user" emphasis is slightly off (this is the web page; web users get the toast/reload path on success), but the core claim — stranded composing screen, no in-page error/retry, and no failure notification on any channel — is verified by the code.
suggested_fix: In pollUntilDone's timeout branch, don't stop watching entirely: keep a slow background poll alive (e.g. every ~30s, perhaps capped) so a post-timeout status=failed still flips phase to failed (surfacing the in-page error + orb retry) and a late status=ready still resolves the toast/library. Cheaper alternative: in the composing/longRunning UI, render a visible "Try again" / "Refresh" affordance, or make onOrb re-enter compose() once longRunning is true. Backend defense-in-depth: have the failed path also send a short channel ping (mirroring notify_meditation_ready) so a user who stepped away is told it failed rather than left waiting.

expected_behaviour: pollUntilDone(id) polls GET /api/v1/core/sessions/{id}/ every 3500ms. If session.status is 'ready' or 'delivered', the session is optimistically prepended to state, phase transitions to 'ready', showToast is set to true, and loadLibrary() is called to reconcile. If session.status is 'failed', phase transitions to 'failed' with the message 'Your meditation couldn't be composed just now. Please try again in a moment.' Network errors during polling are swallowed and polling continues. After 4 minutes total, polling stops and longRunning is set to true. The pollRef allows the loop to be cancelled on unmount.

feature: Today's Check-In Form (Weight, Sleep, Resting HR)
detail: Confirmed in part, with a correction to the claim's framing. handleSubmit fires all dirty field saves in parallel via Promise.allSettled then unconditionally calls setForceEdit(false) (today-checkin.tsx:192-193). The fuel mutations (useCreateBodyWeightMutation, useUpdateBodyWeightMutation, sleep, HR in lib/queries.ts:1415-1444) do NOT do optimistic cache writes; they only invalidateQueries on success, and they do NOT set meta.skipErrorToast, so each failed save falls through to the global mutation onError in providers.tsx:30-35 and emits a 3s toast. getErrorMessage can surface the backend detail/message so it is not strictly generic, but it never names which field failed. The genuine UX gap is the partial-failure-on-edit case: when both weight and sleep already exist for today (allLogged=true) and the user edits both, if one PATCH fails the cache keeps the stale value (no optimistic write) and the card collapses to its one-liner summary (collapsed = not forceEdit and allLogged and isToday, line 52), showing the updated field next to the stale one, signalled only by a transient toast. The claim's stronger wording (collapses unconditionally, split state with no indication) overstates the common case: collapse is gated by allLogged, so a FAILED first-time create leaves todayBW/todaySleep undefined, allLogged stays false, and the form does NOT collapse; the failed field then visibly lacks its Logged badge and stays editable and retryable. No data is lost in any case (server is source of truth and the cache reflects it). Net: a real but narrow medium-low UX issue (no per-field error state, and a partial edit-failure can collapse to a stale-looking summary signalled only by a transient toast), not a data-integrity bug.
suggested_fix: In handleSubmit, capture the Promise.allSettled results and only call setForceEdit(false) when every result is fulfilled; if any are rejected, keep the form expanded so the user can see and retry. Better: give each field mutation an onError that surfaces a per-field inline error (the FieldShell already has a slot for badges), or pass meta linking the toast to the field name, so a partial failure marks the specific field rather than relying on a single transient toast.
evidence: frontend/lib/queries.ts:1415-1444 (BW mutations: invalidate-on-success only, no optimistic write, no skipErrorToast)
frontend/app/providers.tsx:25-36 (global mutation onError emits toast unless meta.skipErrorToast)
frontend/lib/errors.ts:33-55 (getErrorMessage builds the toast text, no field identity)

detail: Confirmed real. summaryChips() in the Fuel history list hardcodes kilometers for cardio rows and never reads the user's distance_unit preference. At history.tsx:113-114 it renders `${d.distance_km} km` and `${d.pace}/km` directly. distance_unit is a genuine, established preference (FuelProfile.distance_unit, type DistanceUnit = "km"|"mi") with a useDistanceUnit() hook and kmToDisplay() helper in use-distance-unit.ts. Both sibling components convert correctly: workout-detail-readonly.tsx CardioStatsReadOnly (lines 289-306) uses kmToDisplay(storedKm, unit) for distance and labels pace as /unit, and workout-detail.tsx (lines 1147-1187) does the same on the edit path. history.tsx imports none of these helpers (only useMemo/useState, queries, skeleton, category-meta). User impact for a mi-preference user, on every cardio history row: distance shows the raw stored km value labeled "km" (e.g. "5.0 km") instead of the converted "3.1 mi" -- both value and label wrong; pace label shows "/km" instead of "/mi". Severity is low: it is a summary chip, the canonical value is stored in km, and tapping into the workout shows correct units -- but it is a real, visible unit inconsistency for mi users. Note: pace is stored as a raw string and even the detail views only swap the pace label without numerically converting it, so the history pace defect is label-only, consistent with the rest of the app.
suggested_fix: In history.tsx call useDistanceUnit() inside WorkoutRow (or pass unit into summaryChips), and in the cardio branch render distance via kmToDisplay(d.distance_km, unit) with the unit string as the label and pace with /unit, mirroring CardioStatsReadOnly in workout-detail-readonly.tsx. Since summaryChips is a plain function, give it a unit parameter or move the cardio chip rendering into the component where the hook is available.
expected_behaviour: Fetches GET /api/v1/fuel/workouts/?status=done&limit=200. During load shows a skeleton of 7 WorkoutRow skeletons. Once loaded, renders category filter chips at the top (ALL + each category that has at least 1 done session). Each chip shows a colored dot and count badge. Active filter chip has category-accent background. Filtering is client-side (no re-fetch). Filtered list renders as WorkoutRow buttons; clicking opens WorkoutDetail. Empty state: 'No workouts logged yet.' when filtered list is empty (or when total is 0).

feature: Weight Unit Toggle (Strength Stats & Body Weight)
detail: Real but minor UX gap. The weight-unit preference is device-local (localStorage key fuel:weight-unit) across ALL consumers: BodyWeight, StrengthEditor (workout-detail.tsx:947), progress.tsx:187, today-checkin.tsx:33, and workout-detail-readonly.tsx:125 all call the same useWeightUnit() hook. FuelProfile (types.ts:855-867) has NO weight_unit field anywhere in frontend or backend (grep returned zero hits), so the spec's expected_behaviour ('In StrengthEditor the unit is read from the profile') is NOT implemented — that part of the claim is a spec/doc inaccuracy, not a runtime defect. The substantive, genuine issue: unlike distance_unit (which IS server-persisted via use-distance-unit.ts reading profile.distance_unit and writing back through updateMutation), the weight unit has no per-account server-side preference. Runtime behavior is actually consistent and not broken: BodyWeight's toggle and StrengthEditor stay in sync via the shared localStorage value plus a 'fuel-unit-change' custom event, and weights are always stored as kg with conversion only at the display/commit boundary (kgToDisplay/displayToKg), so there is no data loss or display corruption. The only user-facing consequence is that the preference does not follow the account: a different browser/device, or clearing localStorage, silently resets to kg with no warning or sync. A user would only notice this on device switch or storage clear — a low-impact inconsistency relative to distance unit, not a functional bug.
suggested_fix: Treat weight unit like distance unit for cross-device consistency: add a weight_unit field to the FuelProfile model/serializer (backend migration + TypeScript type), then add a use-weight-unit variant that reads profile.weight_unit and writes back via the profile update mutation (mirroring use-distance-unit.ts), keeping localStorage only as an instant-load fallback. Alternatively, accept the device-local design and correct the feature spec's expected_behaviour, since the live sync behavior is otherwise sound.
evidence: grep weight_unit across frontend/ apps/ → zero hits
expected_behaviour: A kg/lbs UnitToggle appears in the BodyWeight component header and is also used in StrengthEditor for weight fields. Selecting a unit in BodyWeight updates the display immediately (local state via useWeightUnit hook). In StrengthEditor the unit is read from the profile. Weight values are always stored as kg; conversion happens at the display/commit boundary. The toggle in BodyWeight is a styled button group; changing unit in the strength editor requires accessing the profile preference.

detail: Confirmed. In handleSubmit (templates/page.tsx:110-136), when payload.slug, payload.name, or payload.sections.length is falsy, the function returns at line 126 without setting any error/validation state. The Name and Slug inputs (lines 179-201) and section slug/title inputs (lines 222-241) carry NO required attribute, so native browser validation does not fire either. The only error UI (lines 293-302) is gated on createMutation.isError / updateMutation.isError, which can only become true after a mutation actually runs — never reached on this early return. Result: a user who fills in sections but forgets the Name (or clears the Slug) clicks Create template, the button immediately re-enables (no pending state since no mutation fires), and nothing visibly happens — no message indicating what is missing. The feature's own audit inventory_notes corroborates this. User impact is mild: the form is seeded with 5 default sections and a normalized slug placeholder, so a fully-empty submit is uncommon; the realistic trigger is a forgotten/blank Name field, which leaves the user with a dead button and no guidance.
suggested_fix: Add lightweight client validation feedback in handleSubmit before the early return: set a local error-string state describing which field is missing (name/slug/at least one valid section) and render it in a rose panel near the submit button, clearing it on successful submit and on field edits. Optionally also add the required attribute to the Name and Slug inputs for native validation. The local-error approach is preferred since section completeness cannot be expressed via required.

'+ Add section' appends an empty section. On submit, if slug, name, and at least one section with both slug+title are present, createMutation.mutateAsync (POST /api/v1/journal/templates/) is called. While pending, submit shows 'Creating...'. On success editingId is set to null. On error, the error message is shown in a rose panel below the form. Empty sections (missing slug or title) are silently filtered out on submit.

feature: Scheduled Tasks — edit a cron job (desktop inline form)
detail: Confirmed real but mild. In handleUpdate the success path runs setEditingName(null) (L396) immediately before setSuccess(setEditFeedback) (L397). Both run in the same async continuation after the awaited mutateAsync, and under React 19 automatic batching collapses them into one re-render where editingName is already null. The success UI (the Saved-checkmark text branch at L1101-1102 and success button styling via getSaveButtonClass at L1097) exists only inside the inline form, conditionally mounted on editingName === jobIdentifier (L1028). The mobile portal (L936) is gated on the same condition and has no success affordance at all (its button shows only ... / Save at L948). So in the render where status becomes success, the host form is already unmounted and the success flash can never appear. The create path has the identical structure (setShowCreate(false) then setSuccess, L358-359), so its success flash at L680 is also dead code, confirming a consistent pattern. Impact is real but low: on success the form collapses and the list re-renders with the updated job via query invalidation, an implicit success signal; the save works and errors DO surface (the error branch at L1020/L1117 renders because the error path leaves the form mounted). So this is a missing positive-confirmation affordance, not a true silent failure; the feels-like-silent-failure framing is overstated.
suggested_fix: Defer the unmount so the success state is visible: call setSuccess first, then close the form on a short timer (e.g. fold setEditingName(null) into the SAVE_CLEAR_DELAY_MS timeout, or close after a brief delay). Alternatively show a toast on success since the inline form unmounts anyway. Apply the same fix to the create path (L358-359) for consistency.
expected_behaviour: Clicking "Edit" on a job row sets `editingName` and renders an inline form with TimezoneSelector, ScheduleBuilder, Delivery select, SessionModeSelector, PromptEditor, and CapabilityChips. The form is pre-populated from `toEditForm(job)` (stripping the date prefix from the message). Submitting calls `PATCH /api/v1/cron/jobs/<name>/` via `useUpdateCronJobMutation`. On success `✓ Saved` flashes green for 2 s. On error the button turns red for 4 s.

feature: Scheduled Tasks — edit a cron job (mobile full-screen overlay)
detail: Confirmed. In handleUpdate, on success setEditingName(null) (line 396) makes editingName === jobIdentifier false, so the entire edit UI unmounts immediately. Line 397's setSuccess() then writes status:success into editFeedback, but on mobile nothing is left to surface it: the mobile portal Save button (lines 942-948) only renders "..." while pending or "Save" otherwise (no success state), and the portal's only feedback paragraph (lines 1020-1022) renders error text only. The 2s success window (SAVE_CLEAR_DELAY_MS, lines 180/310) elapses against an unmounted tree. Net mobile impact: after tapping Save the full-screen overlay simply disappears with no explicit success confirmation; the only implicit cue is the overlay closing plus the refetched list. Low severity because the save genuinely succeeds and the closing overlay is a weak implicit signal. Nuance the QA verdict missed: the desktop inline form DOES contain the "Saved" success markup (lines 1099-1103), but it is effectively dead code for the same reason (setEditingName(null) unmounts the form before that branch can render). So the tear-down-before-feedback bug is not strictly mobile-only; only the missing-markup observation is specific to the mobile portal. The core claim stands.
suggested_fix: Decouple the success acknowledgement from the editing-row teardown. Either (a) defer setEditingName(null) until after the success window so the form/overlay stays mounted long enough to show feedback, or (b) render a transient success toast/banner OUTSIDE the per-row editingName conditional so it survives the overlay closing, and add a "Saved" success state to the mobile portal Save button (lines 942-948) mirroring the desktop button at lines 1099-1103. Option (b) plus a brief close delay gives clear confirmation on both layouts.

feature: Authenticated app shell — sticky header with scroll border
detail:
The finding bundles two claims; one is real, the other is largely a misread.

REAL - nested anchor elements: In the authenticated shell the brand logo is rendered as a Link to /journal (app-shell.tsx:354-359) wrapping BrandLogo, but BrandLogo itself unconditionally renders its own Link to / (brand-logo.tsx:17). There is no prop to suppress that inner link (only showText exists). The result is an anchor (href=/journal) containing another anchor (href=/), which is invalid HTML. During parsing, browsers auto-close the outer anchor before the inner one, so the inner href=/ wins: clicking the logo navigates to / (the full-bleed landing/galaxy), NOT to /journal as the FA-0494 expected_behaviour requires. It is also an accessibility defect (ambiguous nesting, inconsistent keyboard activation). Impact is modest since the full nav bar offers other routes and / resolves sensibly for logged-in users, hence low severity. The public-page usage at app-shell.tsx:325 is NOT wrapped and is fine.

NOT A DEFECT - headerBorder reset: The claim that the border/shadow stays stuck on the next page is largely a misread. The scroll listener binds to #main-content, the overflow-y-auto scroll container that persists across client navigations (app-shell.tsx:406-408). On Next.js App Router navigation the framework resets this scroll container to top; that programmatic scroll fires a scroll event, which runs handleScroll then setHeaderBorder(main.scrollTop greater than 8) = false. So the border is recomputed to false on navigation in practice. The spec's own wording (border resets because the listener is re-attached) describes the wrong mechanism, but the user-visible outcome is correct. Adding an explicit setHeaderBorder(false) would only make it deterministic; its absence is not a real user-facing bug.
suggested_fix: Fix the nested anchor (the load-bearing part). Give BrandLogo and BrandIcon an optional prop such as asLink (default true) that, when false, renders the logo as a plain div/span instead of a Link. Then in app-shell.tsx:354-359 pass asLink={false} so the only anchor is the outer Link to /journal. This restores the spec'd /journal navigation, makes the logo keyboard-navigable, and produces valid HTML. Public-page usage (line 325) keeps the default link to /. Optionally, for robustness, add setHeaderBorder(false) at the top of the scroll useEffect (line 267) so the header border is reset deterministically rather than relying on Next's scroll-to-top firing a scroll event.

detail: Both sub-claims confirmed in code; impact is latent (no production consumer or caller). (1) Count mismatch: create_monthly_snapshots increments created_count after every _create_snapshot_for_tenant call that does not raise. The helper returns None without raising for the duplicate-skip (snapshot.py:46-47) and the no-accounts skip (snapshot.py:49-51), so the count reflects tenants-attempted, not snapshots-created, contradicting the docstring at snapshot.py:23 ('Returns the number of snapshots created'). Tests codify the mismatch: test_idempotent_no_duplicate_snapshots asserts count==1 on a no-op re-run (tests.py:1437-1438); test_skips_tenants_without_accounts asserts count==1 with a comment 'counts tenants attempted' while FinanceSnapshot.objects.count()==0 (tests.py:1447-1450). (2) Not scheduled: the only callers are in apps/finance/tests.py; there is no TASK_MAP entry in apps/cron/views.py (verified the full map, lines 70-188 — it has schedule_finance_welcome and snapshot_gravity_weekly but nothing for a monthly finance snapshot), and apps/finance/tasks.py contains only schedule_finance_welcome_task. So the function never runs outside tests and no snapshots are produced in production. WHY LOW/LATENT: the inflated count has no production consumer (only tests read it); the snapshot-creation logic itself is correct and idempotent; the never-scheduled point makes the whole feature dormant rather than malfunctioning, and the feature record's own inventory_notes already flag the caller as unconfirmed and describe the function as 'designed to be called', so the wiring is acknowledged as not-yet-done rather than a regression. No user or operator experiences a misbehavior today — this is a docstring-vs-behavior inaccuracy plus a not-yet-wired feature.
suggested_fix: Two small fixes. (a) Make the count truthful: capture the return of _create_snapshot_for_tenant and do created_count += 1 only when it returns a non-None FinanceSnapshot; update the two tests that currently assert attempted-count semantics. (b) Wire scheduling: add a monthly_finance_snapshot entry to TASK_MAP (apps/cron/views.py) pointing at a new apps.finance.tasks task that calls create_monthly_snapshots, and register a QStash monthly cron (1st of month) — or expose it as a management command if scheduled delivery is intentionally deferred.

apps/finance/snapshot.py:44-51 (_create_snapshot_for_tenant returns None on duplicate-skip and no-accounts-skip without raising)
apps/finance/tests.py:1437-1438 and 1447-1450 (tests assert count==1 for no-op / no-accounts runs)

expected_behaviour: apps/finance/snapshot.py:create_monthly_snapshots is designed to be called on the 1st of each month (via QStash or a management command). Queries all Tenants where finance_enabled=True and status=ACTIVE. For each tenant: skips if a FinanceSnapshot with that date already exists (idempotent). Loads active accounts, computes total_debt, total_savings, and total_payments_this_month (sum of payment transactions in the previous calendar month).
