detail: Part 2 (the TOCTOU race) is real; part 1 (404 vs 400) is a cosmetic spec-wording mismatch only. _make_unique_slug (topic_resolver.py:38-44) does .filter(pillar,slug).exists() then resolve_topic .create()s (lines 78-85) against unique_together=(pillar,slug) (models.py:94). No IntegrityError handling or get_or_create exists in the record path. @transaction.atomic gives rollback, not isolation, so under READ COMMITTED two concurrent transactions creating the same new (pillar,slug) both pass .exists() and both .create(); the loser raises an unhandled IntegrityError. TopicRegistry is GLOBAL (keyed pillar+slug, not tenant), so different tenants' OpenClaw containers proposing the same brand-new topic simultaneously can collide. On the direct record paths (_record_insight_impl views.py:355 via runtime_views.py:331 and RecordInsightView) this surfaces as a 500. Low impact: trigger requires two near-simultaneous creates of an identical NOT-yet-seeded topic string; common topics are pre-seeded (migration 0002) so most calls early-return on the canonical/alias branches and never reach .create(); the loser loses one insight write and gets one retryable 500; and the parallel marker path (markers.py:80-94) already swallows the exception. Re part 1: _validate_record_body (runtime_views.py:88-92) returns 404 (pillar_not_available) for a bad pillar where the FA-0607 spec says 400; this is deliberate, consistent across 6+ sites, returns a structured error, never crashes, and only the internal runtime caller sees it.
suggested_fix: Make topic creation race-safe instead of check-then-create: wrap resolve_topic's final TopicRegistry.objects.create in a nested transaction.atomic() with try/except IntegrityError, and on conflict re-query for the existing (pillar,slug) row and return it (re-running _make_unique_slug if the base slug now exists). Alternatively fold into a get_or_create on (pillar,slug). Optionally align the pillar error to 400 to match the spec, or update the spec to document the intentional 404.
expected_behaviour: POST /api/v1/insights/runtime/<tenant_uuid>/insights/record/ authenticates via internal key headers. Requires pillar (in ALLOWED_PILLARS), topic, statement; optionally evidence_refs, confidence (0.0-1.0), model_version. Calls resolve_topic to canonicalize the topic string. Creates AssistantInsight with status=open. Returns 201 with serialized insight. Missing/invalid fields return 400.

detail: Confirmed as a real but low-severity robustness gap. resolve_topic (apps/insights/topic_resolver.py:47-85) is decorated @transaction.atomic and uses a read-then-create pattern: _make_unique_slug (lines 38-44) runs an existence-check SELECT loop, then TopicRegistry.objects.create (line 78) inserts, with no IntegrityError handling. The model enforces unique_together(pillar, slug) (apps/insights/models.py:94). Two requests for the SAME tenant both supplying the same novel slug can both observe the slug as absent and both attempt the INSERT; the second raises IntegrityError. The markers path catches this (apps/insights/markers.py:80-94 wraps resolve_topic + create in try/except Exception with logging), but _record_insight_impl (views.py:355, called unguarded from RecordInsightView.post at views.py:433) and _upsert_voice_pref_impl (views.py:638, called unguarded from VoicePrefSetView.post at views.py:669) have no outer catch, so a collision surfaces as an unhandled 500. User impact: the per-tenant OpenClaw assistant (sole caller) gets a transient 500 on the colliding record/voice-pref call; a retry succeeds because the slug then resolves. Real-world probability is very low: deployment is single-tenant-per-container and OpenClaw processes turns serially per tenant (concurrent-turn rejection), so two simultaneous identical-novel-slug requests require an edge case such as a retry overlapping the original or a cron-driven extraction turn overlapping an interactive turn. Still a genuine gap: the guarded-vs-unguarded asymmetry across the three call sites shows it is an oversight, not intended behaviour.
suggested_fix: Make resolve_topic self-healing rather than relying on each caller to guard. Wrap the final TopicRegistry.objects.create in a nested transaction.atomic() block and catch IntegrityError; on collision re-run the slug/alias resolution (re-SELECT) and return the now-existing row. This centralizes the fix so all three call sites (markers, record-insight, voice-pref) are protected uniformly. Alternatively, use get_or_create on (pillar, slug) for the proposed-row insert, though the _make_unique_slug suffixing makes the explicit catch-and-retry cleaner.
evidence: apps/insights/topic_resolver.py:47-85 (@transaction.atomic, no IntegrityError handling, create at 78)
apps/insights/markers.py:80-94 (markers path DOES wrap in try/except Exception)
expected_behaviour: TopicRegistry rows are seeded on every deploy via migration 0002 which calls seed_topics() — idempotent get_or_create. Gravity topics include income, fixed_expenses, subscriptions, discretionary, dining, debt, savings, large_purchases with seeded aliases. Fuel topics include sleep_quantity, sleep_quality, hydration, meals, energy_level, exercise, alcohol, caffeine. When the assistant uses an unknown slug, resolve_topic creates a proposed row (status=proposed, source=proposed_by_model). Operators promote proposed topics to canonical or merge them via aliases in Django Admin (TopicRegistryAdmin, TopicAliasAdmin). TopicAlias resolution is case/whitespace-insensitive via __iexact on the normalized candidate.

feature: Lesson rewrite to actionable — management command
detail: Confirmed real. After a lesson text is rewritten and saved, process_approved_lesson (services.py:99-103) regenerates the embedding and calls create_connections (services.py:79-96), which ONLY adds edges via LessonConnection.objects.get_or_create. No code path deletes the lesson pre-existing LessonConnection rows on a text/embedding change; deletions happen only via the model CASCADE on lesson deletion. The audit record inventory_notes even claim rewriting rebuilds connections from scratch, but the code strictly accretes, so edges that were only similar under the OLD embedding survive forever. Impact: the constellation/galaxy views (views.py:143-206, 210-277) return every stored LessonConnection as a graph edge AND suppress the recomputed affinity edge for any pair that already has a stored edge (strong_pairs/stored set at views.py:159-162, 237-240). A stale stored edge therefore draws a spurious link and hides the correct affinity edge for that pair. Secondary: get_or_create with similarity only in defaults never updates similarity on still-valid edges, so weights also drift stale. Severity low: only reachable via the operator-run one-time rewrite_lessons_actionable command (plus reseed/backfill tools); no user-facing lesson-text-edit endpoint re-runs process_approved_lesson, so it is not hit in normal product use; impact is cosmetic graph pollution, not data loss; star-stage promotion (growth.py:33 counts connections_out) is promote-only so stale counts cannot cause a visible demotion.
suggested_fix: In create_connections (or in process_approved_lesson before re-linking), first delete the lesson existing auto-generated edges in both directions, then recreate from the fresh embedding. e.g. LessonConnection.objects.filter(Q(from_lesson=lesson)|Q(to_lesson=lesson), connection_type='similar').delete() before the get_or_create loop, scoped to connection_type='similar' so user_linked/builds_on/contradicts edges are preserved. Alternatively use update_or_create instead of get_or_create so similarity is refreshed for surviving edges. Wrap in a transaction.
evidence: apps/lessons/services.py:79-103 (create_connections + process_approved_lesson: get_or_create only, no deletion)
apps/lessons/models.py:104-129 (LessonConnection: CASCADE only on lesson delete)
apps/lessons/views.py:143-206 (constellation: stored edges + affinity suppression of stored pairs)
apps/lessons/views.py:210-277 (galaxy: same stored-edge return + affinity suppression)
apps/lessons/growth.py:26-44 (connections_out count feeds promote-only star stage)
expected_behaviour: python manage.py rewrite_lessons_actionable [--dry-run] [--tenant UUID] — Queries all approved lessons (or per-tenant). For each: calls OpenRouter (claude-sonnet-4.6, temp=0.2) with the rewrite prompt. If rewritten text differs: saves updated lesson.text, calls process_approved_lesson (regenerates embedding, creates new connections). Re-clusters all affected tenants. Dry-run prints before/after without saving. Skips lessons where LLM returns unchanged text.

feature: LINE postback — generic button forward to agent
detail: Real but narrow. The generic postback fallback in _handle_postback (line_webhook.py:1311) forwards arbitrary tapped-button data straight to _forward_to_container, which only blocks PROVISIONING status (line 1214). It skips the SUSPENDED check, the synchronous check_budget call, and the hibernation/wake routing that the typed-message path (_handle_message, lines 1020-1067) enforces. The agent can emit arbitrary generic buttons via [[button:label|data]] markers (line_flex.py:815-839); any data not matching the tz_/gate_/extract:/task_action:/lesson: prefixes hits this fallback. The same ungated pattern exists on the Telegram poller (poller.py:831-836, with its gates only after, at 855-874). However, real impact is heavily mitigated, so the 'trigger AI turns without paying' framing is overstated: (1) Billing-suspended tenants have their container HIBERNATED (billing/services.py:556) with hibernated_at cleared to None (line 546), so the forwarded postback 404s, drops, and does NOT re-wake -- no turn, no spend. (2) Over-budget tenants already hibernated have hibernated_at set (views.py:135), so the drain hits the 404 branch (pending_queue.py:739) where check_budget re-fires and drops without waking. The ONLY genuinely exploitable window is an over-budget tenant whose container is still LIVE because _hibernate_for_quota has not fired yet: a typed message would run check_budget and hibernate/block, but a tapped generic button skips it and lands one billable turn. Even then the cost is debited via record_usage overage and the 402 breaker trips on the next call -- one leaked turn in a narrow race, not unlimited free usage.
suggested_fix: Extract the suspension + budget + hibernation pre-flight gate from _handle_message into a shared helper and invoke it at the top of the generic-postback branch in _handle_postback (before line_webhook.py:1311), returning early on SUSPENDED/over-budget. Mirror the same fix on the Telegram poller generic button-tap path (poller.py:831-836). The special-prefix branches (gate_/extract:/task_action:/lesson:/tz_) act on stored DB state rather than spawning chat turns, so they can stay ungated.
evidence: apps/router/poller.py:822-874 (Telegram generic button forward at 831-836 precedes gates at 855-874)
apps/billing/services.py:545-558 (suspension hibernates container, sets hibernated_at=None at 546)
apps/router/pending_queue.py:739-746 (drain re-checks budget only on hibernated 404 path)
expected_behaviour: Handles any postback event not matched by tz_/gate_/extract_/task_action_/lesson_ prefixes. Resolves tenant. Calls _forward_to_container with the synthetic text '[User tapped button: "<data>"]'. This goes through the full pipeline: enqueued in PendingMessage, drained to OpenClaw, reply delivered via LINE Push. The postback data (callback_data) is used as the button identifier.

feature: LINE onboarding flow for new subscribers
detail: Confirmed. In _handle_message, the audio branch (lines 913-945) runs unconditionally and early: it shows a loading indicator (924), makes the paid OpenAI Whisper API call via _transcribe_line_audio (925 — downloads audio + POSTs to whisper-1, typically 5-10s), then re-shows loading (933). Only later, at the onboarding gate (lines 1092-1099), does the code reject any non-text message during onboarding with "please type your answer." So a user still in onboarding who sends a voice message experiences: loading -> ~5-10s wait through full transcription -> loading again -> a rejection telling them voice isn't supported yet. User impact: a confusing >10s loading animation before an outright rejection, plus wasted Whisper API cost on a transcript that is discarded. The expected_behaviour documents the rejection text but not this wasteful ordering. Severity is low: it only affects voice-during-onboarding (a narrow window — first interaction or after re-introduction), still produces a correct rejection, and has no data/security impact.
suggested_fix: Reject non-text messages during onboarding before incurring the Whisper call. The onboarding check needs the resolved tenant (line 996), which currently sits after the audio branch, so restructure: for audio messages, resolve the tenant first (cheap DB lookup) and if (not tenant.onboarding_complete or tenant.onboarding_step == 0), send the "please type your answer" bubble and return immediately — skipping both loading indicators and the Whisper API call. Alternatively, defer transcription so it only fires after the tenant/onboarding gates pass.
evidence: apps/router/line_webhook.py:912-945 (early unconditional audio branch: _show_loading 924, _transcribe_line_audio 925, re-show loading 933)
apps/router/line_webhook.py:99-162 (_transcribe_line_audio makes a real paid OpenAI Whisper API call to whisper-1)
apps/router/line_webhook.py:996 (tenant resolved only after the audio branch)
apps/router/line_webhook.py:1092-1099 (onboarding gate rejects msg_type != 'text' AFTER transcription already ran)
expected_behaviour: For tenants where not onboarding_complete or onboarding_step == 0, _handle_message routes through onboarding. Non-text messages (stickers, voice) during onboarding are rejected with a short bubble 'please type your answer'. Text messages are passed to get_onboarding_response(tenant, text, telegram_lang='') — LINE always passes empty string for language since LINE webhooks don't include a language_code. Onboarding replies rendered via _send_onboarding_reply: builds short_bubble Flex, attaches Quick Reply buttons by converting the Telegram keyboard format via telegram_keyboard_to_quick_reply. Country and timezone selection uses postback events (tz_country:*, tz_zone:*) handled by _handle_onboarding_postback → handle_onboarding_callback. needs_reintroduction triggers reset of onboarding_step to 0 for users with stale onboarding data.

detail: Confirmed for the Telegram channel. Every Telegram reply in production flows poller._forward_to_container (poller.py:1307-1380) -> enqueue_message_for_tenant -> queue drain task -> relay_ai_response_to_telegram (pending_queue.py:1543-1644). That relay handles PII rehydration, insight markers, chart rendering, and MEDIA image sending, but does NOT parse [[button:Label|callback_data]] markers; the comment at pending_queue.py:1615-1619 explicitly says 'no inline button parsing yet'. The poller's _send_rich_response (poller.py:317-431), which DOES parse buttons (lines 404-413, building agent:-prefixed callback_data + reply_markup), is dead in production: it is referenced only from tests (test_proxy_phase3.py:62-85). Git history confirms it was the pre-queue synchronous send path (commit bef0156 'Phase 3 proxy improvements - inline buttons'), orphaned when the per-tenant queue landed (commit e89e08a) and forwarding moved fully into the queue. As a result the agent: callback handler at poller.py:819-837 is unreachable on Telegram because no button is ever rendered to tap. User impact: when the agent emits a button marker on Telegram, the user sees literal [[button:Label|data]] text instead of a tappable inline keyboard, and the callback flow is unusable. Severity low (not medium): button markers are a LINE-spec feature (docs/line-phase2-spec.md:104) and the LINE drain path DOES honor them (line_webhook.py:714 extract_quick_reply_buttons); no system-prompt guidance promotes [[button:]] for Telegram, so the agent rarely emits them, limiting frequency. When it does, it is a genuine leaked-marker + dead-callback UX defect on Telegram.
suggested_fix: Port the button-parsing block from poller._send_rich_response (poller.py:404-413) into relay_ai_response_to_telegram in pending_queue.py before the final _send_telegram_markdown at line 1644: regex-extract [[button:Label|data]], strip the markers from text, build inline_keyboard with callback_data 'agent:<data>', and send the last chunk with reply_markup (use _send_telegram_message with reply_markup support, mirroring the poller). The callback receiver at poller.py:819-837 already handles agent:-prefixed taps, so no receiver change is needed. Alternatively, if Telegram inline buttons are intentionally out of scope, strip [[button:...]] markers in the relay (as cron_delivery.py:351 already does) so users never see literal marker text.
evidence: apps/router/poller.py:404-413 (_send_rich_response button parsing - the only parser)
apps/router/poller.py:819-837 (agent: callback handler, unreachable on Telegram)
grep: _send_rich_response referenced only in test_proxy_phase3.py / test_poller test names
git log -S _send_rich_response: bef0156 (added) superseded by e89e08a (per-tenant queue)
apps/router/line_webhook.py:714 (LINE drain DOES parse buttons via extract_quick_reply_buttons)
expected_behaviour: In the poller, callback_data starting with 'agent:' is handled. Strips 'agent:' prefix to get button_value. Answers the callback query with '✓'. Edits the original message's reply_markup to remove buttons. Calls _forward_to_container with '[User tapped button: "{button_value}"]' and raw_user_text=button_value. The agent receives the button tap as if the user typed the button label. Inline buttons in AI responses are parsed from [[button:Label|callback_data]] markers by _send_rich_response and sent via reply_markup.

feature: Fleet-wide password rotation with reset email (privacy campaign)
detail: The mechanism is real. set_unusable_password() (line 106) bumps password_last_changed_at and the save persists it (line 107) BEFORE the email try-block. On email failure, email_failed is incremented and rotated is not (lines 117-122), but the password row is already mutated. The skip check (lines 96-98) compares password_last_changed_at >= since_dt and routes such users to skipped_already on re-run. So if the operator re-runs with a --since at or before a failed user's rotation time, that user is never re-emailed and stays with an unusable password and no reset link, locked out until manual intervention. There is no --resend-only flag (acknowledged as a future enhancement at line 132). However, severity is lower than the QA verdict's medium: this is fully documented behavior (docstring lines 10-13 and 63-66; ERROR warning printed at lines 128-134; inventory_notes), not a hidden bug. A documented workaround exists: re-running with an explicit --since EARLIER than the failure time (e.g. the original campaign start, the usage shown at line 19) causes failed users' password_last_changed_at to not satisfy >= since, so they fall through to rotation and re-email. The footgun only bites when the operator relied on the default --since=now() on the first run and then re-runs without reusing that exact timestamp. Real but narrow operational ergonomics gap with a self-documented recovery path, not a silent data-integrity defect. When it does bite, affected subscribers cannot log in (unusable password) and never receive a fresh reset link via the naive retry.
suggested_fix: Implement the already-stubbed --resend-only flag: re-send the reset email to users whose password is unusable (user.has_usable_password() is False) regardless of the --since cutoff, without re-bumping password_last_changed_at. This closes the gap independently of the operator remembering an earlier --since. Cheaper mitigation: echo the resolved since_dt prominently in the run summary so operators capture it for the retry run, and/or have the email-failure summary list the affected user emails/ids for targeted manual resend.
evidence: apps/tenants/management/commands/rotate_all_passwords.py:104-122 (unconditional set_unusable_password+save before email try; email_failed++ not rotated++)
expected_behaviour: management command: python manage.py rotate_all_passwords --reason=<string> [--dry-run] [--since <ISO8601>]. Excludes PLATFORM_OWNER_EMAIL user from rotation (warns if not set). For each remaining User: skips if no email, skips if password_last_changed_at >= --since cutoff (idempotency on retry). Calls user.set_unusable_password() (which bumps password_last_changed_at via model override and therefore invalidates all outstanding JWTs for that user via the pw_iat JWT claim), saves with update_fields=['password','password_last_changed_at']. Then calls _send_reset_email: generates Django default_token_generator.make_token + urlsafe_base64_encode(user.pk) and renders email/privacy_rotation_2026/email_1_{subject,body}.{txt,html} with reset_url={FRONTEND_URL}/reset-password?uid=&token=. Per-user email failures are logged but do NOT prevent password rotation (user is locked out until manual resend). --dry-run prints would-rotate list without any mutations. Also registered in QStash TASK_MAP as 'rotate_all_passwords' wrapping rotate_all_passwords_task().

detail:
Confirmed by code. handleSaveProfileField writes both success and failure text into a single saveMessage state: on success it sets "Saved!"/etc. (page.tsx:327-331), and in the catch block it sets the error string into the SAME variable (page.tsx:334: setSaveMessage(error instanceof Error ? error.message : "Failed to save. Please try again.")). There is exactly one banner rendering saveMessage (page.tsx:789-793), hardcoded to border-signal/30 bg-signal-faint text-signal. The --signal token is teal/green (#4ECDC4, globals.css:18-20), conventionally a success/positive color. So a 400/500 from PATCH /tenants/profile/ surfaces the error message styled identically to a "Saved!" confirmation — error feedback rendered in success-green. User impact: a user may misread a failed save as successful, or be momentarily confused, assuming their change persisted when it did not. Real but minor: the message TEXT still says "Failed"/echoes the backend error, so the content disambiguates, and the banner auto-clears after 3s (page.tsx:349). No data loss or blocked workflow — purely a color/affordance mismatch. The palette has amber/status tokens available for a proper error variant; their absence here makes this low rather than medium.</confirmed_detail>
<parameter name="suggested_fix">Track success vs. error separately (e.g. add a saveError boolean or a typed status alongside saveMessage), and conditionally style the banner: keep border-signal/30 bg-signal-faint text-signal on success, switch to an error variant on failure using the amber/rose tokens already in the palette (amber-border/amber-bg/amber-text). Optionally add role="alert" for accessibility.</suggested_fix>
<parameter name="fix_complexity">trivial
expected_behaviour: The Display Name field shows the current value (or "Not set") with an Edit button. Clicking Edit switches to an inline text input pre-filled with the current value. Clicking Save calls `PATCH /api/v1/tenants/profile/` with `{ display_name }`. On success the field reverts to read-only, a "Saved!" toast banner appears for 3 s, and the form closes. If the new value equals the old one, the form closes with no API call. On error the error message replaces the toast. The Save button shows "Saving..." and is disabled while in flight.
